paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 1: Family, Identity, and History
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Past Simple and more

This file has five grammar lessons for Unit 1. Work through one lesson at a time, try the practice exercises, then check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: The Past Simple Tense
What it means and why it matters

The past simple tense is used to talk about actions or events that happened and finished in the past. It is the most important tense for telling stories, describing experiences, and talking about history. When we say "I played football yesterday" or "She went to the market last week," we are using the past simple.

This tense matters because it helps us share stories about our families, our communities, and ourselves. Almost every sentence in a biography, a history book, or a personal story uses the past simple.
How to form the past simple
Step 1: Identify the verb in your sentence. Verbs are action words like play, eat, or go.
Step 2: Determine if the verb is regular or irregular.
Regular verbs add -ed to the base form:
play → played
work → worked
visit → visited
travel → travelled (double the final consonant after a short vowel + consonant)
study → studied (change y to i when y follows a consonant)
live → lived (just add -d when the verb already ends in e)
Irregular verbs do not follow a pattern. You must memorise them:
Negatives
To make a negative sentence, use did not (didn't) + the base form of the verb:
I did not play football. (not "did not played")
She didn't go to school. (not "didn't went")
They did not see the film. (not "did not saw")
Questions
To make a question, put Did before the subject and use the base form of the verb:
Did you play football yesterday?
Did she go to school?
Did they see the film?
Common mistakes
Using present tense instead of past: "She go to school yesterday" → She went to school yesterday.
Forgetting to change irregular verbs: "He goed home" → He went home.
Using the past form after did: "Did he went?" → Did he go?
Double negatives: "He didn't went nowhere" → He didn't go anywhere.
Memory tip

For regular verbs, remember: base form + ed = past simple. For irregular verbs, try making flashcards. Write the base form on one side and the past simple form on the other. Practise a few every day, and you will know them quickly.
Practice 1
Write the past simple form of each verb:
visit → ______
begin → ______
Practice 2
Complete each sentence with the correct past simple form:
Yesterday, I ______ (play) video games with my brother.
Last week, she ______ (go) to the zoo with her class.
They ______ (visit) their grandparents last summer.
He ______ (see) a beautiful bird in the garden.
My family ______ (move) to a new city three years ago.
The children ______ (sit) quietly and ______ (listen) to the story.
Lesson 2: Present Perfect vs Past Simple
What it means and why it matters
Both the present perfect and the past simple talk about the past, but they are used in different situations. Understanding the difference is one of the most important skills in English grammar.
Past simple is used for actions that are finished and happened at a specific time in the past. We often use time markers like yesterday, last week, in 2010, or three years ago.
"I visited Paris last summer." (finished action, specific time)
"She lived in Nairobi for five years." (she does not live there now)
Present perfect is used for actions that happened at an unspecified time in the past, or for actions that connect to the present. We use have/has + past participle.
"I have visited Paris." (at some point in my life — no specific time)
"She has lived in Nairobi for five years." (she still lives there now)
How to form the present perfect
Subject + have / has + past participle:
I / you / we / they + have + past participle: "We have finished our work."
he / she / it + has + past participle: "He has gone to school."
Key differences
Use the past simple when you mention a specific time:
"I saw that film last night." (specific time = past simple)
Use the present perfect when you do not mention a specific time:
"I have seen that film before." (no specific time = present perfect)
Use the past simple for finished situations:
"He lived in London for three years." (He doesn't live there now.)
Use the present perfect for situations that continue now:
"He has lived in London for three years." (He still lives there.)
Common mistakes
Mistake: "I have visited Paris last summer." ✗ (Specific time = use past simple.)
Fix: "I visited Paris last summer." ✓
Mistake: "She gone to the store." ✗ (Missing the auxiliary verb.)
Fix: "She has gone to the store." ✓
Memory tip

Think of it this way: Past Simple = "What did you do?" Present Perfect = "What have you done?" If someone asks "When?", use the past simple. If the answer is "at some point" or "ever", use the present perfect.
Practice 3
Choose the correct tense for each sentence:
I ______ (visit) my grandmother last weekend. (past simple / present perfect)
She ______ (never / see) a lion before. (past simple / present perfect)
They ______ (play) video games yesterday. (past simple / present perfect)
We ______ (be) to the beach many times. (past simple / present perfect)
He ______ (live) in Hargeisa since 2015. (past simple / present perfect)
My family ______ (move) to this city in 2010. (past simple / present perfect)
Lesson 3: Apostrophes for Possession
What it means and why it matters

Apostrophes are punctuation marks that help us show that something belongs to someone or something. Using apostrophes correctly is important because it helps others understand your writing clearly. Without them, sentences can be confusing.
Singular possession ('s)
When one person or thing owns something, add 's to their name:
"The boy's toy." (The toy belongs to one boy.)
"Halima's notebook." (The notebook belongs to Halima.)
"My grandmother's house." (The house belongs to one grandmother.)
Plural possession (s')
When more than one person or thing owns something, add s' to the plural noun:
"The boys' toys." (The toys belong to more than one boy.)
"The families' traditions." (The traditions belong to many families.)
"The teachers' meeting." (The meeting belongs to many teachers.)
Irregular plurals
If a plural noun does not end in -s (like children, men, women, people), add 's:
"The children's books." (The books belong to the children.)
"The men's room." (The room is for the men.)
"The women's team." (The team belongs to the women.)
Common mistakes
Mistake: "The cats toy is broken." ✗ (Missing apostrophe.)
Fix: "The cat's toy is broken." ✓ (One cat owns the toy.)
Mistake: "The boy's are playing." ✗ (Apostrophe used for a plural, not possession.)
Fix: "The boys are playing." ✓ (No apostrophe needed — just a plural.)
Memory tip
Ask yourself: "Does something belong to someone?" If yes, use an apostrophe. If the word is just a plural (more than one), do NOT use an apostrophe.
Practice 4
Add the correct apostrophe to each sentence:
The ______ (dog) collar is red. (one dog)
The ______ (girls) dresses are beautiful. (many girls)
I found the ______ (teacher) pen on the desk. (one teacher)
The ______ (children) playground is new. (many children)
My ______ (grandmother) stories are always interesting. (one grandmother)
The ______ (families) houses were rebuilt after the flood. (many families)
Lesson 4: Defining Relative Clauses
What it means and why it matters

Defining relative clauses are parts of a sentence that give essential information about a noun. They help us understand exactly who or what we are talking about. Without the clause, the sentence would not make clear sense.
For example: "The girl who sings is my friend." The clause "who sings" tells us which girl we mean. Without it, we would just say "The girl is my friend," and the listener might not know which girl.
Relative pronouns
Use different relative pronouns depending on what you are describing:
who — for people: "The man who lives next door is a teacher."
which — for things: "The book which I borrowed was very interesting."
that — for people or things (more informal): "The boy that scored the goal is my friend."
whose — for possession: "The girl whose father is a doctor wants to study medicine."
where — for places: "The village where my grandmother grew up is very small."
How to form a defining relative clause
Follow these steps:
Identify the noun you want to give more information about.
Choose the correct relative pronoun (who, which, that, whose, where).
Place the clause directly after the noun it describes.
Do NOT use commas around a defining relative clause.
Example: Combine these two sentences:
"The man is my uncle." + "He lives in Mogadishu."
Result: "The man who lives in Mogadishu is my uncle."
Common mistakes
Mistake: "The car who is red is mine." ✗ (Use who for people, not things.)
Fix: "The car that is red is mine." ✓
Mistake: "The boy, who runs fast, is my brother." ✗ (No commas in defining clauses.)
Fix: "The boy who runs fast is my brother." ✓
Memory tip
Remember the "WH" rule: Who = People. Which/That = Things. Whose = Possession. Where = Places.
Practice 5
Complete each sentence with the correct relative pronoun (who, which, that, whose, where):
The woman ______ lives next door is very kind.
This is the book ______ I borrowed from the library.
I know a boy ______ father works at the hospital.
The village ______ my family came from is near the coast.
The cat ______ sleeps on the sofa is mine.
Lesson 5: Should / Shouldn't
What it means and why it matters

The words should and shouldn't are used to give advice or make suggestions. When we say "You should study every day," we are telling someone what we think is a good idea. When we say "You shouldn't shout during an argument," we are advising them to avoid something.
In the story "The Fire Behind the Fence," characters use should and shouldn't several times to give advice about handling conflict:
"You shouldn't let others walk over you."
"You should stay calm and choose peace."
"You should speak honestly, not throw blame."
"You shouldn't have done this."
How to form sentences
Positive advice: Subject + should + base form of the verb.
"You should listen to your friend."
"He should apologise."
Negative advice: Subject + shouldn't + base form of the verb.
"You shouldn't shout during a disagreement."
"She shouldn't ignore the problem."
Questions: Should + subject + base form of the verb.
"Should I talk to my teacher about it?"
"Should we try to solve this together?"
Common mistakes
Mistake: "You should told the truth." ✗ (Use the base form, not the past form.)
Fix: "You should tell the truth." ✓
Memory tip
Think of should as your advice-giving word. "Should = this is a GOOD idea." "Shouldn't = this is a BAD idea."
Practice 6
Complete each sentence with should or shouldn't:
If you are feeling upset, you ______ talk to someone you trust.
You ______ be rude to your classmates.
When you make a mistake, you ______ admit it.
You ______ keep your promises to your friends.
If someone antagonizes you, you ______ try to stay calm.
You ______ spread rumours about other people.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Past Simple — Verb Forms)
1. played   2. went   3. visited   4. ate   5. lived   6. saw   7. worked   8. began
Practice 2 (Past Simple — Sentences)
1. played   2. went   3. visited   4. saw   5. moved   6. sat, listened
Practice 3 (Present Perfect vs Past Simple)
1. visited (past simple)   2. has never seen (present perfect)   3. played (past simple)   4. have been (present perfect)   5. has lived (present perfect)   6. moved (past simple)
Practice 4 (Apostrophes)
1. dog's   2. girls'   3. teacher's   4. children's   5. grandmother's   6. families'
Practice 5 (Defining Relative Clauses)
1. who   2. which / that   3. whose   4. where   5. that / which
Practice 6 (Should / Shouldn't)
1. should   2. shouldn't   3. should   4. should   5. should   6. shouldn't

paragraphs: Unit 1: Family, Identity, and History
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Year 7 English at Ehel Academy! This is Unit 1: Family, Identity, and History. In this unit, you will explore some of the most important questions a person can ask: Where do I come from? Who are the people in my family? What stories have been passed down through the generations? You will read about families, learn how to describe your own background, and discover how people across East Africa and the wider world have moved, settled, and built new lives over the centuries.

Family is at the heart of every culture. In Somali culture, for example, knowing your ancestry is deeply important. Many people can name their great-great-grandparents and trace their family line back many generations. In other East African communities, family histories are kept alive through oral stories, songs, and special objects passed from one generation to the next. This unit will help you explore these ideas in English, building your reading, writing, grammar, and speaking skills along the way.

You will also study the past simple tense, which is essential for telling stories about what happened in the past. Whether you are describing your grandmother's journey across a desert or explaining how your family settled in a new city, the past simple tense is your most important tool. Alongside the past simple, you will learn about present perfect vs past simple, apostrophes for possession, and defining relative clauses to add detail to your sentences.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a passage about family history and migration, and discuss its ideas in English.
Use the past simple tense correctly to describe completed actions and events.
Distinguish between the past simple and the present perfect tense.
Use apostrophes correctly to show possession (e.g., my grandmother's house, the families' traditions).
Use defining relative clauses to give essential information about people and things.
Write a detailed family biography using precise vocabulary and correct grammar.
Present your family history clearly in a short spoken presentation.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the lessons in the Grammar file (Past Simple, Present Perfect vs Past Simple, Apostrophes, Defining Relative Clauses, Should / Shouldn't).
Read the Story file, "The Fire Behind the Fence", and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary

Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it. Remember to say each word out loud as you learn it. Hearing yourself speak the word helps your brain remember it.
Family and Identity
Conflict and Behaviour
History and Stories
Reading: The Journey of Halima's Family

Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words and notice how the past simple tense is used throughout. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions and explore the grammar of the passage.

Halima sat on the floor of her grandmother's sitting room in Hargeisa, her legs crossed beneath her. The evening air carried the smell of bariis iyo hilib from the kitchen. Ayeeyo settled into her favourite chair and smiled.
"You want to know about our family?" Ayeeyo asked. "Then listen carefully, because this story travelled across deserts and seas before it reached you."

Ayeeyo began with her own grandfather, a man named Guled. Guled lived in a small village near the coast in the early 1900s. He belonged to a well-known clan that traded goods along the Indian Ocean. Guled worked as a boat-builder. He crafted vessels from local timber and sold them to merchants who sailed between Berbera and Aden.

"When Guled grew older," Ayeeyo explained, "he married a woman named Amina from a farming family inland. They moved to a town near the mountains and raised seven children. One of those children was my father, your great-grandfather, Nuur."

Nuur studied at a small school where he learned Arabic and mathematics. Later, he travelled to Aden for work. He spent five years there and saved enough money to return home and open a small shop. He married a woman called Fadumo, and together they had eight children.

"Your grandmother — that is me — was the youngest," Ayeeyo said, laughing. "I grew up hearing stories about Guled and the boats he built. My father kept a piece of old rope from one of those boats in a wooden box. It was our family's most precious artifact."

When conflict erupted in the region in the late 1980s, Ayeeyo's family faced a difficult choice. They packed what they could carry and joined thousands of others in a long migration south. They walked for days. They slept under trees. They arrived in a refugee camp exhausted but alive.

"We lost many things," Ayeeyo said quietly. "But we never lost our story. Every night in the camp, I told your mother the same stories my father told me. That is our heritage — it lives in words, not in objects."

Years later, the family resettled and rebuilt their lives. Halima's mother studied hard, became a nurse, and moved to Hargeisa. She named Halima after Guled's wife — the original Amina — to honour the ancestry that connected them all.
Halima looked at her grandmother. "So I am a descendant of a boat-builder?"
Ayeeyo nodded. "And a trader, a farmer, a scholar, and a survivor. You carry all of them inside you. That is what family means — a legacy that never disappears, even when everything else changes."
That night, Halima opened her notebook and wrote the first line of her family chronicle: "My name is Halima, and this is the story of where I come from."
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers. Remember to use past simple tense when talking about events in the passage.
Where does the story take place? Describe the setting at the beginning of the passage.
Who was Guled, and what did he do for a living? Give at least two details from the text.
Why did Guled and his wife Amina move to a town near the mountains?
What did Nuur do in Aden, and how long did he stay there?
What was the family's most precious artifact, and why was it important?
What caused the family to leave their home in the late 1980s?
How did Ayeeyo keep the family's heritage alive in the refugee camp?
Why did Halima's mother name her after Guled's wife?
What does Ayeeyo mean when she says, "You carry all of them inside you"?
What did Halima do at the end of the passage? Why is this significant?
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family. Try to use vocabulary from this unit in your responses.
Ayeeyo says, "Heritage lives in words, not in objects." Do you agree or disagree? Give your reasons.
Why do you think it is important for families to share stories about their past? What might happen if those stories are not told?
The passage describes a migration that was forced by conflict. How does migration change a family? Think about what is lost and what is gained.
Halima decides to write down her family's story. Do you think writing is a good way to preserve family history? What other ways can families keep their stories alive?
How does knowing your ancestry help you understand who you are today?
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context. Use your own words for the explanation.
ancestry
clan
migration
heritage
artifact
descendant
conflict
legacy
chronicle
Grammar Focus: Past Simple

This section introduces the most important grammar topic for Unit 1: the past simple tense. You will study it in more detail in your Grammar companion file, but here is a summary to get you started. The past simple tense is essential for telling stories, describing experiences, and talking about history — everything this unit is about.
What is the past simple tense?

The past simple tense is used to talk about actions or events that happened and finished in the past. When we tell stories about our families, describe historical events, or explain what we did yesterday, we use the past simple. It is one of the most common tenses in English.
Look at these examples from the reading passage:
Guled lived in a small village near the coast.
He worked as a boat-builder.
They packed what they could carry.
She named Halima after Guled's wife.
In each sentence, the action is complete — it started and finished in the past.
How to form the past simple
There are two types of verbs in the past simple:
Regular verbs follow a simple pattern. You add -ed to the base form of the verb:
play → played
work → worked
travel → travelled
settle → settled
marry → married (change y to i, then add -ed)
Irregular verbs do not follow a pattern. You need to memorise their past simple forms:
go → went
sit → sat
grow → grew
build → built
have → had
tell → told
write → wrote
begin → began
lose → lost
sleep → slept
Negatives and questions
To make a negative sentence in the past simple, use did not (or didn't) + the base form of the verb. Do NOT use the past form after did not:
She did not lose her story. (not "did not lost")
They didn't walk alone. (not "didn't walked")
To make a question in the past simple, put did before the subject and use the base form of the verb:
Did Guled build boats? (not "Did Guled built?")
Did they move to the mountains? (not "Did they moved?")
Common mistakes to avoid
Learners often make these mistakes with the past simple:
Using the present tense instead of the past tense: "He live in a village" should be "He lived in a village."
Forgetting to change irregular verbs: "She goed to school" should be "She went to school."
Using the past form after "did": "Did he worked?" should be "Did he work?"
Doubling "did" in negatives: "He did not didn't come" — use only one negative.
Time markers for the past simple
These words and phrases often signal the past simple tense:
yesterday, last week, last year, last night
in 1990, in the early 1900s, when I was young
ago (three days ago, a long time ago)
once, one day, at that time
Grammar Practice

These exercises focus on the past simple tense. Complete them in your notebook, then check your answers at the bottom or ask your AI tutor. Try to complete each exercise without looking at the answers first.
Exercise A: Regular or Irregular?
Write the past simple form of each verb. Then write (R) for regular or (I) for irregular.
study → ______
build → ______
travel → ______
begin → ______
marry → ______
sleep → ______
Answers: 1. walked (R)  2. went (I)  3. studied (R)  4. built (I)  5. lived (R)  6. told (I)  7. travelled (R)  8. began (I)  9. married (R)  10. slept (I)
Exercise B: Fill in the Past Simple
Complete each sentence with the past simple form of the verb in brackets.
Halima ______ (sit) on the floor and ______ (listen) to her grandmother.
Guled ______ (craft) boats from local timber.
The family ______ (move) to a town near the mountains.
Nuur ______ (spend) five years in Aden.
They ______ (pack) what they could carry and ______ (leave).
Ayeeyo ______ (tell) her children stories every night in the camp.
Halima's mother ______ (study) hard and ______ (become) a nurse.
Halima ______ (open) her notebook and ______ (write) the first line.
Answers: 1. sat, listened  2. crafted  3. moved  4. spent  5. packed, left  6. told  7. studied, became  8. opened, wrote
Exercise C: Make It Negative
Rewrite each sentence as a negative sentence using did not (didn't).
She walked to school yesterday.
They arrived on time.
He told his friend the secret.
The family lost their story.
Answers: 1. She did not walk to school yesterday.  2. They did not arrive on time.  3. He did not tell his friend the secret.  4. The family did not lose their story.
Exercise D: Make It a Question
Rewrite each sentence as a question using did.
Guled built boats near the coast.
The family moved to a new town.
Nuur studied Arabic and mathematics.
Halima wrote in her notebook.
Answers: 1. Did Guled build boats near the coast?  2. Did the family move to a new town?  3. Did Nuur study Arabic and mathematics?  4. Did Halima write in her notebook?
Exercise E: Write Your Own
Write six sentences about your family's past using the past simple tense. Follow these prompts:
Write about where a family member grew up. (Use an irregular verb.)
Write about something a relative did for work. (Use a regular verb.)
Write about a journey or move your family made. (Use a time marker like "in 2010" or "a long time ago".)
Write about a tradition your family kept. (Use the word heritage or tradition.)
Write a negative sentence about something that did not happen in your family's past.
Write a question you would like to ask an older relative about the past.
Writing Workshop: My Family Biography

In this section, you will plan and write a family biography — a true story about a member of your family or your family's history. This is your main writing project for Unit 1. A biography is a piece of writing that tells the story of a real person's life. When you write about your own family, you are creating something precious: a written record of your heritage and ancestry.

This writing project connects everything you have learned in this unit: past simple tense, vocabulary about family and identity, and the skill of telling a story in a clear and interesting way. Take your time with this project. A good biography is one that makes the reader feel they know the person you are writing about.
Step 1: Choose Your Subject
Think about which family member you want to write about. This could be someone you know well or someone you have heard stories about. Here are some suggestions:
A grandparent or great-grandparent and their early life.
A parent and their journey to where you live now.
An uncle, aunt, or older relative who did something remarkable.
Your family's migration story — how and why your family moved to a new place.
A relative who kept an important family tradition alive.
Step 2: Gather Information
Before you write, talk to family members and ask questions. Here are some good questions to ask:
Where were you born, and where did you grow up?
What was life like when you were a child?
What did your parents or grandparents do for work?
Did the family ever move to a different place? Why?
What is a story or memory from the past that you want younger people to know?
What traditions or values did your family pass down?
What challenges did the family face, and how did they overcome them?
Write the answers in your notebook. You will use them to plan your biography.
Step 3: Plan Your Biography
Use this structure to organise your ideas before you write:

Paragraph 1 — Introduction: Introduce the person you are writing about. Say their name, their relationship to you, and one interesting fact about them. For example: "My grandmother, Hawa, was born in a small village near the Jubba River in 1955."
Paragraph 2 — Early Life: Describe where the person grew up, what their family was like, and what their childhood was like. Use past simple verbs and include details about the place and time.

Paragraph 3 — Important Events: Describe the most important events in the person's life. This might include education, work, marriage, a migration, or a challenge they faced. Use time markers like "in 1980" or "when she was twenty years old".

Paragraph 4 — Legacy and Lessons: Explain what the person taught you or your family. What legacy did they leave behind? What values or traditions did they pass on? How did their experiences shape your family?

Paragraph 5 — Conclusion: End with a personal reflection. Why is this person important to you? What have you learned from their story? You could end with a quote from the person or a statement about what their life means to your family.
Step 4: Write Your Biography
Write your biography in five paragraphs. Aim for 250 to 350 words in total. Use the past simple tense for events that happened in the past. Include at least:
Eight past simple verbs (at least three irregular).
Five vocabulary words from this unit.
One defining relative clause (e.g., "My uncle, who served in the navy, told many stories").
Two possessive apostrophes (e.g., "my grandmother's village", "my parents' house").
One time marker (e.g., "in the 1990s", "when I was young").
Step 5: Edit and Improve
Use this checklist to review your writing before you hand it in:
Have I used the past simple tense correctly for past events?   ☐
Have I included at least five vocabulary words from this unit?   ☐
Have I used at least one defining relative clause?   ☐
Have I used apostrophes correctly for possession?   ☐
Does every sentence start with a capital letter and end with punctuation?   ☐
Have I organised my writing into clear paragraphs?   ☐
Have I read my work out loud to check that it sounds natural?   ☐
Model Paragraph
Here is an example of a well-written first paragraph to guide you:

“My grandfather, Hassan, was born in 1948 in a small town called Burao, in what is now Somaliland. He grew up in a large family with six brothers and three sisters. His father worked as a livestock trader who travelled between towns along the northern plains. Hassan's mother, whose name was Khadija, kept the household together and raised all the children while her husband was away. My grandfather always said that his mother's strength was the foundation of the whole family.”

Notice how this paragraph uses past simple verbs (was born, grew up, worked, travelled, kept, raised, said), a defining relative clause (whose name was Khadija), a possessive apostrophe (mother's strength), and family-related vocabulary.
Listening & Speaking
Listening: A Family Story from East Africa
Ask your AI tutor to read this short story aloud while you listen. Then read it yourself. Pay attention to the past simple verbs and the family vocabulary.
Interviewer: Amira, can you tell us about your family's history?

Amira: Of course. My family originally came from a farming region in southern Ethiopia. My great-grandfather owned a piece of land where he grew coffee and maize. He married young and had twelve children.
Interviewer: That is a big family! What happened next?

Amira: In the 1960s, there was a drought. The crops failed, and the family struggled to survive. My grandfather decided to leave the farm. He took his wife and four children and walked for three weeks to reach the city. It was an incredibly difficult migration.
Interviewer: How did the family manage in the city?

Amira: It was hard at first. My grandfather worked as a porter at the market. My grandmother sold spices from a small table. Slowly, they saved money and opened a shop. My father grew up in that shop, and he always said it was the heart of the family.
Interviewer: What is the most important thing you learned from your family's story?

Amira: That heritage is not about wealth or possessions. It is about the strength and courage your family showed when life was hard. My grandfather's legacy is not the shop — it is the lesson that hard work and family always come first.
After listening, answer these questions:
Where did Amira's family originally come from?
What caused the family to leave the farm?
How did Amira's grandfather earn money when they first arrived in the city?
What did Amira's grandfather eventually open?
What does Amira say is her grandfather's true legacy?
Find five past simple verbs in the interview and write them in your notebook.
Speaking 1: Present Your Family Biography
Prepare a short spoken presentation (about two minutes) about the family member you wrote about in the Writing Workshop. Follow this structure:
Introduction: Say the person's name, their relationship to you, and when they were born.
Early life: Describe where they grew up and what their childhood was like.
Key events: Share two or three important events from their life.
Legacy: Explain what they taught you or what they left behind for the family.
Conclusion: Say why you admire this person or what their story means to you.
Record yourself and listen back. Check that you used past simple verbs and spoke clearly. Ask your AI tutor for feedback on your pronunciation and grammar.
Speaking 2: Family Tree Discussion
Draw a simple family tree showing at least three generations of your family. Then describe it to your AI tutor or a classmate using these sentence starters:
"My great-grandfather's name was... He lived in..."
"My grandmother came from a clan called..."
"My parents moved to... because..."
"I am a descendant of people who..."
After your description, the listener should ask at least two follow-up questions. For example: "When did your grandparents move?" or "What did your great-grandfather do for work?"
Speaking 3: "Where Do You Come From?" Game
Play this game with your AI tutor or a classmate:
One person describes a family member without saying their name. Use past simple tense.
Include three clues. For example: "This person grew up near the coast. They worked as a trader. They built things from wood."
The other person guesses who it is based on the reading passage, the story, or your own family.
Take turns giving clues and guessing.
Check Your Learning

You have now completed Unit 1: Family, Identity, and History. This is the first unit of Year 7 — well done for getting started! Use the checklist below to see what you have learned and what you might want to review before moving on to Unit 2.
Self-Assessment
Tick "Yes" or "Not yet". For every "Not yet", go back to that section and try again, or ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can read and understand a passage about family history and migration.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use the past simple tense correctly for regular and irregular verbs.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can form negatives and questions using did and did not.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use apostrophes to show possession (singular and plural).   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use a defining relative clause to add detail to a sentence.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a family biography using vocabulary and grammar from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can speak clearly about my family's history in a short presentation.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I know at least fifteen vocabulary words from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can explain why knowing your family history is important.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Reflection
In your notebook, write a short paragraph (three to five sentences) answering this question:
What is the most important thing you learned about your family, your identity, or your history during this unit? How will you use what you learned outside of school?
Share your reflection with your AI tutor or your teacher at your next session.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?"
Practise speaking: "Can you listen to my family presentation and give me feedback?"
Get feedback: "Can you check my biography and tell me what to fix?"
Ask for more practice: "Can you give me more past simple verb exercises?"
Prepare for your teacher: "What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?"
A Simple Daily Routine

Great start to Year 7! You have taken the first step in exploring your family's story and building your English skills. Keep going — every word you learn and every sentence you write brings you closer to fluency. 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 1: Family, Identity, and History
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "The Fire Behind the Fence"
The Fire Behind the Fence

Musa stood at the edge of the dusty football field, his fists clenched. The sun was setting, casting long shadows across the small apartment buildings in their Nairobi neighbourhood. The field was the only open space nearby, a place where joy and rivalry mixed like dust and sweat. Today, however, it had become a battlefield.
"They argue too much," whispered Omondi beside him. "And now they want to fight."

Across the field, another group of boys had gathered. Their leader, Baraka, was known for being belligerent. He walked with a swagger and often shouted orders. Musa remembered when Baraka used to be friendly. But something had changed over the past few months.

It all started with an argument about football. Musa's team claimed Baraka's group had broken the goalpost, which Baraka denied. The conflict grew quickly. Words turned into shouting. Then came threats. The air between them became thick with animosity.
Baraka stepped forward, his chin up. "This is our field. You lost it when you lied about the post."
"You're the liar," Musa said sharply. "Your whole team is just devious and loud."
Baraka's jaw clenched. "Say that again."
"You heard me," Musa replied.
Mutua, Baraka's friend, stepped forward. "We should retaliate," he muttered. "Let them know who really owns this field."

Musa's stomach tightened. He didn't want to fight. But walking away felt like losing. His father once said, "You shouldn't let others walk over you." But his mother always told him, "You should stay calm and choose peace."

That night, Musa lay awake, the sounds of Nairobi's night outside his window. He thought about what his grandfather had told him: "Revenge is like a fire in dry grass — it spreads fast, but leaves only ash."
The next morning, everything changed.

A neighbour's house caught fire. Smoke billowed above the buildings. Musa and others ran toward the fire. When he reached the house, he was shocked to see Baraka inside the smoky yard, trying to free a goat tied to a wooden fence.
"Baraka!" Musa screamed. "Get out of there!"
Baraka didn't listen. He kicked at the fence until it broke. The goat bolted. Musa ran to help Baraka, grabbing his arm just as part of the fence fell behind them.
Afterward, they sat on the curb, coughing and covered in ash.
"You could've died," Musa said.
Baraka shrugged. "The goat didn't do anything wrong. I had to try."
People around the neighbourhood praised Baraka's bravery. Musa began to see him differently. Maybe the boy who shouted and pushed people wasn't all bad. Maybe there was more behind his abrasive mask.
But peace was short-lived.
A week later, Musa returned from school to find his football slashed open. A knife mark ran right through it. His anger flared like dry kindling. He knew exactly who had done it.
He stormed across the field, the ruined ball in hand. "You think you're funny?" he shouted at Baraka.
"What are you talking about?" Baraka looked genuinely confused.
"Don't act innocent," Musa said. "Only someone as vindictive as you would do this."
Baraka frowned. "It wasn't me. You're always so quick to blame. You're becoming as abrasive as your team."
"You're just trying to antagonize me again!"
Baraka's face hardened. "You're full of malevolence. You want me punished."
Their shouting drew a crowd. A teacher walking by came over.
"Enough," she said. "What's going on?"
Musa showed her the torn ball. "He destroyed it."
"I didn't," Baraka replied. "He's always defiant, never listens."
The teacher made them sit on the nearby bench. "You should speak honestly, not throw blame. Talk."
They sat in silence. Then Baraka said, "I really didn't do it. Maybe someone else wants us to fight."
Musa's anger began to cool. "But who?"
That evening, Omondi came running with something in his hand — a knife with the initials M.O. carved into the handle.

"Mwangi Omari!" Musa gasped. Mwangi had once been on their team but felt left out. He had stopped coming to play, and lately, he had been making derogatory remarks from the edge of the field, trying to jeer at the players.
"He used to say we treated him unfairly," Baraka remembered. "Maybe he did this to exacerbate our conflict."
They went to Mwangi's house. When they confronted him, he tried to connive his way out.
"That's not mine," he said, his eyes shifting.
"Don't be mendacious," Musa replied. "It has your name."
Mwangi sighed. "Fine. I did it. I just... I felt left out. You all stopped talking to me. I was angry."
"You could've talked to us," Musa said. "You shouldn't have done this."
"Can I still play?" Mwangi asked.
Baraka hesitated. "Only if you're honest. No more tricks. No more lies."
Mwangi nodded quickly. "I promise."

In the weeks that followed, things slowly changed. Mwangi rejoined the group. Baraka and Musa became better at talking before shouting. Whenever small problems came up, they tried to solve them with words.
One afternoon, Musa and Baraka sat on the field's edge, watching the younger boys practise.
"Remember how angry we used to get?" Musa asked.
"We were hotheads," Baraka said, laughing. "But I guess we were just kids with too much pride."
Musa smiled. "Yeah. Pride and no patience."
Baraka nodded. "But we've learned. And we're better for it."
Behind them, Omondi called out, "Hey, captains! We need you for the next game."
Musa and Baraka stood up. They looked at each other and grinned. Not long ago, they were enemies. Now, they were leaders.
And they weren't just leading a game anymore. They were leading by example.

Even in moments of tension, they reminded others, "You should stay calm." When younger players began to argue, they guided them to talk. When someone felt alienated, they made space. They organised a weekend clean-up of the field, inviting everyone — including those who used to be rivals.

The field changed, and so did the boys. Baraka, once narcissistic and loud, now listened before speaking. Musa, once quick to anger, now paused and considered. The old discrepancy between them faded, replaced by respect.

One day, a new student tried to harass Mwangi during practice. Musa stepped in immediately. "That's not how we treat each other here," he said firmly. "You shouldn't bring problems from outside to our team."
The student backed down, surprised.
Later that evening, Musa told Baraka, "I used to think the biggest problem was losing a ball or a match. But now I see — it's losing trust."
Baraka nodded slowly. "And once trust breaks, it takes a lot to fix it. But we did."
And the field, once a place of battles, became a place of healing.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the story.
Where and when does the story take place? Describe the setting.
What started the conflict between Musa and Baraka?
How did Musa feel about fighting? What advice did his parents give him?
What happened the morning after the argument? How did this event change Musa's view of Baraka?
Who actually destroyed Musa's football, and why did they do it?
How did Musa and Baraka find out the truth about who slashed the ball?
What was Mwangi's real reason for causing trouble? What did he want?
The teacher said, "You should speak honestly, not throw blame." What does this advice mean?
How did Musa and Baraka change by the end of the story? Give at least two examples.
Musa says, "The biggest problem is losing trust." What does he mean by this?
What does the grandfather's saying about fire and dry grass mean in relation to the story?
The story ends by calling the field "a place of healing." What has it healed?
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
Musa's grandfather said, "Revenge is like a fire in dry grass." Do you agree? Can you think of a saying in your own language that means something similar?
The teacher told Musa and Baraka to "talk." How important is honest conversation in solving conflicts? Can you think of a time when talking helped solve a problem?
Mwangi felt alienated and left out. How can groups make sure nobody feels excluded?
Baraka saved a goat from a fire even though he had been acting belligerent. What does this tell us about judging people by first impressions?
The story is set in Nairobi. Could this story happen in your own community? Why or why not?
If you were Musa, would you have forgiven Mwangi? Explain your reasoning.
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find each of these Unit 1 vocabulary words and read out the sentence they appear in:
belligerent, malevolence, argue, argument, conflict, vindictive, retaliate
abrasive, derogatory, alienate, animosity, antagonize, connive
defiant, devious, discrepancy, exacerbate, harass, mendacious
narcissistic, jeer, problem
Grammar in the Story
The story uses several grammar features from this unit. Find examples of each:
Past simple tense: Find at least five sentences that use past simple verbs. Write them in your notebook.
Should / Shouldn't: Find at least three sentences where characters give advice using should or shouldn't.
Present perfect: Find at least two sentences that use "have" or "has" + past participle.
Apostrophes: Find at least three examples of possessive apostrophes (e.g., Baraka's face, Musa's team).
Now You Try: Writing
Writing Activities
Retell a scene: Choose your favourite part of the story and retell it in your own words. Write at least five sentences. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit.
Write a letter of advice: Imagine Musa writes to you and tells you about his conflict with Baraka. Write a letter back giving him advice. Use should and shouldn't at least three times each.
Character comparison: Write a paragraph comparing Musa and Baraka at the beginning of the story and at the end. How did each boy change? Use past simple verbs and vocabulary from the unit.

Different ending: What if Mwangi had never been discovered? Write a new ending to the story (at least six sentences) where Musa and Baraka never find out who slashed the ball. What might have happened?
Personal connection: Write a paragraph about a time you had a conflict with someone. What happened? How was it resolved? Use past simple tense throughout.
Reflection
In your notebook, write three to five sentences answering this question:
What is the most important lesson from this story, and how can you apply it in your own life?
