paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 2: Celebrations and Festivals
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Prepositions for Time Phrases

This file covers the key grammar topic for Unit 2: Prepositions for Time Phrases (in, on, at). Work through the explanation, study the examples, and then complete the practice exercises. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Prepositions of Time — in, on, at
What This Rule Means and Why It Matters

Prepositions are small but powerful words that show relationships between things. When we talk about time, we use in, on, and at to tell people exactly when something happens. Getting these right makes your English sound natural and clear. Getting them wrong can cause confusion — for example, “I’ll meet you in Friday” does not make sense to an English listener, but “I’ll meet you on Friday” is perfectly clear.
How to Use Them: Step by Step
Step 1 — Use IN for longer periods of time:
Think of in as a big container that holds a stretch of time.
Months: I was born in July. / Ramadan falls in different months each year.
Years: Kenya gained independence in 1963.
Seasons: The harvest festival is held in autumn.
Parts of the day: We pray Fajr in the early morning. / I study best in the evening.
Centuries and decades: Great changes happened in the 21st century.
Step 2 — Use ON for specific days and dates:
Think of on as landing on a particular spot on the calendar.
Days of the week: The market is busiest on Saturday.
Dates: My birthday is on 15 March.
Special days: We celebrate Independence Day on 12 December. / We feast on the first day of Eid.
Day + part of day: The guests arrived on Friday evening.
Step 3 — Use AT for precise times and fixed expressions:
Think of at as pointing to an exact moment.
Clock times: The ceremony begins at 10:00 AM.
Specific moments: We break our fast at sunset. / The fireworks start at midnight.
Meal times: Let’s talk about it at lunch.
Fixed expressions: at the weekend (British English), at night, at the moment.
Festival periods (general): We visit relatives at Eid. (Here “at Eid” means during the whole Eid period.)
Special Notes and Exceptions
English has a few tricky cases. Here are the most important ones:
Night vs. other parts of the day: We say in the morning / afternoon / evening, but at night. This is an exception you must memorise.
On vs. At for festivals: “On Eid morning” (a specific day) vs. “At Eid” (the general period). Both are correct, but they mean slightly different things.
No preposition needed: We do not use in, on, or at before today, tomorrow, yesterday, this, last, next, or every. Example: “I will see you next Monday” (NOT “on next Monday”).
Twelve Real Sentence Examples
The Maulidi festival is celebrated in the month of Rabi ul-Awal.
We always visit our grandparents on the first day of Eid.
The Fajr prayer is performed at dawn.
She was born in 2012.
The school concert takes place on Wednesday.
The fireworks display starts at 9:00 PM.
In the rainy season, many festivals are postponed.
My cousin’s wedding is on 5 August.
We break our fast at sunset during Ramadan.
The sports day is always held in November.
The parade begins on New Year’s Day.
I like to read quietly at night before I sleep.
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Study these common errors carefully:
Mistake 1: “I was born on July.”
Fix: July is a month (a long period), so use in: “I was born in July.”
Mistake 2: “We have a meeting in Monday.”
Fix: Monday is a specific day, so use on: “We have a meeting on Monday.”
Mistake 3: “The show starts in 7 PM.”
Fix: 7 PM is a clock time, so use at: “The show starts at 7 PM.”
Mistake 4: “I’ll see you on the evening.”
Fix: “The evening” is a part of the day, so use in: “I’ll see you in the evening.”
Mistake 5: “She arrives at next Friday.”
Fix: Drop the preposition before “next”: “She arrives next Friday.”
Memory Trick
Practice Exercises
Practice 1: Choose the Correct Preposition
Write in, on, or at in each gap.
The festival begins ______ the 20th of March.
We always have a big meal ______ Eid.
My grandmother was born ______ 1955.
Classes start ______ 8:30 AM every day.
The school play is ______ Friday.
It gets very cold ______ winter.
The call to prayer sounds ______ dawn.
We planted trees ______ the afternoon.
Independence Day is celebrated ______ 1 July.
I finished my homework ______ the evening.
The Timkat celebration happens ______ January.
The family gathers for dinner ______ sunset.
Practice 2: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence has one preposition error. Rewrite the sentence correctly.
The wedding is in Saturday.
We celebrate Eid on the end of Ramadan.
She was born at 2010.
The bus leaves in exactly 3:00 PM.
The market opens at every morning.
I saw my friend in last Tuesday.
The programme starts on midnight.
They always travel in New Year’s Day.
Practice 3: Write Your Own Sentences
Write one sentence for each of the following, using the correct preposition of time.
A sentence about something you do in a particular month.
A sentence about something that happens on a specific day of the week.
A sentence about something that starts at an exact time.
A sentence about a festival that takes place in a particular season.
A sentence about an event on a specific date.
A sentence about something you do at night.
Practice 4: Festival Paragraph
Write a short paragraph (five to six sentences) about a festival or celebration. You must use in at least twice, on at least once, and at at least once. Underline each preposition of time.

Example: In the month of Ramadan, my family fasts from dawn to sunset. On the last day, we feel excited because Eid is coming. At sunrise on the morning of Eid, we put on our best clothes and walk to the prayer ground. We eat a big meal together in the afternoon.
Answer Key
Practice 1 Answers
1. on   2. at   3. in   4. at   5. on   6. in   7. at   8. in   9. on   10. in   11. in   12. at
Practice 2 Answers
The wedding is on Saturday.
We celebrate Eid at the end of Ramadan.
She was born in 2010.
The bus leaves at exactly 3:00 PM.
The market opens every morning. (No preposition before “every.”)
I saw my friend last Tuesday. (No preposition before “last.”)
The programme starts at midnight.
They always travel on New Year’s Day.
Practice 3 and 4
These are open-ended. Check your answers with your AI tutor or teacher.
tables:
💡  The I-O-A Rule
IN = Intervals (months, years, seasons, parts of the day)
ON = Occasions (days of the week, dates, special named days)
AT = Actual times (clock times, dawn, sunset, midnight, night)
Exception: AT night (not IN night). And no preposition before today, tomorrow, yesterday, this, last, next, every.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘in the morning’ but ‘at night’?”
“Can you give me more practice sentences with prepositions of time?”
“What is the difference between ‘on Eid’ and ‘at Eid’?”

paragraphs: Unit 2: Celebrations and Festivals
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will explore celebrations and festivals from around the world, with a special focus on traditions that matter to communities across East Africa and the Muslim world. Festivals bring people together. They remind us of our history, our values, and the things that make each culture unique. Whether it is the joy of Eid al-Fitr after a month of fasting, the colourful lanterns of a harvest celebration, or the excitement of a local sports day, every festival has a story to tell.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand texts about different festivals and celebrations.
Use prepositions of time (in, on, at) correctly in speech and writing.
Recognise and form compound adjectives (hand-made, brightly-coloured).
Identify participle adjectives (-ed / -ing) and use them accurately.
Write a detailed description of a festival you know or have attended.
Discuss and compare celebrations from different cultures.
Use new vocabulary about festivals, food, traditions, and community values.
Analyse poetry about celebrations and perform a short reading aloud.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Key Vocabulary section below, then study the full Vocabulary companion file.
Read the festival passage in the Reading section carefully.
Work through the Understanding questions to check your comprehension.
Study the Grammar: Prepositions for Time section and the companion Grammar file.
Complete all the Practice exercises.
Follow the Writing Workshop to write your own festival description.
Do the Listening & Speaking activities with your AI tutor or a partner.
Finish with Check Your Learning to measure your progress.
Key Vocabulary

Below is a preview of the most important words for this unit. You will find the full list, with definitions, word types, and example sentences, in your Vocabulary companion file. Try to learn five or six new words each day.
Reading: Festivals That Bring Us Together
Read the following passage carefully. Pay attention to the prepositions of time (in, on, at) and the vocabulary words in bold.

Every community around the world has special times when people come together to celebrate. These gatherings are called festivals, and they are woven into the rhythm of human life. Some festivals mark the changing seasons, others honour religious beliefs, and still others celebrate harvests, victories, or the memory of important people. What they all share is a sense of joy, belonging, and gratitude.

In many parts of East Africa, the most eagerly awaited festival of the year is Eid al-Fitr, which comes at the end of Ramadan. Families rise early on the morning of Eid and put on their finest clothes. Children wear brightly-coloured outfits that have been hand-stitched or newly bought for the occasion. At sunrise, people walk together to the prayer ground, greeting neighbours with warm smiles and the words “Eid Mubarak!” After the prayer, the real feasting begins. Tables are spread with freshly-made sambusas, sweet halwa, fragrant rice, and tender roasted meat. The air is filled with the rich smell of cardamom tea and the sound of laughter. Elders sit in the shade and tell stories of past celebrations, while children run through the streets, their pockets full of sweets and small gifts. On the first day, families visit relatives, and on the second day, friends gather for games and community events. It is a time of generosity: those who have more share with those who have less, and old quarrels are often reconciled.

Far to the north, in the highlands of Ethiopia, the festival of Timkat is celebrated in January. Timkat marks the baptism of Jesus in the River Jordan, and it is one of the most colourful events in the Ethiopian calendar. Priests wear magnificent, gold-embroidered robes, and they carry the Tabot — a sacred replica of the Ark of the Covenant — through the streets in a joyful procession. Crowds line the roads, singing hymns and clapping. At dawn on the main day, water is blessed and sprinkled over the faithful. The atmosphere is both solemn and enthusiastic, a mixture of deep respect and pure celebration.

Meanwhile, in the coastal towns of Kenya and Tanzania, the Maulidi festival is held every year in the month of Rabi ul-Awal to honour the birth of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). On the night before the main celebration, the narrow streets of Lamu come alive with lanterns, drums, and the sweet chanting of Swahili poetry. Men perform traditional stick dances, and women prepare elaborate, sweet-scented dishes. At noon the next day, scholars give talks about the Prophet’s life, emphasising values such as compassion, honesty, and benevolence. Children listen attentively, and many feel inspired to be more diligent and kind in their daily lives.

What makes festivals truly powerful is not the food or the decorations alone, but the way they strengthen the bonds between people. In every culture, a festival is a reminder that we belong to something larger than ourselves. The next time you attend a celebration, look around: you will see authentic traditions passed down through generations, resilience in communities that keep their customs alive, and the simple, tranquil happiness that comes from being together.
Understanding the Passage
Comprehension Questions
Answer the following questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What are three reasons the passage gives for why communities hold festivals?
Describe what happens on the morning of Eid al-Fitr, according to the passage.
What foods are mentioned in the description of the Eid feast?
The passage says old quarrels are often “reconciled” during Eid. What does this word mean, and why do you think festivals encourage reconciliation?
What is the Tabot, and what role does it play in the Timkat festival?
In which month and in which country is the Maulidi festival held?
The passage says festivals “strengthen the bonds between people.” Explain what this means in your own words.
Find and copy three examples of prepositions of time (in, on, at) from the passage. For each one, explain why that particular preposition is used.
Think and Discuss
These questions do not have a single correct answer. Think carefully and share your ideas with a partner or your AI tutor.
Which festival described in the passage sounds most interesting to you? Why?
The passage mentions that festivals remind us we “belong to something larger than ourselves.” What do you think this means?
Think of a celebration in your own community. How is it similar to one of the festivals in the passage? How is it different?
Why do you think it is important for younger generations to learn about traditional festivals?
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of the following words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
enthusiastic (paragraph about Timkat)
benevolence (paragraph about Maulidi)
diligent (paragraph about Maulidi)
authentic (final paragraph)
resilience (final paragraph)
tranquil (final paragraph)
Grammar Focus: Prepositions for Time

Prepositions are small words that show the relationship between a noun and other parts of a sentence. When we talk about time, the three most important prepositions are in, on, and at. Using the correct one makes your English clear and natural.
The Rules
Use in for longer periods of time:
Months: in January, in Ramadan
Years: in 2026, in the 1990s
Seasons: in summer, in the rainy season
Parts of the day: in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening
Use on for specific days and dates:
Days of the week: on Monday, on Friday
Dates: on 15 June, on the first day
Special days: on Eid morning, on my birthday, on Independence Day
Use at for precise times and certain fixed expressions:
Clock times: at 9:00 AM, at half past three
Specific moments: at sunrise, at midnight, at noon
Festivals (general period): at Christmas, at Eid (when talking about the whole period)
Common Mistakes
Mistake: “I was born on July.”
Correction: “I was born in July.” (July is a month — use in.)
Mistake: “We have a meeting in Monday.”
Correction: “We have a meeting on Monday.” (Monday is a specific day — use on.)
Mistake: “The show starts in 7 PM.”
Correction: “The show starts at 7 PM.” (7 PM is a clock time — use at.)
Memory Trick
For a full grammar lesson with more examples and practice exercises, see your Grammar companion file: Prepositions for Time Phrases.
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Preposition (in / on / at)
Write the correct preposition of time in each gap.
The Eid prayer begins ______ sunrise.
My family always celebrates together ______ the evening.
We visited our grandparents ______ the first day of Eid.
Timkat is celebrated ______ January every year.
The Maulidi procession starts ______ Friday.
School reopens ______ September after the long holiday.
The fireworks display begins ______ 9:00 PM.
______ the month of Ramadan, many people fast from dawn to sunset.
The harvest festival takes place ______ autumn.
We broke our fast together ______ sunset.
Answers: 1. at  2. in  3. on  4. in  5. on  6. in  7. at  8. In  9. in  10. at
Exercise 2: Spot the Error
Each sentence has one mistake with a preposition of time. Rewrite the sentence correctly.
The concert is in Saturday evening.
We celebrate Eid on the end of Ramadan.
She was born at March.
The guests arrived in exactly 3:00 PM.
The school play is at the 20th of December.
Answers: 1. on Saturday evening  2. at the end of Ramadan  3. in March  4. at exactly 3:00 PM  5. on the 20th of December
Exercise 3: Compound Adjectives
Combine the words in brackets to form a compound adjective and rewrite the sentence.
She wore a ______ (brightly / coloured) dress to the festival.
The children carried ______ (hand / made) lanterns through the streets.
A ______ (well / known) singer performed at the concert.
They served a ______ (freshly / baked) cake at the celebration.
He is a ______ (hard / working) student who helps organise school events.
Answers: 1. brightly-coloured  2. hand-made  3. well-known  4. freshly-baked  5. hard-working
Exercise 4: Participle Adjectives (-ed / -ing)
Choose the correct form of the word in brackets.
The festival was really ______ (excited / exciting).
I was ______ (amazed / amazing) by the fireworks display.
The long ceremony was ______ (bored / boring) for the little children.
She felt ______ (tired / tiring) after cooking all day for the feast.
The traditional dances were ______ (fascinated / fascinating) to watch.
He was ______ (confused / confusing) about which day the celebration would start.
Answers: 1. exciting  2. amazed  3. boring  4. tired  5. fascinating  6. confused
Exercise 5: Vocabulary Challenge
Replace the underlined word or phrase with a vocabulary word from this unit. The first letter is given as a hint.
The kind and helpful (b__________) woman prepared meals for the whole neighbourhood during the festival.
His ability to attract and inspire (c__________) personality made everyone want to join his team.
She showed great determination and refusal to give up (t__________) when the decorations kept falling down.
The festival helped the two friends become friendly again (r__________).
She was careful and paid great attention (m__________) when arranging the flowers.
The park was calm and peaceful (t__________) after the celebrations ended.
Answers: 1. benevolent  2. charismatic  3. tenacity / tenacious spirit  4. reconcile  5. meticulous  6. tranquil
Exercise 6: Relative Clauses
Join each pair of sentences using the relative pronoun in brackets.
The woman organised the festival. She is my neighbour. (who)
The cake was delicious. My aunt baked the cake. (that)
Lamu is a beautiful island. The Maulidi festival is held there. (where)
My friend won the poetry competition. His poem was about Eid. (whose)
The decorations looked wonderful. They were hand-made by the children. (which)
Sample answers:
The woman who organised the festival is my neighbour.
The cake that my aunt baked was delicious.
Lamu is a beautiful island where the Maulidi festival is held.
My friend whose poem was about Eid won the poetry competition.
The decorations, which were hand-made by the children, looked wonderful.
Writing Workshop: Describe a Festival
In this section you will plan, draft, and polish a description of a festival or celebration you have experienced or know about. Your finished piece should be at least 200 words long.
Step 1: Choose Your Festival
Pick a celebration you know well. It could be:
A religious festival (Eid al-Fitr, Eid al-Adha, Maulidi, Timkat, Christmas, Diwali)
A national holiday (Independence Day, cultural heritage day)
A family celebration (a wedding, a naming ceremony, a graduation)
A community event (a harvest festival, a sports day, a school open day)
Step 2: Plan Your Writing
Use this planning frame to organise your ideas before you start writing.
Name of the festival: What is it called? When does it take place?
Background: Why is this festival celebrated? What is its history or meaning?
Preparations: What do people do to get ready? (clothes, food, decorations)
The day itself: What happens? Describe the events in order.
Atmosphere: How does the festival feel? Use adjectives and sensory details (sights, sounds, smells, tastes).
Why it matters: Why is this festival important to you or your community?
Step 3: Write Your First Draft
Here is an example opening paragraph to help you get started:

Every year, in the month of Shawwal, my family celebrates Eid al-Fitr. It is the most joyful day of the year for us. On the morning of Eid, I wake up early and put on my new brightly-coloured outfit. The house smells of freshly-made sambusas and sweet cardamom tea. I feel excited and grateful because the long month of fasting is finally over.
Step 4: Revise and Edit
Read your draft aloud. Then check:
Does every sentence begin with a capital letter and end with a full stop, question mark, or exclamation mark?
Have you used in, on, and at correctly?
Have you hyphenated your compound adjectives when they come before a noun?
Have you chosen the right participle adjective form (-ed for feelings, -ing for causes)?
Does your description paint a vivid picture that a reader can see, hear, and smell?
Have you organised your writing into clear paragraphs?
Step 5: Share and Reflect
When you are satisfied with your festival description:
Read it aloud to your AI tutor and ask for feedback.
Share it with a family member or classmate.
Write a short reflection (three to four sentences): What did you learn from writing this? What would you do differently next time?
Listening & Speaking
Listening Activity: Festival Interview
Ask your AI tutor to role-play as someone who has just returned from a festival. Listen to their description and then answer these questions:
Which festival did the person attend?
When did it take place? (Listen for prepositions of time.)
What were the most memorable parts of the celebration?
What food was served?
How did the person feel about the experience?
Speaking Activity 1: Compare Two Festivals
Choose two festivals — one from the reading passage and one from your own experience. Prepare a short talk (two to three minutes) comparing them. Use these sentence starters:
“Both festivals are celebrated in ...”
“On the main day of [Festival A], people ... , whereas on the main day of [Festival B], people ...”
“One important difference is that ...”
“What both festivals have in common is ...”
Speaking Activity 2: Poetry Performance
Read this short poem about celebration aloud. Practise reading it with expression and feeling. Then perform it for your AI tutor, a family member, or a classmate.
The drums begin at dawn,
A rhythm old and strong.
The streets fill up with colour,
And everyone belongs.
We feast on what we’ve gathered,
We dance until the night.
We tell the ancient stories
By lantern’s golden light.
For festivals remind us
Of all that we hold dear:
The love of friends and family,
And a brand-new hopeful year.
After performing, discuss:
What is the mood of this poem? How does the poet create that mood?
Which line is your favourite? Why?
Can you find a compound adjective in the poem?
Write your own two-line addition to this poem about a festival you love.
Speaking Activity 3: Festival Poster Pitch
Imagine you are organising a community festival. Design a simple poster (on paper or digitally) and then present it to your AI tutor or a classmate. Your pitch should include:
The name and date of the festival.
Where and when it will take place (use prepositions of time!).
What activities, food, and entertainment will be available.
Why people should come.
Additional Reading: Cultural Comparisons
Festivals look different around the world, but they serve similar purposes. Read the short descriptions below and think about what they have in common.
Diwali — The Festival of Lights (India)

Diwali is celebrated in October or November. It lasts for five days and marks the victory of light over darkness. Families clean their homes, light clay lamps called diyas, and share sweets with neighbours. On the main night, fireworks fill the sky and the streets sparkle with candles. Children wear brand-new, brightly-coloured clothes and receive gifts from their elders.
Thanksgiving — A Harvest Celebration (United States)

Thanksgiving is celebrated on the fourth Thursday in November. Families gather around a large table to share a meal of roast turkey, potatoes, and pumpkin pie. Before eating, people take turns saying what they are grateful for. The day is about giving thanks for the harvest and for the people in your life.
Nowruz — The Persian New Year (Iran and Central Asia)

Nowruz is celebrated on 21 March, the first day of spring. Families prepare a special table called the Haft-sin, set with seven symbolic items. People clean their homes, buy new clothes, and visit friends and relatives. The celebration lasts thirteen days and is filled with music, dancing, and feasting. Nowruz is about new beginnings and hope for the year ahead.
Neeroosh — A Somali New Year Tradition

In parts of Somalia and the Somali diaspora, the start of the solar new year has historically been marked by a celebration sometimes called Neeroosh or Dabshid. Traditionally held on or around 21 July, communities would light bonfires at dusk, and children would jump over the flames as a symbol of leaving behind the old year and welcoming the new one. Families gathered outdoors, shared meals of rice and roasted meat, and the elders would recite poetry and tell stories late into the night. Although the way it is celebrated has changed over the generations, the spirit of the occasion remains the same: gratitude for the past year and hope for the year to come. Like many festivals around the world, Neeroosh shows that fire, food, and storytelling are universal ways of bringing people together.
After reading all the festival descriptions, answer these questions:
What do Diwali, Thanksgiving, and Nowruz have in common with Eid al-Fitr and Timkat?
Choose one of these festivals and compare it with a celebration from your own culture. What are the similarities? What are the differences?
Why do you think almost every culture in the world has some kind of harvest or new-year festival?
Critical Thinking: Why Festivals Matter

Festivals are more than parties and food. They carry deep meaning for communities. Think about the following questions and write your answers in full paragraphs. Each answer should be at least three sentences long.

Cultural preservation: Many young people today spend more time online than learning about traditional festivals. Do you think traditional celebrations are in danger of disappearing? What could communities do to keep them alive?

Inclusion and belonging: Festivals often bring together people of different ages, backgrounds, and even beliefs. Think of a celebration you have attended where people who were different from each other came together. How did the festival create a sense of belonging?
The role of food: Almost every festival involves special food. Why do you think food plays such an important role in celebrations? Think about what happens when people cook together and eat together.

Generosity and kindness: During Eid, families share food and give gifts to those in need. During Thanksgiving, people express gratitude. Why is generosity such a common theme in festivals across the world? How does giving to others affect both the giver and the receiver?
Idiom Corner
Idioms are phrases whose meaning cannot be understood from the individual words. Here are four idioms that connect to the themes of this unit. Learn them and try to use them in your own sentences.
1. Catch on — to begin to understand something.
Example: “It took me a while to catch on to the rules of the traditional game they were playing.”
2. By the way — used to introduce something extra or slightly off-topic.
Example: “By the way, did you hear about the cultural festival next weekend?”
3. Nice one — used to praise someone for doing something well.
Example: “You organised the whole event yourself? Nice one!”
4. Hang on — wait for a short time.
Example: “Hang on a minute while I finish decorating this lantern.”
Practice: Write a short dialogue (six to eight lines) between two friends preparing for a festival. Use at least two of the idioms above in your dialogue.
Creative Projects
Choose at least one of these projects to complete during the unit.
Project 1: Festival Scrapbook Page

Create a scrapbook page (on paper or digitally) about a festival you have attended or would like to attend. Include drawings or descriptions, labels with vocabulary words, and at least five sentences describing the event. Use compound adjectives and prepositions of time.
Project 2: Festival Recipe Card
Write a recipe card for a dish that is served at a celebration you know. Include:
The name of the dish and which festival it belongs to.
A list of ingredients.
Step-by-step instructions using clear, sequenced language (First, Next, Then, Finally).
A sentence explaining why this dish is special: “This dish is important because ...”
Project 3: Compare and Contrast Essay
Write a short essay (150–200 words) comparing two festivals: one from the reading passage and one from your own experience. Use a clear structure:
Introduction: Name the two festivals and say what you will compare.
Similarities: What do the two festivals have in common?
Differences: How are they different?
Conclusion: Which festival do you prefer, and why?
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is available 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don’t understand prepositions of time. Can you explain them differently?”
Practise speaking: “Can you interview me about a festival I’ve been to?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my festival description and tell me what to improve?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more exercises on compound adjectives?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher from Unit 2?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet” for each statement. For every “Not yet”, go back to the relevant section or ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can read and understand a text about festivals and celebrations.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use in, on, and at for time correctly.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can form and use compound adjectives (e.g. hand-made, well-known).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can choose the correct participle adjective (-ed or -ing).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a detailed description of a festival using varied vocabulary.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare two festivals and discuss similarities and differences.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use at least ten vocabulary words from this unit confidently.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can read a poem aloud with expression and discuss its meaning.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 3
Great work completing Unit 2! In Unit 3, you will explore Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability — what we wear, why it matters, and how fashion affects the environment.
Make sure you know all the Unit 2 vocabulary words.
Keep practising your prepositions of time in daily conversation.
Try describing what people are wearing using compound adjectives.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 3? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — every unit you complete makes your English stronger. Be patient with yourself, celebrate your progress, and keep going!
tables:
🧭  How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. Work through each section at your own pace: What You Will Learn, Key Vocabulary, Reading, Understanding, Grammar, Practice, Writing, Listening & Speaking, and Check Your Learning.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary (detailed word lists), Grammar (Prepositions for Time Phrases), and the Story “The Hero of Kitale”.
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you need help, and bring your completed work to your teacher sessions.
The benevolent elder organised free meals during Eid.
Having a charm that attracts and inspires people
The charismatic speaker kept the whole crowd listening.
Working hard and paying careful attention
She was diligent in preparing the festival decorations.
The children were enthusiastic about the lantern parade.
The ability to recover from difficulties
Her resilience helped her organise the event despite many setbacks.
To restore a friendly relationship after a disagreement
The festival helped reconcile the two families who had quarrelled.
They served authentic Somali dishes at the community gathering.
The evening after the feast was tranquil and still.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you test me on the Unit 2 vocabulary words?”
“Can you use the word ‘benevolent’ in three different sentences for me?”
“What is the difference between ‘enthusiastic’ and ‘zealous’?”

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read this passage aloud with me and explain the hard words?”
“What does ‘reconciled’ mean in the passage about Eid?”
“Can you tell me more about the Timkat festival?”

💡  Remember: I – O – A
IN = Intervals (long periods: months, years, seasons, parts of day)
ON = Occasions (specific days and dates)
AT = Actual times (clock times, sunrise, midnight, noon)

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Why do we say ‘in the morning’ but ‘at night’?”
“Can you give me ten sentences and I’ll choose in, on, or at?”
“What is the rule for festivals — do we say ‘on Eid’ or ‘at Eid’?”

✍️  Writing checklist
Use at least three prepositions of time (in, on, at) correctly.
Include at least two compound adjectives (e.g. brightly-coloured, hand-stitched).
Use at least one participle adjective (e.g. excited, fascinating).
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit.
Organise your writing into at least three clear paragraphs.
Include sensory details: what you see, hear, smell, taste, and feel.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 2: Celebrations and Festivals
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Hero of Kitale”
The Hero of Kitale

It was early on a Monday morning in April when Amina rose before the sun. The room was still dark, and the only sound was the soft breathing of her younger brother, Musa, asleep on a straw mat beside her bed. Their mud-brick house sat near the edge of Kitale, close to the maize farms that spread out like a green sea across the land.

Amina, now fourteen, had become known in their school as both diligent and punctual. Every teacher said the same thing: “Amina is a paragon of responsibility.” But Amina didn’t care much about praise. What she cared about was the community.

Their town had faced many problems: broken water taps, rising food prices, and a school library whose roof had fallen in during the last rainy season. Some students complained, but Amina chose to act.

Her teacher, Mr. Wafula, was a charismatic man who believed in youth leadership. One day, he announced that the school would be holding its first ever Student Leadership Election. Posters appeared around the compound with names of candidates and bright slogans.

Some candidates were loud and zealous, others charming, and one boy named Felix was incredibly charismatic. Felix came from a prominent family. His uncle owned the largest hardware store in Kitale. His campaign posters were printed, not handwritten. He offered sweets to younger students and even brought a speaker to play music at lunchtime.
“Wow,” whispered Musa as the music played. “Felix is legendary.”
Amina smiled. “He may be loud, but we need a leader who is authentic.”

Amina did not have money for speakers or printed posters. What she had was a notebook, a group of close friends, and ideas. She spent her evenings creating meticulous, hand-drawn posters. They showed a tree growing from dry ground with the words: “Real leaders endure. They don’t just promise. They act.”
Her speeches were not flashy. But they were honest. “We must fix the roof,” she told the school. “We need more books. I cannot promise you music, but I promise to work.”

Amina’s words began to spread. Students whispered about how determined she seemed. Even some teachers mentioned her name in staff meetings. “That girl,” said Ms. Jebet, “has the spirit of a lion. She’s so intrepid.”

On the day of the election, the school hall was filled with a clamor of voices. Felix walked in first, wearing a brightly-coloured, fur-lined jacket. Amina walked in later, wearing her usual neat school uniform, her braids tied back, and a quiet smile on her face.
When the votes were counted, it was close. But Amina won.
Some students cheered. Others were surprised. Felix looked disappointed but shook her hand.

From that day on, Amina became a different kind of leader. She stayed late at school on Thursdays to help younger students with reading. She worked with the head teacher to organise a book drive. Her friend Hassan, who was industrious, helped carry donated books every Friday.

Amina also helped reconcile students who had quarrelled. Once, two girls fought over a missing pen. Amina sat with them under the tree near the tap and helped them talk it out. “We must be impartial,” she reminded them. “We judge by facts, not emotions.”
That same week, she helped build a small garden behind the kitchen, teaching others how to grow sukuma wiki. She asked Mr. Wafula to support them, and he agreed. Her leadership was quiet but steady.
One day, Amina returned home late, tired but happy. Her mother looked up from the stove. “You look exhausted.”
Amina sat beside her. “We planted spinach today. Musa helped too. He’s becoming quite agile, you know.”
Her mother laughed. “Well, that’s good. But don’t forget to rest. Even lions must sleep.”

Despite her busy days, Amina always made time for study. She was tenacious. Even when the lights failed, she lit a kerosene lamp and reviewed her notes. Her aunt once said, “Amina will be legendary one day.”
Then one Friday in June, something unexpected happened.

A teacher’s motorcycle was stolen from outside the school gate. Suspicion fell on a new student named Boniface. He was quiet, often late, and didn’t have many friends. Some students shouted, “He did it!” Others said nothing.
But Amina stood in front of the class. “Let’s not rush. He is innocent until proven guilty. We must be vigilant but also fair.”
The next day, the motorcycle was found abandoned near the river. Boniface had nothing to do with it. Amina had saved him from false blame.
People began to see her not just as a student leader, but as a true role model. Even the elders in town heard of her efforts. One afternoon, a woman named Mama Lillian came to school to donate books.
“You must be the girl,” she said. “My grandson told me you’re the reason he wants to lead.”
Amina blushed. “I’m just doing my part.”
Over time, Amina’s name reached the local newspaper. An article was published titled: “The Benevolent Girl Who Changed Her School.”
One morning, the governor of the county came to visit. He walked through the classrooms and smiled when he saw the small garden. “Who started this?” he asked.
“Amina,” several students said.
The governor turned to her. “You’re quite young. What inspires you?”
Amina replied, “Kindness, honesty, and hard work. I believe if we help one another, we all rise. It’s just benevolence.”
He nodded. “Nice one.”

That weekend, Amina received an invitation to speak at the county youth forum. Her school bus arrived early on Saturday. She wore a clean green dress, hand-stitched by her aunt. It was modest, simple, and very much Amina.
At the forum, she spoke with confidence. Her words were enthusiastic, her manner calm and tranquil. She spoke about resilience, the need to push forward even when things are tough.
“Many people wait for a hero,” she said. “But what if you are the one? What if you can change just one thing?”
The crowd clapped, not because of music or fancy clothes, but because they believed her. Her speech was later posted online, where thousands watched and commented.
On the last day of the term, Mr. Wafula gave Amina a letter. “Scholarship application,” he said. “For a leadership camp in Nairobi. I believe you’d represent Kitale well.”
Amina stared at the form. She had never left their county.
That evening, she sat with her family under the stars. “Should I go?” she asked.
“You should,” her mother said. “Your light is not meant to stay hidden.”
Musa, with eyes full of pride, added, “You’ll do great. And don’t forget to write!”
Amina laughed. “I won’t. But you must take care of the garden. And be punctual!”

As the bus left Kitale on Monday, Amina looked out the window. She saw the farms, the school roof—now repaired—and the smiling faces of students waving goodbye. Her heart swelled, not with pride, but with hope.
She didn’t want fame. She wanted change. And that, she knew, took more than speeches. It took resilience, diligence, and the courage to lead quietly.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer in full sentences, using evidence from the story.
Where does Amina live, and what problems does her town face?
What event does Mr. Wafula announce, and why is it important?
How does Felix’s campaign differ from Amina’s? Give at least two differences.
What does Amina’s poster say, and what message does it send about her values?
After winning the election, what three things does Amina do to help her school community?
How does Amina handle the situation when Boniface is falsely accused?
What does the governor ask Amina, and how does she respond?
At the county youth forum, Amina says: “Many people wait for a hero. But what if you are the one?” What does she mean by this?
Why does Amina’s mother say, “Your light is not meant to stay hidden”?
The story ends with Amina on a bus leaving Kitale. How does she feel, and what does the ending suggest about her future?
Think and Discuss
These are open questions. Share your ideas with a partner or your AI tutor.
The story’s moral is: “True leadership is not about power or fame, but about authentic service, fairness, and resilience.” Do you agree? Can you think of a leader you know who fits this description?
Felix’s campaign was flashy and loud, while Amina’s was simple and honest. Which approach do you think is more effective in real life? Why?
Amina says, “We must be impartial. We judge by facts, not emotions.” Why is this important, especially when someone is accused of something?
How does Amina show resilience throughout the story? Find at least two examples.
If you were running for student leader, what three things would you promise to do for your school?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find each of these Unit 2 vocabulary words and write the sentence they appear in. Then write your own sentence using the same word.
benevolent / benevolence
charismatic
determined
diligent
enthusiastic
industrious
intrepid
legendary
meticulous
paragon
punctual
reconcile
resilience
tenacious
vigilant
zealous
agile
authentic
tranquil
clamor
impartial
prominent
endure
encourage
Language Focus
Prepositions of Time in the Story
Find at least five examples of in, on, or at used for time in the story. Copy each sentence and underline the preposition. Then explain why that preposition was used.
Example: “It was early on a Monday morning in April.” — We use on because Monday is a specific day, and in because April is a month.
Compound Adjectives in the Story
The story contains several compound adjectives. Find them and write them below:
hand-drawn (posters)
brightly-coloured (jacket)
fur-lined (jacket)
hand-stitched (dress)
Now write two sentences of your own using compound adjectives.
Relative Clauses in the Story
Find two sentences in the story that contain a relative clause (using who, whose, that, or which). Copy them and underline the relative clause.
Example: “Her friend Hassan, who was industrious, helped carry donated books every Friday.”
Now You Try: Writing
Character Profile: Write a character profile of Amina (about 100 words). Describe her personality, her actions, and why she is a good leader. Use at least five vocabulary words from the unit.

Alternative Ending: Imagine Amina arrives at the leadership camp in Nairobi. Write a short continuation of the story (150–200 words). What does she learn? Who does she meet? What challenge does she face?
Letter to Amina: Write a letter to Amina telling her what you admired about her and asking her two questions about her experience.
Reflection: In three to four sentences, explain what lesson the story taught you and how you could apply it in your own life.

tables: Kind and generous; wanting to help others.
The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.
Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people.
The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.
Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up.
She was determined to finish her homework before going out to play.
Working hard and being careful to do things well.
She is a diligent student who always completes her homework on time.
Showing great excitement and interest about something.
She was enthusiastic about joining the school soccer team.
Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks.
She is an industrious student who always completes her homework on time.
Brave and not afraid of danger or difficulty.
The intrepid explorer climbed the highest mountain without fear.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor

Very famous and admired, often because of a special story or history.
The legendary hero saved the village from the dragon.
Very careful and paying great attention to details.
She was meticulous when painting her model, making sure every tiny part was perfect.
A person or thing that is a perfect example of a particular quality.
She is a paragon of kindness because she always helps others.
It is important to be punctual for school so you do not miss any classes.
Not giving up easily; holding on with great determination.
She was tenacious in her efforts to finish the race, even when she felt tired.
Always watching carefully and ready to notice anything wrong or dangerous.
The vigilant guard kept an eye on the entrance to make sure no one entered without permission.
Very enthusiastic and eager about something.
She was zealous about helping her friends with their homework.

To make two people or groups friendly again after a disagreement.
After their argument, they talked and tried to reconcile their differences.
To do something that someone asks you to do; to help.
I will oblige my friend by helping him with his homework.
To give someone support or confidence to do something.
My teacher always encourages me to try my best in class.
To continue to exist or to suffer through difficult times.
She had to endure the cold weather while waiting for the bus.
The quality of being kind and wanting to help others.
Her benevolence was shown when she donated her allowance to help the local animal shelter.
The ability to recover quickly from difficulties or challenges.
Her resilience helped her overcome the challenges she faced in school.
A loud noise or outcry, especially from a crowd.
The clamor of the crowd made it hard to hear the speaker.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor

“Can you explain the difference between ‘endure’ and ‘resilience’?”
Able to move quickly and easily.
The cat is very agile and can jump from one roof to another.
Very pleasant or attractive; able to make people like you.
The charming puppy won everyone’s heart at the pet store.
The prominent scientist gave a speech about climate change.
Not taking sides; treating everyone equally and fairly.
The judge was impartial and made a fair decision for both sides.
Not strict; allowing some freedom or flexibility.
The teacher was lenient and gave us extra time to finish our homework.
Real and genuine; not fake or copied.
The museum has an authentic dinosaur skeleton on display.
Calm and peaceful; not disturbed or noisy.
The lake was tranquil in the early morning, with no waves or sounds.

A group of people moving forward in an orderly way, often as part of a ceremony.
The colourful procession wound through the streets during the festival.
A large, special meal; to eat and drink plentifully.
The family prepared a magnificent feast to celebrate Eid.
The feeling of being thankful and appreciative.
She expressed her gratitude to everyone who helped organise the event.
A custom or belief passed down through generations.
It is a tradition in our family to share dates at the start of every Eid.
A formal event performed on a special occasion.
The opening ceremony of the festival included speeches and traditional dances.
Something added to make a place look more attractive.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 3: Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Five grammar lessons

This file contains five grammar lessons that support Unit 3. Each lesson explains a rule, gives clear examples, warns you about common mistakes, and ends with a short practice activity. Work through one or two lessons a day. Say the example sentences out loud -- grammar sticks better when you hear it! Check your answers using the Answer Key at the end of this file.
Lesson 1: Multi-Word Verbs
What the rule means and why it matters

Multi-word verbs (also called phrasal verbs) are special combinations of a main verb and one or more particles (small words like on, off, up, down, away, out). Together, these words create a new meaning that is often different from the meaning of the verb alone. For example, "put" means to place something, but "put on" means to wear something. Multi-word verbs are extremely common in everyday English, and knowing them will help you communicate more naturally.
How to form and use multi-word verbs
Identify the main verb: The first part is usually a common verb (put, take, give, throw, turn, look, carry).
Add a particle: The second part is a preposition or adverb (on, off, up, down, away, out, after) that changes the verb's meaning.
Combine them: The verb and particle together form the multi-word verb.
Use in a sentence: Place the multi-word verb where a normal verb would go.
Examples with clothing and sustainability
put on = to wear: "I will put on my warm coat before going outside."
take off = to remove: "Please take off your shoes at the door."
dress up = to wear smart or special clothes: "We like to dress up for Eid."
throw away = to discard: "Do not throw away clothes that can be mended."
give away = to give for free: "We give away clothes we have outgrown."
try on = to test if clothes fit: "She tried on three scarves before choosing one."
turn off = to switch off: "Turn off the sewing machine when you finish."
carry out = to do or complete: "The students carried out a survey on fast fashion."
Separable and inseparable multi-word verbs
Some multi-word verbs are separable -- you can put the object between the verb and the particle:
"I put my jacket on." or "I put on my jacket." (both correct)
"She threw the old dress away." or "She threw away the old dress." (both correct)
When the object is a pronoun (it, them, her, him), you must separate:
"I put it on." (correct)   NOT "I put on it." (incorrect)
"She threw them away." (correct)   NOT "She threw away them." (incorrect)
Some multi-word verbs are inseparable -- the verb and particle must stay together:
"She looks after her little brother." (correct)   NOT "She looks her little brother after." (incorrect)
"We came across an old sewing machine in the attic." (correct)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using the wrong particle. "Put off your shoes" means to delay, not to remove!
Fix: "Take off your shoes" is correct for removing them.
Mistake: Forgetting irregular past forms. "I putted on my jacket."
Fix: "put" does not change: "I put on my jacket" (past tense is the same).
Memory tip

Think of multi-word verbs as a team: the verb is the player and the particle is the coach that changes the game plan. "Put" alone is ordinary, but "put on", "put off", "put up with" -- each coach gives it a totally different strategy!
Practice 1
Choose the correct multi-word verb to complete each sentence:
I need to ______ my sweater because it is cold outside. (put on / take off)
Can you ______ the radio? It is too loud! (turn up / turn off)
We will ______ for the school ceremony tonight! (dress down / dress up)
Please do not ______ that plastic bottle. Recycle it instead. (throw away / give away)
She ______ the dress in the shop, but it did not fit. (tried on / put on)
We ______ our old school uniforms to younger students every year. (give away / throw away)
Lesson 2: Present Passive
What the rule means and why it matters

In English, we usually talk about who does an action: "Farmers grow cotton." But sometimes we want to focus on what happens to the object rather than who does it. The present passive lets us do this: "Cotton is grown in many countries." This is especially useful when we do not know who did the action, when the doer is not important, or when we want to focus on the process or result.
How to form the present passive
The structure is: subject + is / are + past participle.
Identify the object of the active sentence: "Workers sew the uniforms." (Object: the uniforms)
Move the object to the front to become the new subject: "The uniforms..."
Use is (singular) or are (plural) + the past participle of the main verb: "The uniforms are sewn."
Add 'by' + doer (optional): "The uniforms are sewn by workers."
Examples
"Cotton is grown in Egypt, India, and the United States."
"Clothes are made in factories around the world."
"Water is used in huge amounts to dye fabrics."
"Old garments are collected and sorted for recycling."
"The school uniform is designed by a local tailor."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: "The books are write."
Fix: Use the past participle: "The books are written."
Mistake: "The cake is bake by my mother."
Fix: "The cake is baked by my mother."
Memory tip
Think of the present passive as asking: "What happens to the object?" If you can answer that question, you are on the right track!
Practice 2
Fill in the blanks with the correct present passive form of the verb in brackets:
The letter __________ (send) by the postman every morning.
These dresses __________ (make) from organic cotton.
The school uniforms __________ (design) by a local company.
Plastic bottles __________ (collect) for recycling every Friday.
The fabric __________ (dye) using natural colours from plants.
Lesson 3: Modal Verbs + Passive Voice
What the rule means and why it matters

Modal verbs (can, could, must, should, will, might, may) express possibility, ability, permission, obligation, or advice. When we combine them with the passive voice, we can talk about what should, must, or can be done to something -- without naming who does it. This is very useful for rules, recommendations, and formal writing.
How to form it
The structure is: modal verb + be + past participle.
"Old clothes should be recycled, not thrown away."
"Waste must be reduced to protect the environment."
"Natural fabrics can be composted at the end of their life."
"This book may be borrowed from the library."
"New designs will be created from recycled materials."
"Mistakes might be corrected later."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Incorrect word order. "Should cited be the book."
Fix: modal + be + past participle: "The book should be cited."
Mistake: Forgetting the past participle. "The homework should be do."
Fix: "The homework should be done."
Mistake: Mixing active and passive. "You should write the report" is active. The passive version is: "The report should be written."
Memory tip
Remember M-B-P: Modal + Be + Past participle. This simple formula will keep your sentences in the right order every time!
Practice 3
Complete each sentence using the correct modal + passive structure:
The project ______ (should / finish) by next week.
This song ______ (can / sing) at the school concert.
The instructions ______ (must / follow) carefully.
The old uniforms ______ (could / donate) to charity.
More trees ______ (should / plant) around the school.
Lesson 4: Strong and Weak Obligation
What the rule means and why it matters

In English, we use different words to express how necessary something is. Understanding the difference between strong obligation (you have no choice) and weak obligation (it is a good idea, but you can choose) helps you communicate clearly about rules, advice, and expectations.
Strong obligation
Use must or have to when something is required or there is no choice:
must (the speaker feels it is necessary): "You must wear a seatbelt in the car."
have to (an external rule requires it): "Students have to wear school uniform every day."
"I have to finish my eco-project report by Friday."
"We must reduce plastic waste to protect the oceans."
Weak obligation
Use should or might when something is recommended or suggested, but not required:
should (advice or recommendation): "You should try to buy fewer new clothes."
might (a gentle suggestion or possibility): "You might want to repair that tear instead of buying a new shirt."
"We should reuse bags instead of throwing them away."
"You might enjoy the upcycling workshop after school."
Key differences
must comes from the speaker's own feeling of necessity.
have to often comes from an outside rule, law, or authority.
should is for advice -- it is a good idea, but you can choose.
might is the gentlest -- it suggests a possibility.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "should" for a strict rule: "You should wear a seatbelt" (too weak for a safety rule).
Fix: "You must wear a seatbelt." (strong obligation)
Mistake: Confusing "must" and "have to": "I must go to the dentist" (if it is an appointment, not your personal feeling).
Fix: "I have to go to the dentist." (external requirement)
Memory tip
Think of must and have to as hard rules -- like walls you cannot walk through. Think of should and might as soft guides -- like signposts that point the way but let you choose your path.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with must, have to, should, or might:
You ______ eat your vegetables to stay healthy. (weak obligation)
I ______ finish my project by Friday! (strong obligation -- external deadline)
You ______ wear your coat; it is freezing outside! (strong obligation -- personal feeling)
We ______ visit the recycling centre this weekend. (gentle suggestion)
Students ______ hand in their homework on time. (school rule)
You ______ try upcycling old clothes -- it is fun! (advice)
Lesson 5: Non-Defining Relative Clauses
What the rule means and why it matters

A non-defining relative clause adds extra information about a noun. It does not change the main meaning of the sentence -- it just gives us more detail. Non-defining relative clauses are separated from the rest of the sentence by commas, and they use who (for people) or which (for things). They make your writing more interesting and informative.
How to form non-defining relative clauses
Identify the noun you want to add information about: "My grandmother makes beautiful dresses."
Think of the extra detail: "She is a tailor."
Use who (people) or which (things) to connect: "My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses."
Place commas before and after the clause.
Examples
"My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses." (who = people)
"Cotton, which needs a lot of water to grow, is one of the most common fabrics." (which = things)
"The Eco Club, which meets every Thursday, organised a clothing swap." (which = things)
"Mr. Girma, who is the school principal, praised Lomi's writing." (who = people)
"Polyester, which is made from oil, takes hundreds of years to decompose." (which = things)
Important rules
Always use commas before and after the clause.
Do NOT use "that" in non-defining relative clauses. Use who or which only.
The sentence must make sense even if you remove the relative clause.
Test: "My grandmother makes beautiful dresses." -- still makes sense without the clause. The clause ("who is a tailor") is extra, not essential.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Forgetting commas. "My brother who is very talented loves football."
Fix: Add commas: "My brother, who is very talented, loves football."
Mistake: Using "that" instead of "who" or "which". "The car, that is red, belongs to my mum."
Fix: "The car, which is red, belongs to my mum."
Memory tip

Think of non-defining relative clauses as bonus gifts of information. They are like sprinkles on a cupcake -- the cupcake is complete without them, but sprinkles make it extra special! Always wrap them in commas, and use who for people and which for things.
Practice 5
Combine each pair of sentences using a non-defining relative clause. Remember to add commas!
My best friend is very creative. She loves to draw.
The school library has many books. It was built last year.
Mr. Ahmed teaches us English. He is from Djibouti.
Fast fashion produces a lot of waste. It is a growing global problem.
Lomi wrote poems in her red notebook. She lived in a small highland village.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Multi-Word Verbs)
1. put on   2. turn off   3. dress up   4. throw away   5. tried on   6. give away
Practice 2 (Present Passive)
1. is sent   2. are made   3. are designed   4. are collected   5. is dyed
Practice 3 (Modal + Passive)
1. should be finished   2. can be sung   3. must be followed   4. could be donated   5. should be planted
Practice 4 (Strong and Weak Obligation)
1. should   2. have to   3. must   4. might   5. have to   6. should
Practice 5 (Non-Defining Relative Clauses)
1. My best friend, who loves to draw, is very creative.
2. The school library, which was built last year, has many books.
3. Mr. Ahmed, who is from Djibouti, teaches us English.
4. Fast fashion, which is a growing global problem, produces a lot of waste.
5. Lomi, who lived in a small highland village, wrote poems in her red notebook.
