💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you check my grammar answers?"
"Why do we say 'Cotton is grown' and not 'Cotton is grow'?"
"Can you give me five more sentences to practise the passive voice?"
"Can you explain the difference between must, should, and might?"
"Can you help me add a non-defining relative clause to my writing?"

paragraphs: Unit 3: Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 3. This unit explores the world of clothing, fashion, and sustainability. You will learn about how clothes are made, why the choices we make as consumers matter, and what people around the world are doing to protect the environment through sustainable fashion. You will also build your skills in multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs like "put on", "dress up" and "turn off") and learn how to use the passive voice to describe processes and actions. Alongside the topic work, you will read the story "A Voice in the Valley", about a quiet girl named Lomi in rural Ethiopia who discovers that her writing can inspire an entire community. By the end of the unit, you will write an eco-project report, give a short speech about sustainable fashion, and feel confident using passive structures and multi-word verbs in your own writing.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use vocabulary related to clothing, materials, fast fashion, and the environment.
Understand and use multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) such as put on, take off, dress up, throw away, and give away.
Form sentences in the present passive ("Cotton is grown in many countries") and past passive ("The dress was made by hand").
Combine modal verbs with the passive voice ("Clothes should be recycled", "Waste must be reduced").
Distinguish between strong obligation (must, have to) and weak obligation (should, might).
Add extra information to sentences using non-defining relative clauses ("My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses").
Use literary vocabulary (simile, metaphor, imagery, personification) to analyse and improve your own writing.
Read and respond to texts about fashion and sustainability.
Write an eco-project report with clear structure and persuasive language.
Prepare and deliver a short speech about sustainable fashion choices.
Key Vocabulary Preview
Before you begin, look through these important words. You will meet them throughout the unit. Full definitions and practice activities are in your separate Vocabulary file.
Topic vocabulary
These words relate to the unit theme of clothing, fashion, and the environment:
fast fashion -- cheap clothing produced quickly and in large quantities, often causing waste and pollution.
sustainable -- able to continue over time without harming people or the environment.
upcycle -- to take something old or unwanted and turn it into something new and useful.
textile -- any type of cloth or woven fabric, such as cotton, silk, or polyester.
garment -- a piece of clothing.
landfill -- a place where rubbish and waste are buried in the ground.
carbon footprint -- the total amount of greenhouse gases produced by our actions.
ethical -- acting in a way that is fair, honest, and does not harm others.
consumer -- a person who buys goods or services.
recycle -- to process used materials so they can be made into new products.
Literary vocabulary
These words help you analyse stories and improve your writing:
imagery -- words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
simile -- comparing two things using like or as ("Her words flowed like river water").
metaphor -- saying something is something else to show a similarity ("The moon is a mirror").
personification -- giving human qualities to non-human things ("The wind whispered secrets").
alliteration -- repeating the same sound at the beginning of several words ("Peter Piper picked peppers").
narrate -- to tell a story or describe events in order.
dialogue -- a conversation between two or more people in a story or play.
figurative -- using words in a way that is not literal, to create a special effect.
persuade -- to convince someone to do or believe something.
thesis -- a statement or main idea that someone wants to prove in their writing.
Reading: Fashion, Faith, and Our Planet
Read this passage carefully. It is about clothing choices, sustainability, and how young people can make a difference. Pay attention to the vocabulary words you have just learned.

When you open your wardrobe each morning, you make a choice. You might put on a cotton thobe, a colourful dirac, a school uniform, or a pair of jeans and a T-shirt. But have you ever stopped to think about where those clothes came from, who made them, and what happens to them when you throw them away?

The modern fashion industry is one of the biggest polluters on Earth. Every year, around one hundred billion garments are produced worldwide. Many of them are made from cheap synthetic textiles like polyester, which is produced from oil. These clothes are often worn only a few times before they are thrown away. In fact, the average person today buys sixty percent more clothing than people did just fifteen years ago, and keeps each item for about half as long. This cycle of buying, wearing briefly, and discarding is called fast fashion.

Fast fashion harms the environment in several ways. Growing cotton uses huge amounts of water: it takes about ten thousand litres to produce a single pair of jeans. Dyeing fabrics releases toxic chemicals into rivers and lakes. Transporting clothes from factories in one country to shops in another adds to carbon emissions. And when unwanted clothes end up in landfill, they can take hundreds of years to decompose, releasing harmful gases as they break down.

However, there is hope. Around the world, young people are finding creative solutions. Some organise clothing swaps, where friends and neighbours exchange garments they no longer wear instead of throwing them away. Others learn to upcycle old clothes: a worn-out shirt can be turned into a tote bag, and faded jeans can become a pencil case. In East Africa, tailors and seamstresses have a long tradition of repairing and reimagining garments, giving them a second life. Many Muslim families already practise modest and mindful consumption, choosing quality over quantity and passing clothes down through the family.

Schools are getting involved too. Some have started eco-fashion projects, where students design outfits from recycled materials and present them in a fashion show. Others write reports and give speeches to persuade their communities to buy less and choose better. These projects show that fashion can be both beautiful and sustainable.

The next time you get dressed, think about the story behind your clothes. Every garment has a journey -- from the field where the cotton was grown, to the factory where it was sewn, to the shop where it was sold. By choosing carefully, repairing what we have, and sharing what we no longer need, we can all help protect the planet we share. As the saying goes, the most sustainable garment is the one already in your wardrobe.
Understanding the Reading
Comprehension questions
Answer these questions in full sentences:
What is fast fashion? Explain it in your own words.
Name three ways that fast fashion harms the environment, according to the passage.
How much water does it take to produce a single pair of jeans?
What is upcycling? Give one example from the passage and one of your own.
How do some schools help fight fast fashion?
The passage says "the most sustainable garment is the one already in your wardrobe." What do you think this means?
Vocabulary in context
Find these words in the passage and explain what each one means:
garments
synthetic textiles
landfill
carbon emissions
sustainable
Language analysis
Look at these sentences from the passage and answer the questions:
"Mountains of textiles end up in landfill." Is the word "mountains" used literally or figuratively here? What effect does it create?
"The most sustainable garment is the one already in your wardrobe." This is the final sentence. Why do you think the writer chose to end with this idea?
"Every garment has a journey." What does the word "journey" suggest about the life of a piece of clothing?
The passage uses the passive voice: "Around one hundred billion garments are produced worldwide." Why is the passive voice a good choice here? Who is the doer?
Think and discuss
Do you or your family already do anything to reduce clothing waste? What could you do differently?
Why do you think fast fashion is so popular, even though it is harmful?
The passage mentions that many Muslim families practise mindful consumption. How does your faith or culture guide the way your family buys and uses clothes?
If you could design one school project to fight fast fashion, what would it be?
Imagine you are a piece of clothing. Tell the story of your "journey" from the cotton field to a landfill. How would you feel? What would you want people to know?
Grammar Focus: Multi-Word Verbs and Passive Voice
This unit covers two important grammar areas. Your separate Grammar file has full lessons with lots of practice. Here is a summary to get you started.
Multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs)
A multi-word verb is a verb made up of two or three words that work together to create a new meaning. The main verb combines with a particle (a small word like on, off, up, down, away).
Examples related to clothing and fashion:
put on -- to wear something: "I put on my jacket before going outside."
take off -- to remove something: "Please take off your shoes at the door."
dress up -- to wear smart or special clothes: "We dress up for Eid celebrations."
throw away -- to put in the bin: "Do not throw away clothes that can be repaired."
give away -- to give something to someone for free: "We give away clothes we have outgrown."
try on -- to put on clothes to see if they fit: "She tried on three dresses before choosing one."
turn off -- to stop a machine working: "Turn off the sewing machine when you finish."

Some multi-word verbs are separable (you can put the object between the verb and the particle): "I put my coat on" or "I put on my coat." Others are inseparable (the verb and particle must stay together): "She looks after her little brother" -- NOT "She looks her little brother after."
Present passive
We use the present passive when we want to focus on what happens to something, rather than who does the action. Form: is / are + past participle.
"Cotton is grown in many countries."
"Clothes are made in factories around the world."
"Water is used in large amounts to dye fabrics."
"Old garments are collected and sorted for recycling."
Past passive
The past passive describes completed actions. Form: was / were + past participle.
"The dress was designed by a local tailor."
"The uniforms were sewn by hand."
"The report was written by a group of Year 7 students."
Modal verbs + passive
We can combine modal verbs (can, could, must, should, will, might) with the passive voice. Form: modal + be + past participle.
"Old clothes should be recycled, not thrown away."
"Waste must be reduced to protect the planet."
"Natural fabrics can be composted at the end of their life."
"New designs will be created from recycled materials."
Strong and weak obligation
Strong obligation means there is no choice -- you have to do it:
must: "You must wear a uniform to school."
have to: "I have to finish my report by Friday."
Weak obligation means it is a good idea, but you can choose:
should: "You should try to buy fewer new clothes."
might: "You might want to repair that tear instead of throwing the shirt away."
Non-defining relative clauses
A non-defining relative clause adds extra information about a noun. It is separated by commas and uses who (for people) or which (for things). You cannot use that.
"My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses."
"Cotton, which needs a lot of water to grow, is one of the most common fabrics."
"The Eco Club, which meets every Thursday, organised a clothing swap."
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Multi-word verbs -- fill in the blank
Choose the correct multi-word verb to complete each sentence.
Please ______ your coat before you go outside. It is cold today. (put on / take off)
Do not ______ plastic bags. Use them again! (throw away / dress up)
We ______ old clothes to the charity shop every month. (give away / turn off)
She went to the shop to ______ a new hijab. (try on / put on)
We should ______ for the school ceremony on Friday. (dress up / take off)
Answers: 1. put on   2. throw away   3. give away   4. try on   5. dress up
Exercise 2: Active to passive
Rewrite each active sentence in the passive voice.
Active: "Farmers grow cotton in Egypt."   Passive: "Cotton ______ in Egypt."
Active: "Workers sew the uniforms by hand."   Passive: "The uniforms ______ by hand."
Active: "A local designer created the dress."   Passive: "The dress ______ by a local designer."
Active: "Schools should recycle old textbooks."   Passive: "Old textbooks ______ by schools."
Answers: 1. is grown   2. are sewn   3. was created   4. should be recycled
Exercise 3: Strong or weak obligation?
Decide whether the sentence uses strong obligation (S) or weak obligation (W).
You must wear a seatbelt in the car. ( )
You should try to drink more water. ( )
Students have to hand in their homework on time. ( )
You might want to bring an umbrella tomorrow. ( )
We should reduce, reuse, and recycle whenever we can. ( )
Answers: 1. S   2. W   3. S   4. W   5. W
Exercise 4: Add a non-defining relative clause
Combine each pair of sentences using a non-defining relative clause with who or which.
My sister is a fashion designer. She studied in London.
Polyester is a synthetic fabric. It is made from oil.
Amina started an Eco Club. She cares deeply about the environment.
Fast fashion is a growing problem. It produces enormous waste.
Example answers:

1. My sister, who studied in London, is a fashion designer.  2. Polyester, which is made from oil, is a synthetic fabric.  3. Amina, who cares deeply about the environment, started an Eco Club.  4. Fast fashion, which produces enormous waste, is a growing problem.
Exercise 5: Vocabulary in sentences
Use each word in a sentence of your own about clothing or sustainability.
sustainable
upcycle
garment
landfill
ethical
Writing Workshop: Eco-Project Report

In this section you will plan, draft, and write a short eco-project report. Imagine your school has started a sustainability project related to clothing. You will write a report about the project for your school newsletter or website.
Step 1: Choose your project
Pick one of these project ideas, or think of your own:
A clothing swap event at school, where students exchange clothes they no longer wear.
An upcycling workshop, where old clothes are turned into new items like bags, cushion covers, or headbands.
A poster campaign to persuade students and families to buy less fast fashion.
A fashion show using outfits made entirely from recycled or upcycled materials.
Step 2: Plan your report
Use this structure:
Title: Give your report a clear, interesting title.
Introduction: What is the project? Why did your school start it? (2-3 sentences)
What we did: Describe the main activities. Use past passive where possible ("Posters were designed...", "Old clothes were collected..."). (4-5 sentences)
Results: What happened? How many people took part? What did you achieve? (2-3 sentences)
Conclusion: What did you learn? What should happen next? Use modal + passive ("More events should be organised..."). (2-3 sentences)
Step 3: Draft your report
Write a first draft. Aim for 200 to 300 words. Remember to:
Use at least three multi-word verbs (for example: give away, throw away, set up, carry out, find out).
Use at least two passive voice sentences.
Use at least one non-defining relative clause.
Use persuasive language to convince the reader that sustainability matters.
Check your spelling, punctuation, and paragraph structure.
Step 4: Edit and improve
Read your draft out loud. Then check:
Does every sentence start with a capital letter and end with a full stop?
Have I used the passive voice correctly?
Have I included multi-word verbs?
Is my report clear and well-organised?
Would a reader be persuaded to support this kind of project?
Ask your AI tutor or a classmate to read your report and give feedback before you write the final version.
Example opening paragraph

"Last month, our school set up a clothing swap to help reduce textile waste. Old garments were collected from every class, and a special event was organised in the school hall. The Eco Club, which meets every Thursday, planned the whole project. Over fifty students took part, and more than one hundred items of clothing were exchanged. No clothes were thrown away -- everything found a new home."
Critical Thinking: Fashion Choices and Consequences
Good learners do not just memorise facts -- they think carefully about what they read and form their own opinions. Use the questions below to practise your critical thinking skills.
Fast fashion consequences
Read each statement and decide whether you agree or disagree. Write two sentences to explain your opinion, using evidence from the reading passage or your own experience.
"Fast fashion is not the fault of consumers -- companies are to blame."
"It is impossible for a young person to avoid fast fashion completely."
"Governments should impose strict rules on how clothing companies produce their products."
"Buying second-hand clothes is better for the environment than buying new clothes."
Upcycling challenge
Look at this list of old or unwanted items. For each one, think of a creative way to upcycle it into something new and useful:
An old pair of jeans with holes in the knees.
A T-shirt that is too small for you.
A worn-out school bag.
A pile of fabric scraps left over from sewing.
Share your ideas with a partner, your teacher, or your AI tutor. Which idea is the most creative? Which would be the easiest to do at home?
Listening and Speaking
Listening 1: A radio interview about fast fashion
Ask your AI tutor to read this interview aloud, or read it with a partner. One person is the interviewer (I) and the other is the guest (G), a young fashion designer called Amira.
I: Welcome to the show, Amira. You design clothes from recycled materials. How did you start?

G: Thank you! I started when I was about twelve. My mother, who is a seamstress, taught me to sew. One day I found a pile of old curtains that were going to be thrown away. I thought, "Why not turn these into something beautiful?" I made a skirt and a bag. My friends loved them.
I: That is amazing. Why do you think sustainable fashion is important?

G: Fast fashion produces so much waste. Clothes are made cheaply, worn a few times, and then thrown away. Mountains of textiles end up in landfill. I believe fashion should be enjoyed, but it must be done responsibly. We should buy less, choose well, and make our clothes last longer.
I: What advice would you give to young people?
G: Learn to mend and upcycle. Organise a swap with your friends. And remember -- style is not about buying new things all the time. It is about expressing who you are with what you have.
After listening, answer:
How old was Amira when she started making clothes from recycled materials?
Who taught Amira to sew?
What did Amira make from the old curtains?
What three pieces of advice does Amira give to young people?
Find one non-defining relative clause in the interview.
Find one sentence that uses the passive voice.
Listening 2: A short sustainability pledge
Listen to this pledge (or read it aloud with your class):

"I promise to look after my clothes and repair them when they are torn. I promise to give away clothes I have outgrown instead of throwing them away. I promise to think before I buy, and to choose quality over quantity. Together, we can protect our planet, one garment at a time."
Discussion: Would you make this pledge? Is there anything you would add or change?
Speaking 1: Describe what you are wearing
Stand up and describe your outfit to a partner or your AI tutor. Use at least two multi-word verbs and two adjectives:
"Today I put on my blue school uniform. The shirt, which is made of cotton, feels comfortable."
"I also put on a warm jumper because it is cold outside."
"My shoes are brown. They were given to me by my older cousin."
Speaking 2: Persuasive speech -- Our school should fight fast fashion
Prepare a short speech (1-2 minutes) to persuade your classmates that your school should take action against fast fashion. Use the planning frame below:
Opening: State your opinion clearly. ("I believe our school should...")
Reason 1: Give your first reason with evidence. ("Fast fashion is harmful because...")
Reason 2: Give a second reason. ("Furthermore, millions of garments are thrown away...")
Solution: Suggest what the school could do. ("We should organise...")
Closing: End with a strong final sentence. ("Together, we can make a difference.")
Tips for speaking well:
Speak clearly and at a steady pace.
Look at your audience, not at your notes.
Use persuasive words: should, must, important, together, imagine.
Practise at least twice before your final performance.
Connecting to the Story: A Voice in the Valley

Your companion Story file contains "A Voice in the Valley", about Lomi, a quiet girl living in a highland village in Ethiopia. Lomi loves to write -- she fills her red notebook with poems, stories, and figurative language. When her school launches a journal, Lomi finds the courage to share her voice, and in doing so, she inspires a whole community of young writers.
After reading the story, think about these connections to Unit 3:
Lomi uses imagery, simile, and metaphor in her writing. Can you find examples in the story?
Lomi had to overcome fear to share her work. Have you ever been nervous about sharing something you created?
The story shows that one voice can start a movement. How is this similar to one student starting an eco-fashion project at school?
Lomi's story teaches that creativity and courage can change a community. How can creativity help solve the problem of fast fashion?
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I do not understand. Can you explain it in a different way?"
Practise speaking: "Can you listen to me describe my outfit and give me feedback?"
Get feedback on writing: "Can you check my eco-project report for grammar mistakes?"
Ask for more practice: "Can you give me more sentences to practise the passive voice?"
Prepare for your teacher: "What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?"
Explore the topic: "Can you tell me more about sustainable fashion in East Africa?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Tick "Yes" or "Not yet". For every "Not yet", ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can use vocabulary about clothing, fashion, and sustainability.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use multi-word verbs like put on, throw away, and give away.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can form present passive and past passive sentences.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use modal verbs with the passive voice (should be recycled, must be reduced).   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I know the difference between strong and weak obligation.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can add extra information using non-defining relative clauses.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write an eco-project report with clear structure.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can give a short persuasive speech about sustainable fashion.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I have read and understood the story "A Voice in the Valley".   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can identify literary devices (simile, metaphor, imagery, personification) in a text.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Getting Ready for Unit 4

Next you will study Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions. You will describe different types of transport, compare cities, and learn about green innovations in urban planning. The grammar skills you have learned in Unit 3 -- passive voice, multi-word verbs, and non-defining relative clauses -- will be very useful!
Make sure you know all the Unit 3 vocabulary.
Finish your eco-project report and share it with your teacher.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 4? Can you get me ready?"

Well done on completing Unit 3. You have learned about an important global topic -- sustainability -- and you have built strong grammar skills that will help you throughout Year 7 and beyond. Be proud of your progress, and keep writing with courage, just like Lomi.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 3: Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “A Voice in the Valley”
A Voice in the Valley

The sun rose slowly over the green ridges of the valley, painting golden light on the rooftops of houses made of clay and straw. In the village of Selota, people woke with the rhythm of roosters, the smell of injera cooking, and the soft chanting of morning prayers.

At the heart of this small village lived Lomi, a quiet girl with a loud imagination. Her name meant "lemon," but her thoughts were sweet and strong, like spiced tea. Lomi kept a red notebook under her bed, where she loved to narrate stories, write poems, and paraphrase tales she had heard from her grandmother.

Though her voice was soft, her words were bold. In her stories, trees could walk, rivers could whisper, and the sun signified hope. Her writing was full of personification, similes, and metaphors, which made even the cows seem like scholars and the mountains like sleeping giants.
But no one else ever read her notebook.
“You should share your writing,” her older brother Bekele often said. “People would be amazed.”
Lomi would shake her head. “No. They’ll laugh. They’ll say it’s childish.”
Bekele made an assertion that stuck in her mind: “A voice kept silent is like a drum never played.”

At school, Lomi was invisible. She was not the student who won races or spoke in assemblies. But in literature class, when their teacher asked them to find imagery in a poem, Lomi’s hand always went up.
“Imagery,” she said once, “is not just about what you see. It’s what you feel. The sound of a lonely flute can make your heart shiver.”
Her teacher, Miss Alem, smiled. “That’s quite an inference.”
One Thursday afternoon, Miss Alem brought a surprise. “We are launching a school journal. Anyone may submit a story, poem, or article. The best ones will be published.”
Lomi’s heart fluttered.

That night, she sat under the mosquito net with her red notebook. Her hands trembled as she tore out two pages. Her story was about a boy who could hear the stars speak. She had used alliteration, rhyme, and a strong voice.
“You must submit this,” Bekele said when he read it. “It should be shared, Lomi.”
She hesitated, then dropped the story into the submission box the next morning.
Weeks passed. Nothing happened.

Then one Monday in July, a poster appeared on the school wall. The journal had been printed. It was titled Echoes of the Highlands. Lomi’s eyes widened when she saw her name under a story titled “Star Listener.”
Beneath the title was a short caption: “A tale where silence speaks louder than words.”
Students crowded around the journal. Some read aloud. Others laughed, clapped, or nodded. A boy pointed at Lomi and said, “This is yours?”
She nodded shyly.
“Your words are powerful,” said Frehiwot, a girl from another class. “How did you write like that?”
Lomi smiled. “I just listened. And I wrote.”
Her story became the talk of the week. Even the school principal, Mr. Girma — who, until then, rarely spoke to students — approached her and said, “You have a gift.”
That week, Miss Alem asked Lomi to help her lead a creative writing session.
“You’ll teach students how to use figurative language,” she said. “You can explain repetition, rhythm, and rhyme. Oh — and the connotation of words too.”
Lomi was nervous. But she agreed.

She prepared her lesson carefully. She used coloured chalk to write examples on the blackboard. She explained that metaphors, like “The moon is a mirror,” make writing richer. She asked, “What does ‘The wind danced’ mean?”
A hand shot up. “It’s personification!”
Lomi grinned. “Exactly.”

During her lesson, she used examples from her story. “Good writers emphasize emotion. They use dialogue to make readers feel connected. A well-placed simile, like ‘as brave as a lion’, helps readers imagine better.”
She also showed students how to infer meaning from a sentence and how to structure their stories in chronological order.
Later that day, Bekele teased her. “Now you’re the teacher!”
“Only a little,” she laughed. “But it feels good.”
News of her teaching spread. Lomi was invited to speak at a youth arts event in the town nearby. She almost said no.
“I can’t. What if I fail?”
“You might fail,” Bekele said. “But your story might be remembered, too. That’s a risk worth taking.”
At the event, she read a new poem filled with rhythm, rhyme, and heart. Her words flowed like river water. The crowd was silent, listening.
One judge stood and made an assertion: “This poem must be shared with schools across the region.”
The next month, it was. Lomi’s poem was printed in three school newsletters.
When reporters visited her school, Lomi was asked, “How did you start?”
She said, “With a notebook I hid under my bed.”

Lomi’s success inspired others. Her classmate Haile, who had never shared a poem before, wrote a piece full of figurative beauty. Frehiwot began a comic strip series. Even shy Bilen submitted an essay about her grandfather’s garden.
Miss Alem told the class, “Creativity should be encouraged, not hidden. And sometimes, a story must be told so that others can find the courage to tell theirs.”
The writing club, which had once been empty, now had over twenty students.

At the end of the term, a ceremony was held at the community hall. Lomi was called to the stage to receive a certificate of excellence. A banner behind her read: “The Pen Paints What the Voice Feels.”
As she held the certificate, she thought of all the students who once feared to speak.
“This,” she said softly, “is not just for me. It’s for every quiet voice that dares to speak.”
The crowd stood to clap.

In the weeks that followed, the journal grew. More pages, more stories, more young writers who felt brave enough to write. Lomi began helping to edit the submissions, teaching others about punctuation and tone. “Even punctuation,” she told one younger student, “can shape how a story is perceived.”

She also began writing a new story titled “Voices in the Dust,” a tale that characterised a group of children from different regions who overcame silence with art. Her new story included strong exposition, expressive dialogue, and a clear thesis: that art can heal what fear breaks.

Lomi reflected on how language could change minds. Words could persuade gently or impose unfairly, depending on how they were used. And in her stories, she chose to persuade — to offer kindness, strength, and truth.

By the following year, Selota School had become known as the “Valley of Voices,” a place where young writers were born. One of Lomi’s favourite moments came when a younger girl handed her a poem titled “My Grandmother’s Hands,” filled with repetition and loving imagery.
“You’re becoming quite persuasive,” Bekele teased again.
Lomi laughed. “Maybe. But now I know — our voices matter.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension questions
Answer these questions in full sentences:
Where does the story take place? Describe the setting.
What does Lomi keep in her red notebook?
What does Bekele mean when he says, "A voice kept silent is like a drum never played"?
What is the name of the school journal? What was Lomi's story called?
How did other students react when they read Lomi's story?
What happened at the youth arts event?
How did Lomi's courage inspire other students?
What does the banner at the ceremony say? What do you think it means?
At the end of the story, Lomi says, "Our voices matter." What do you think she means by this?
Think and discuss
Lomi was afraid to share her writing. Have you ever been afraid to share something you created? What happened?
The story says "creativity should be encouraged, not hidden." Do you agree? Why or why not?
How is Lomi's journey similar to someone who starts a sustainability project at school? Both require courage to lead and inspire others.
Bekele says, "You might fail, but your story might be remembered, too." What does this teach us about taking risks?
If you could write a story or poem for a school journal, what would it be about?
Literary devices in the story
The story is rich in literary devices. Find examples of each:
Simile: Find at least two comparisons using "like" or "as" in the story.
Metaphor: Find an example where something is described as something else without using "like" or "as".
Personification: Find a sentence where a non-human thing is given human qualities.
Imagery: Find a sentence that creates a strong picture in your mind.
Alliteration: Can you find any repeated sounds at the beginning of words close together?
Find the vocabulary words
Read the story again. Find each of these Unit 3 vocabulary words and write the sentence they appear in:
narrate
paraphrase
signify
personification
simile
metaphor
assertion
imagery
inference
alliteration
rhyme
rhythm
caption
figurative
repetition
connotation
emphasize
dialogue
infer
chronological
punctuation
perceive
characterize
exposition
thesis
persuade
impose
persuasive
Now You Try: Writing
Write a short paragraph (4-5 sentences) about a time you found courage to do something difficult. Use at least one simile and one metaphor.
Imagine you are starting a school journal like Echoes of the Highlands. Write a short caption for the front cover and the title of three stories or poems you would include.
Write a short poem (6-8 lines) about your own community or village. Try to use imagery, personification, and rhythm.
Lomi's thesis in her new story is "art can heal what fear breaks." Write your own thesis statement about the power of writing, creativity, or self-expression.
tables:
📖  About this story
Genre: Realistic fiction with literary devices.
Theme: Literary expression and the power of voice -- finding courage to share your creativity.
Setting: A rural highland village in Ethiopia called Selota.
Moral: When you use your voice with courage and purpose, others may find theirs too.
As you read, look for the vocabulary words from your word list. Many appear in bold. Notice how Lomi uses figurative language, imagery, similes, and metaphors in her writing.

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 3: Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 3. This unit focuses on clothing, fashion, sustainability, and the literary vocabulary you need to analyse stories and improve your own writing. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and do the practice activity. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Sound and Structure in Writing
Practise these words
Find an example of alliteration in a book, poem, or song. Write it down and underline the repeated sounds.
Write two pairs of words that rhyme (for example: light / night, dress / less).
Clap out the rhythm of your favourite poem or song. How many beats does each line have?
Why is repetition useful for learning new vocabulary? Explain in one sentence.
Group 2: Figurative Language
Practise these words
Write your own simile about the weather: "The rain was like ______."
Turn your simile into a metaphor by removing "like" or "as".
Write a sentence using personification: give a non-human thing (wind, river, sun) a human action.
Read a paragraph from your Story file. Find one example of imagery and explain what picture it creates in your mind.
The word "snake" can have a negative connotation (dishonesty). What connotation does the word "dove" have?
Group 3: Telling and Analysing Stories
Practise these words
Narrate a short event from your day in three sentences. Tell it in chronological order.
Read a sentence from the story file and paraphrase it in your own words.
Write a short dialogue (4-6 lines) between two friends about what they are wearing today.
Look at a photograph and write a caption for it.
How would you characterize Lomi from the story? Use three adjectives.
Group 4: Argument and Persuasion
Practise these words
Write an assertion about sustainability: "I believe that ______."
What is the thesis of your eco-project report? Write it in one sentence.
How would you persuade your family to buy fewer new clothes? Write two reasons.
Is it fair to impose rules about recycling on everyone? Write your opinion in two sentences.
Write a persuasive sentence about why schools should teach students about fast fashion.
Group 5: Understanding and Interpreting
Practise these words
Read the first paragraph of the story. What can you infer about Lomi's personality?
Make an inference: If someone is carrying an umbrella, what might you conclude about the weather?
Write a prediction about what might happen in the next unit of this course.
How do you perceive your school? Describe it using three senses (sight, sound, smell).
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it correctly in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!

tables: The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
"Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers" is a famous example of alliteration.
A word that has the same ending sound as another word.
In the poem, "cat" and "hat" are examples of words that rhyme.
A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement.
The drummer kept a steady rhythm that made everyone want to dance.
Doing or saying something again and again.
In class, we practise our vocabulary through repetition to remember the words better.
The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks.
Using punctuation correctly helps make your writing clear and easy to understand.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you help me find alliteration in a poem?"
"Can you give me more words that rhyme with 'fashion'?"
"Can you explain the difference between rhythm and rhyme?"
Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
When she said the classroom was a zoo, she was using a figurative expression to show it was very noisy.
A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as".
"Her smile was as bright as the sun" is a simile.
A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity.
In the sentence "Time is a thief," the word "thief" is a metaphor.
A way of describing something non-human by giving it human qualities.
"The wind whispered secrets to the trees" is an example of personification.
Words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
The poet used beautiful imagery to help us imagine the sunset over the valley.
The feelings or ideas that a word makes you think of, beyond its basic meaning.
The word "home" has a warm connotation because it makes people think of comfort and family.
The red light signifies that you must stop.

To tell a story or describe events in order.
In class, we had to narrate a story about our summer holiday.
To say something in a different way, using different words.
Can you paraphrase the story so that it is easier to understand?
A conversation between two or more people.
In the play, the dialogue between the characters was very interesting.
A clear explanation or description, often used to introduce a story's characters and setting.
In the book, the exposition helps us understand the main characters and the setting.
Arranged in the order that things happened, from earliest to latest.
We made a chronological timeline of events in history class.
A short description or title that explains a picture or video.
The caption under the photo tells us what is happening in the picture.
To describe the special qualities or features of someone or something.
We can characterize the main character in the story as brave and kind.
The sound that you make when you speak or sing; also, a writer's unique style.
Lomi found her voice through writing poems and stories.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you help me paraphrase this sentence from the story?"
"Can you check the dialogue I wrote?"
"What does 'finding your voice' mean in writing?"
A strong statement that something is true.
Her assertion that she finished the project on time surprised everyone.
A statement or main idea that someone wants to prove in their writing.
In her essay, Maria wrote a thesis about why recycling is important for the environment.
To show that something is very important or to make it stand out.
The teacher emphasized the need to study for the test.
To convince someone to do or believe something.
I tried to persuade my friend to join the Eco Club with me.
Able to convince someone to do or believe something.
The speaker gave a persuasive argument that made everyone want to join the recycling project.
To force someone to accept something or to make rules that others must follow.
The school decided to impose a new rule about recycling.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you help me write a thesis statement about fast fashion?"
"Can you help me practise using persuasive language in my writing?"
"What is the difference between persuade and impose?"
To understand something that is not directly said by using clues or information.
From her smile, I can infer that she is happy.
A conclusion or opinion formed based on evidence, rather than from what is explicitly stated.
From the clues in the story, we can make an inference about what will happen next.
A guess about what will happen in the future.
My prediction is that it will rain tomorrow.
To notice or understand something using your senses or mind.
I can perceive the smell of fresh cookies baking in the oven.
