paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Prepositions of Time and Place and more

This file has five grammar lessons for Unit 4. Work through one lesson at a time, try the practice exercises, then check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Prepositions of Time and Place
What it means and why it matters

Prepositions are small but powerful words that show relationships between other words in a sentence. The three most important prepositions of time and place are in, on, and at. Using them correctly makes your English sound natural. Getting them wrong is one of the most common mistakes learners make, so this lesson is worth studying carefully.
Prepositions of Time
Each preposition is used for different kinds of time expressions:
Important exception: We say at night (not "in night"), but in the morning, in the afternoon, and in the evening.
Prepositions of Place
The same three prepositions also describe location:
Special phrases to memorise
Some preposition phrases do not follow the main rules. You need to memorise them:
at the weekend (British English) / on the weekend (American English) — both are correct.
in a hurry — "She left in a hurry."
in trouble — "He is in trouble."
at first / at last — "At first, I was nervous. At last, I understood."
on time — "The bus arrived on time."
in time — "We arrived just in time for the lesson."
A common mistake
Mistake: "I will see you in Friday." ✗
Fix: "I will see you on Friday." ✓ (Use on for specific days.)
Mistake: "I was born at 2012." ✗
Fix: "I was born in 2012." ✓ (Use in for years.)
Memory tip
Think of these three pictures:
in = you are inside something (a box, a month, a city).
on = you are on top of something (a surface, a day, a date).
at = you are at a point (a dot on a map, a moment in time).
Practice 1
Fill in the blank with in, on, or at:
The market opens ______ 6 a.m. every day.
I was born ______ Mogadishu.
We have a test ______ Thursday.
She arrived ______ the evening.
The picture is hanging ______ the wall.
Meet me ______ the bus stop after school.
My family moved here ______ 2019.
The cat is sleeping ______ the sofa.
He always prays ______ sunset.
The festival is ______ 12th August.
Lesson 2: Past Simple Passive
What it means and why it matters

The past simple passive is used when we want to talk about something that happened in the past, and we want to focus on what happened rather than who did it. This is very common when talking about cities, buildings, and transport, because we often describe things that were built, designed, or created without needing to name the person who did it.
How to form it
Subject + was (singular) / were (plural) + past participle.
"The road was built in 2020."
"The traffic lights were installed last month."
"The school was designed by a famous architect."
"New trees were planted along the main street."
When to use the past simple passive
When the doer is unknown: "The window was broken last night." (We do not know who broke it.)
When the doer is unimportant: "The bridge was completed in March." (We care about the bridge, not who built it.)
When the doer is obvious: "The suspect was arrested." (Obviously by the police.)
Adding the doer with by
If you do want to mention who did the action, add by + the doer at the end:
"The mural was painted by local artists."
"The road was damaged by heavy rain."
A common mistake
Mistake: "The bridge was build last year." ✗ (Used the base form instead of the past participle.)
Fix: "The bridge was built last year." ✓
Mistake: "The roads was repaired." ✗ (Used "was" with a plural subject.)
Fix: "The roads were repaired." ✓
Memory tip

Think of the passive as a spotlight. Active voice shines the light on the doer. Passive voice moves the spotlight onto the thing that received the action. In the past simple passive, the spotlight is on what happened.
Practice 2
Rewrite these active sentences in the past simple passive:
Workers built the new school in 2021.
The government opened a new hospital last year.
Someone stole my bicycle yesterday.
The community planted 200 trees along the road.
Engineers designed the bridge to carry heavy trucks.
A local artist painted the mural on the wall.
The storm damaged several buildings in the city.
The mayor opened the new bus station on Monday.
Lesson 3: Present Simple Passive
What it means and why it matters

The present simple passive is used to describe things that happen regularly, or general truths, when the focus is on the action or the thing receiving it. This is very common in descriptions of how cities work, how services are provided, and how things are done.
How to form it
Subject + is (singular) / are (plural) + past participle.
"The streets are cleaned every morning."
"Fresh water is delivered to the neighbourhood by truck."
"Tickets are sold at the station entrance."
"English is spoken in many countries around the world."
Negative and question forms
Negative: "The roads are not (aren't) repaired often enough."
Question: "Are the buses checked before they leave the station?"
Negative: "This street is not (isn't) used by many cars."
Question: "Is the market opened every day?"
A common mistake
Mistake: "The homework is give by the teacher." ✗ (Used the base form, not the past participle.)
Fix: "The homework is given by the teacher." ✓
Mistake: "The books is read by the students." ✗ (Used "is" with a plural subject.)
Fix: "The books are read by the students." ✓
Memory tip

The present simple passive describes how things are done as a routine or fact. Think: "How is this done?" or "What happens to this?" — if you can ask that question, you probably need the present simple passive.
Practice 3
Rewrite these active sentences in the present simple passive:
The council cleans the streets every week.
People speak Somali in many East African countries.
The school teaches road safety to young children.
Farmers grow rice in the fields near the river.
The librarian organises the books every Friday.
Workers check the buses before each journey.
Lesson 4: Modal Verbs + Passive Voice
What it means and why it matters

Modal verbs like can, should, must, may, and might express ideas like possibility, obligation, advice, and permission. When we combine them with the passive voice, we can talk about what can be done, should be done, or must be done — without naming who does it. This is very common in discussions about improving cities and solving problems.
How to form it
Subject + modal verb + be + past participle.
Possibility: "Transport problems can be solved with better planning."
Obligation: "Old buses must be replaced with cleaner vehicles."
Advice: "More trees should be planted in city centres."
Permission: "The report may be submitted next week."
Possibility: "Mistakes might be made, but we can learn from them."
Negative forms
"The old bridge cannot (can't) be used any more."
"Plastic bags should not (shouldn't) be thrown in the river."
"This document must not (mustn't) be shared without permission."
A common mistake
Mistake: "The letters must sent by Friday." ✗ (Forgot "be" between the modal and the past participle.)
Fix: "The letters must be sent by Friday." ✓
Mistake: "The road should built next year." ✗
Fix: "The road should be built next year." ✓
Memory tip

The formula is always the same: modal + be + past participle. Never forget the be in the middle! Think of it as a sandwich: the modal is the bread, "be" is the filling, and the past participle is the other slice of bread.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with the correct modal passive form. Use the modal verb in brackets:
The new road ______ (should / build) before the rainy season.
Old vehicles ______ (must / replace) with cleaner ones.
Bus timetables ______ (can / share) on mobile phones.
The park ______ (should / clean) every week.
This bridge ______ (must / not / use) by heavy trucks.
The project ______ (may / complete) by the end of the month.
More bicycle lanes ______ (should / create) in the city centre.
The rules ______ (must / follow) by everyone.
Lesson 5: Comparative Adjectives and Sentence Adverbs
What it means and why it matters
This lesson covers two grammar topics that help you express opinions and make comparisons — both essential skills when writing or talking about cities, transport, and the environment.
Part A: Comparative Adjectives
Comparative adjectives allow us to compare two things, people, or places. They show how one is different from the other.
Rules for forming comparatives:
One-syllable adjectives: add -er to the end. fast → faster, old → older, tall → taller.
One-syllable adjectives ending in consonant-vowel-consonant: double the last letter and add -er. big → bigger, hot → hotter.
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -i and add -er. busy → busier, noisy → noisier, happy → happier.
Two or more syllable adjectives: use more before the adjective. crowded → more crowded, beautiful → more beautiful, dangerous → more dangerous.
Irregular comparatives: good → better, bad → worse, far → farther / further.
Always use than after the comparative when naming the two things being compared:
"Nairobi is bigger than Hargeisa."
"Buses are cheaper than taxis."
"The new road is smoother than the old one."
"Cycling is more environmentally friendly than driving."
Comparing emotional states
Comparative adjectives are also used for feelings and emotions, which connects to the story "A Heart in the Noise":
"She feels more vulnerable than before."
"He was less elated after hearing the news."
"Neema became more confident as the term went on."
"The second speech was better than the first."
Part B: Sentence Adverbs
Sentence adverbs express your opinion or feeling about the entire sentence. They usually come at the beginning or end of a sentence, separated by a comma:
Fortunately — to show good news or relief. "Fortunately, the bus arrived just in time."
Unfortunately — to show bad news or regret. "Unfortunately, the road was closed."
Certainly — to express agreement or confidence. "The city has certainly improved."
Common mistakes with sentence adverbs
Mistake: "Unfortunately we missed the bus." ✗ (Missing comma after the adverb.)
Fix: "Unfortunately, we missed the bus." ✓
Mistake: "Certainly, it was a terrible day." ✗ ("Certainly" expresses confidence or agreement, not sadness. Use "Unfortunately" instead.)
Fix: "Unfortunately, it was a terrible day." ✓
Memory tip
Remember F-C-U: Fortunately (good news), Certainly (agreement), Unfortunately (bad news). Think: "Feeling Confident and Upset" — feelings that match each adverb.
Practice 5
Part A: Write the correct comparative form:
This city is ______ (noisy) than my village.
The train is ______ (fast) than the bus.
Her essay was ______ (good) than mine.
The old market is ______ (crowded) than the new one.
He felt ______ (vulnerable) after the incident.
Walking is ______ (slow) than cycling.
Part B: Add the correct sentence adverb (fortunately, unfortunately, or certainly):
______, the bridge collapsed during the storm and no one could cross.
______, no one was hurt in the accident.
The new bus service has ______ made travel easier for everyone.
______, I left my bag on the bus and it was gone when I returned.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Prepositions of Time and Place)
1. at   2. in   3. on   4. in   5. on   6. at   7. in   8. on   9. at   10. on
Practice 2 (Past Simple Passive)

1. The new school was built in 2021.   2. A new hospital was opened last year.   3. My bicycle was stolen yesterday.   4. 200 trees were planted along the road.   5. The bridge was designed to carry heavy trucks.   6. The mural was painted on the wall.   7. Several buildings were damaged by the storm.   8. The new bus station was opened on Monday.
Practice 3 (Present Simple Passive)

1. The streets are cleaned every week.   2. Somali is spoken in many East African countries.   3. Road safety is taught to young children.   4. Rice is grown in the fields near the river.   5. The books are organised every Friday.   6. The buses are checked before each journey.
Practice 4 (Modal Verbs + Passive)
1. should be built   2. must be replaced   3. can be shared   4. should be cleaned   5. must not be used   6. may be completed   7. should be created   8. must be followed
Practice 5 (Comparatives and Sentence Adverbs)
Part A: 1. noisier   2. faster   3. better   4. more crowded   5. more vulnerable   6. slower
Part B: 1. Unfortunately   2. Fortunately   3. certainly   4. Unfortunately
tables: months, years, centuries, seasons, parts of the day
in January, in 2024, in the morning, in summer
days of the week, dates, special days
on Monday, on 5th April, on Eid day
at 7 a.m., at noon, at sunset, at night
in Somalia, in the classroom, in the box
on the table, on the wall, on Lido Beach
at the bus stop, at the market, at school

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers for all five lessons?
Can you explain the difference between past simple passive and present simple passive?
Can you give me five more sentences to practise modal verbs + passive?
Can you help me write a paragraph about my city using all four grammar topics?

paragraphs: Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions! In this unit you will explore how cities grow, how people move through them, and how communities around the world are finding creative solutions to make urban life better for everyone. You will read about transport systems, green innovations, and the challenges that cities face in East Africa and beyond.

Cities are exciting, noisy, and full of life. But they can also be crowded, polluted, and difficult to navigate. In this unit, you will learn English through the lens of urban living — reading about how young people can shape the cities they live in, writing your own travel guides, and discussing what makes a city a good place to live.

This unit connects reading, grammar, vocabulary, writing and speaking into one rich topic. By the end, you will be able to talk confidently about transport, city planning, and environmental solutions — all while using more advanced grammar like prepositions of time and place, passive voice, comparative adjectives, and sentence adverbs.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a passage about city transport and discuss its ideas in English.
Use prepositions of time and place (in, on, at) correctly and confidently.
Form the passive voice in the past simple, present simple, and with modal verbs.
Use comparative adjectives to compare places, transport systems, and city features.
Use sentence adverbs (fortunately, unfortunately, certainly) to express your opinion.
Write a detailed city travel guide using precise vocabulary and correct grammar.
Present your ideas clearly in a spoken presentation about urban living.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the lessons in the Grammar file (Prepositions, Passive Voice, Comparatives, Sentence Adverbs).
Read the Story file, "A Heart in the Noise", and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary

Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
Transport and Movement
melancholy — a feeling of sadness or being unhappy for a long time.
exuberant — full of energy, excitement, and happiness.
fluctuate — to change or vary in amount or level.
gingerly — in a careful or cautious way.
crude — not finished or made in a simple and rough way.
haphazard — not planned or organized; done in a random way.
Emotions and Behaviour
complacent — feeling satisfied with yourself and not wanting to improve.
ephemeral — lasting for a very short time.
vulnerable — able to be easily hurt or harmed, physically or emotionally.
elated — very happy and excited about something good that has happened.
obnoxious — very unpleasant or rude; annoying.
oblivious — not aware of something happening around you.
Character and Judgement
discipline — the ability to control yourself and do what you should, even if you do not want to.
tempt — to make someone want to do something, especially something not good for them.
ludicrous — very silly or unreasonable; ridiculous.
sophisticated — having a lot of knowledge or experience; complex and advanced.
ostentatious — showing off in a way meant to attract attention.
candor — the quality of being open and honest in what you say.
Reading: Moving Through Mogadishu — A City in Motion
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions.

Every morning at 6:30, the streets of Mogadishu begin to hum with life. Minibuses crowd the main roads, motorcycles weave between cars, and pedestrians walk quickly along dusty pavements. For the people of this growing East African capital, getting from one place to another is both a daily challenge and a daily adventure.

Mogadishu is one of the fastest-growing cities in East Africa. Its population has fluctuated dramatically over the past two decades, rising as people move in from rural areas searching for work, education, and safety. The city’s roads, many of which were damaged in years of conflict, are now being rebuilt. New tarmac is laid every month, and traffic lights have been installed at several busy junctions. But the city is growing faster than its infrastructure can keep up.

The most common form of public transport is the minibus, locally known as a bajaaj or shared taxi. These vehicles are often painted in bright colours and decorated with stickers and patterns. They follow no fixed timetable — they leave when they are full. For passengers, this means that journey times can fluctuate from twenty minutes to over an hour, depending on the traffic and the number of stops.

At the Bakaaraha market, one of the largest open-air markets in East Africa, the streets become even more crowded. Traders push carts loaded with vegetables, electronics, and fabrics. Donkey carts carry heavy goods through narrow lanes. The arrangement of stalls looks haphazard to a visitor, but regular shoppers know exactly where to find what they need.

Young people in Mogadishu are thinking about how to improve urban transport. A group of university students recently proposed a simple bus-route map that could be shared on mobile phones. Their idea is that if passengers know which route a bus follows and when it is likely to arrive, travel will become faster and safer. "The city should be made easier to navigate," said Farah, one of the students. "Better information can be given to everyone."

Other cities in East Africa are already experimenting with solutions. In Nairobi, a digital mapping project has been created by young engineers. In Dar es Salaam, a new rapid-bus system was built to reduce traffic. These projects show that transport problems can be solved when communities work together and young people are encouraged to share their ideas.

Unfortunately, many cities still struggle with pollution from old vehicles, dangerous roads for pedestrians, and overcrowded buses. But there is also reason for hope. Certainly, the energy and creativity of young East Africans are making a difference. As Farah puts it: "A city should not just be built for cars. It should be designed for people."

Mogadishu is louder, busier, and more exuberant than it has been in years. The city is not perfect, but it is alive — and the young people who move through its streets every day are determined to make it better.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What happens on the streets of Mogadishu every morning at 6:30?
Why has Mogadishu’s population grown so quickly? Give at least two reasons from the text.
What is a bajaaj, and how does it work as public transport?
Why do journey times on minibuses fluctuate? Give two reasons.
Describe the Bakaaraha market. What makes it look haphazard to visitors?
What solution did the university students propose? How would it help passengers?
Name two transport projects from other East African cities mentioned in the passage.
Find two examples of the passive voice in the passage. Write the full sentence for each.
What does Farah mean when he says, "A city should not just be built for cars. It should be designed for people"?
Do you think Mogadishu’s transport problems can be solved? Use evidence from the passage to explain your answer.
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
What is the biggest transport challenge in your city, town, or neighbourhood? How could it be improved?
The passage says young people are proposing solutions. Why is it important for young people to be involved in city planning?
Compare Mogadishu to another city you know. What is similar and what is different about how people travel?
If you could design the perfect city, what transport system would you include? Think about buses, bicycles, walking paths, and trains.
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
fluctuated
haphazard
exuberant
installed
navigate
infrastructure
Grammar Focus: Prepositions of Time and Place
This section introduces the first key grammar topic for Unit 4. You will study it in more detail in your Grammar companion file, but here is a summary to get you started.
What are prepositions of time and place?

Prepositions are small words that show relationships between other words in a sentence. The three most important prepositions of time and place are in, on, and at. Getting these right will make your English sound much more natural and precise.
Prepositions of Time
in — for longer periods: months, years, centuries, seasons, parts of the day. Example: "I was born in 2012." / "We study English in the morning."
on — for specific days and dates. Example: "We have class on Monday." / "Her birthday is on 5th April."
at — for exact times and specific moments. Example: "I wake up at 7 a.m." / "We eat iftar at sunset."
Prepositions of Place
in — inside a larger space. Example: "The students are in the classroom." / "She lives in Mogadishu."
on — touching a surface. Example: "The book is on the table." / "The sign is on the wall."
at — a specific point or location. Example: "We are waiting at the bus stop." / "I met him at the market."
Prepositions in the reading passage
Go back to the reading passage and find at least six prepositions of time or place (in, on, at). For each one, write the full sentence and say whether the preposition shows time or place.
Quick Practice
Choose the correct preposition (in, on, or at) to complete each sentence:
The bus leaves ______ 8:15 every morning.
She was born ______ Dar es Salaam.
We will travel ______ Friday.
The new road was built ______ 2023.
Meet me ______ the train station.
The stickers are ______ the side of the minibus.
I usually read ______ the evening before bed.
The market is busiest ______ Saturday mornings.
Answers: 1. at   2. in   3. on   4. in   5. at   6. on   7. in   8. on
Grammar Focus: Passive Voice

The passive voice is used when we want to focus on the action or the thing that receives the action, rather than who does it. It is very common in writing about cities, transport, and buildings, because we often talk about things being built, designed, or created.
Past Simple Passive
Form: was / were + past participle
"The road was built in 2019."
"Traffic lights were installed at the junction."
"The bus station was designed by a local architect."
Present Simple Passive
Form: is / are + past participle
"The streets are cleaned every morning."
"Fresh water is delivered to the neighbourhood by truck."
"Tickets are sold at the station entrance."
Modal Verbs + Passive
Form: modal + be + past participle
Modal verbs like can, should, must, and may combine with the passive to talk about possibility, obligation, and advice:
Possibility: "Transport problems can be solved with better planning."
Obligation: "Old buses must be replaced with cleaner vehicles."
Advice: "More cycle lanes should be built in city centres."
Permission: "The report may be submitted next week."
Passive voice in the reading passage
Find at least four examples of the passive voice in the reading passage. For each one, write:
The full sentence.
Whether it is past simple passive, present simple passive, or modal + passive.
What the focus of the sentence is (what thing is receiving the action).
Quick Practice
Rewrite these sentences in the passive voice:
Workers built the new bridge last year.
The council cleans the streets every week.
Engineers should design safer roads.
People damaged the old bus station during the storm.
The government must improve public transport.
Artists painted a mural on the community hall.

Answers: 1. The new bridge was built last year.   2. The streets are cleaned every week.   3. Safer roads should be designed.   4. The old bus station was damaged during the storm.   5. Public transport must be improved.   6. A mural was painted on the community hall.
Grammar Focus: Comparatives and Sentence Adverbs
Comparative Adjectives
Comparative adjectives let us compare two things. They are essential when writing about cities, because we often compare transport systems, places, and living conditions.
Rules for forming comparatives:
One-syllable adjectives: add -er. Example: fast → faster, tall → taller, old → older.
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -i and add -er. Example: busy → busier, noisy → noisier.
Two or more syllables: use more before the adjective. Example: expensive → more expensive, crowded → more crowded.
Irregular comparatives: good → better, bad → worse, far → farther / further.
Always use than after the comparative when you name the two things being compared:
"Nairobi is bigger than Mogadishu."
"Buses are cheaper than taxis."
"The new road is smoother than the old one."
"Cycling is more environmentally friendly than driving."
Sentence Adverbs
Sentence adverbs express your opinion or feeling about the whole sentence. They usually come at the beginning of a sentence, followed by a comma:
Unfortunately — to show bad news or regret. "Unfortunately, the bus was delayed by an hour."
Fortunately — to show good news or relief. "Fortunately, a new bus route was opened last month."
Certainly — to express agreement or confidence. "The city has certainly improved since the new roads were built."
Quick Practice: Comparatives
Complete each sentence with the correct comparative form:
Dar es Salaam is ______ (hot) than Nairobi.
A bicycle is ______ (cheap) than a car.
This route is ______ (dangerous) than the main road.
The new bus is ______ (comfortable) than the old one.
Walking is ______ (slow) than taking the bus.
Mogadishu is ______ (busy) than my village.
Answers: 1. hotter   2. cheaper   3. more dangerous   4. more comfortable   5. slower   6. busier
Quick Practice: Sentence Adverbs
Add the correct sentence adverb (unfortunately, fortunately, or certainly) to each sentence:
______, we missed the last bus and had to walk home.
______, a kind driver stopped and offered us a lift.
The new bicycle lanes have ______ made the city safer.
______, the bridge was closed for repairs during rush hour.
Answers: 1. Unfortunately   2. Fortunately   3. certainly   4. Unfortunately
Grammar Practice

These exercises bring together all four grammar topics: prepositions, passive voice, comparatives, and sentence adverbs. Complete them in your notebook, then check your answers at the bottom or ask your AI tutor.
Exercise A: Prepositions in Context
Complete each sentence with in, on, or at:
The new market was opened ______ 15th March 2024.
I will meet you ______ the bus station after school.
Traffic is always worse ______ the morning.
The announcement was written ______ a large sign.
They arrived ______ Mogadishu late ______ night.
The mural was painted ______ the side of the building.
We have English class ______ Tuesdays and Thursdays.
The plane landed ______ exactly 3:45 p.m.
Answers: 1. on   2. at   3. in   4. on   5. in, at   6. on   7. on   8. at
Exercise B: Active to Passive
Rewrite each sentence in the passive voice:
The government built a new hospital in the city centre.
Workers repair the roads every summer.
The community should plant more trees along the main road.
Engineers designed the bridge to carry heavy traffic.
Local artists can paint murals on empty walls.
The school teaches road safety to young children.

Answers: 1. A new hospital was built in the city centre.   2. The roads are repaired every summer.   3. More trees should be planted along the main road.   4. The bridge was designed to carry heavy traffic.   5. Murals can be painted on empty walls.   6. Road safety is taught to young children.
Exercise C: Comparative Sentences
Write a comparative sentence using the information given:
Buses / taxis (cheap)
Nairobi / my village (crowded)
The new road / the old road (smooth)
Cycling / driving (environmentally friendly)
Morning traffic / evening traffic (bad)
This neighbourhood / that neighbourhood (quiet)

Sample answers: 1. Buses are cheaper than taxis.   2. Nairobi is more crowded than my village.   3. The new road is smoother than the old road.   4. Cycling is more environmentally friendly than driving.   5. Morning traffic is worse than evening traffic.   6. This neighbourhood is quieter than that neighbourhood.
Exercise D: Mixed Grammar — Write Your Own
Write six sentences about transport or city life. Each sentence must include:
Two sentences with a preposition of time or place used correctly.
Two sentences using the passive voice (any form: past, present, or modal).
Two sentences using a comparative adjective or a sentence adverb.
Writing Workshop: My City Travel Guide

In this section, you will plan and write a short travel guide for a city you know or would like to visit. This could be a real city like Mogadishu, Nairobi, Hargeisa, or Dar es Salaam, or it could be an imaginary city. The goal is to practise your vocabulary, grammar, and descriptive writing skills.
Step 1: Choose Your City
Pick one of these cities, or choose your own:
Mogadishu, Somalia
Nairobi, Kenya
Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Hargeisa, Somaliland
Kampala, Uganda
An imaginary city that you design yourself.
Step 2: Plan Your Guide
Before you write, answer these planning questions in your notebook:
What is the city called, and where is it located?
What is the city famous for? What are its main attractions?
How do people travel around the city? Describe at least two forms of transport.
What is the food like? Name one or two popular dishes.
What is the best time to visit? Use prepositions of time (e.g., "Visit in January" or "The market is open on Saturdays").
What problems does the city face? Use a sentence adverb (e.g., "Unfortunately, the roads can be very crowded").
How does this city compare to another city you know? Use a comparative adjective.
Step 3: Write Your Travel Guide
Write four to five paragraphs. Follow this structure:
Paragraph 1 — Introduction: Name the city, say where it is, and give a general description. Use at least one passive voice sentence (e.g., "The city was founded in...").
Paragraph 2 — Getting Around: Describe the transport options. Use prepositions of place and time. Compare different types of transport using comparative adjectives.
Paragraph 3 — What to See and Do: Describe the main attractions and activities. Use vivid adjectives and vocabulary from this unit.
Paragraph 4 — Challenges and Hopes: Mention any problems the city faces (use sentence adverbs). Then describe how the city is improving (use the passive voice).
Paragraph 5 — Conclusion: End with a recommendation. Would you encourage someone to visit? Why?
Step 4: Edit and Improve
Use this checklist to review your writing:
Have I used at least two passive voice sentences?   ☐
Have I used at least two comparative adjectives?   ☐
Have I used at least one sentence adverb correctly?   ☐
Have I used prepositions of time and place correctly?   ☐
Have I used at least five vocabulary words from this unit?   ☐
Does every sentence start with a capital letter and end with punctuation?   ☐
Have I read my work out loud to check that it sounds natural?   ☐
Model Paragraph
Here is an example of a well-written first paragraph to guide you:

“Mogadishu is the capital city of Somalia, located on the coast of the Indian Ocean. The city was founded centuries ago and has a rich history of trade and culture. Today, it is a bustling, exuberant place where new buildings are being constructed alongside ancient mosques and markets. Certainly, Mogadishu is busier and more colourful than it has been in years. The streets are filled with the sounds of car horns, market traders, and children playing.”
Listening & Speaking
Listening: An Interview About City Transport
Ask your AI tutor to read this interview aloud while you listen. Then read it yourself.
Interviewer: Tell us about transport in your city.
Amina: I live in Mogadishu. The main way to get around is by minibus or bajaaj. The buses are always full, especially in the morning and at around 5 p.m. when people are going home from work.
Interviewer: What is the biggest problem with transport?

Amina: Unfortunately, the roads are sometimes in bad condition. There are potholes and dust, and in the rainy season, some roads flood. Also, there are no maps or timetables, so you never know exactly when the bus will come.
Interviewer: How could transport be improved?

Amina: I think better roads should be built, and a bus timetable should be created. Cycling lanes would help too. Cycling is cheaper than taking a bus and better for the environment. Certainly, young people have many ideas about how to make the city better.
Interviewer: What advice do you have for visitors?

Amina: Be patient! The traffic can be slow, but the city is more exciting than you might expect. Walk through the markets, try the food, and talk to people. Mogadishu is busier and more vibrant than ever before.
After listening, answer these questions:
What are the two main forms of transport Amina mentions?
What time of day is transport busiest?
Name two problems Amina describes with the roads.
What three improvements does Amina suggest?
Find two comparative adjectives in the interview and write the sentences.
Find one example of the passive voice and one sentence adverb.
Speaking 1: Present Your City Travel Guide
Prepare a short spoken presentation (about two minutes) about the city you described in the Writing Workshop. Follow this structure:
Introduction: Name the city and say where it is.
Transport: Describe how people get around. Use a comparative adjective.
Highlights: Describe the best things to see or do.
Recommendation: Would you recommend this city to a visitor? Why?
Record yourself and listen back. Check that you spoke clearly and used vocabulary from this unit.
Speaking 2: Debate — Cars or Public Transport?
In pairs or with your AI tutor, debate this question: Should cities focus on building more roads for cars, or should they invest in better public transport?
One person argues for cars and the other argues for public transport. Use evidence from the reading passage and your own knowledge.
For cars: "Cars are faster than buses because…" / "People need cars for…"
For public transport: "Buses are cheaper than cars because…" / "Public transport is better for the environment because…"
After the debate, write one paragraph explaining which side you agree with and why. Use at least one sentence adverb and one comparative adjective.
Speaking Game: City Comparison
Play this game with your AI tutor or a classmate:
Choose two cities (they can be real or imaginary).
Take turns making comparative sentences about the cities.
Example: "Nairobi is bigger than Hargeisa." / "Hargeisa is quieter than Nairobi."
Keep going until one person cannot think of another comparison. The other person wins!
Challenge: try to include a sentence adverb in at least one comparison.
Check Your Learning
You have now completed Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions. Use the checklist below to see what you have learned and what you might want to review.
Self-Assessment
Tick "Yes" or "Not yet". For every "Not yet", ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can read and understand a passage about city transport and urban solutions.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use prepositions of time and place (in, on, at) correctly.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can form the passive voice in past simple, present simple, and with modal verbs.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use comparative adjectives to compare two things.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use sentence adverbs (fortunately, unfortunately, certainly) to express my opinion.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a city travel guide using vocabulary and grammar from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can speak clearly about transport and city life in a short presentation.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I know at least fifteen vocabulary words from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Reflection
In your notebook, write a short paragraph (three to five sentences) answering this question:
What is the most important thing you learned in Unit 4, and how does it connect to your own life?
Think about:
How do you travel to school or to the shops? Could it be better?
What would you change about the area where you live?
How can young people help improve their cities or towns?
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?"
Practise speaking: "Can you listen to my presentation and give me feedback?"
Get feedback: "Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?"
Ask for more practice: "Can you give me more questions about the passive voice?"
Prepare for your teacher: "What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Great work — you are building skills that will help you describe the world around you, compare different places, and express your ideas with confidence. Keep going!

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "A Heart in the Noise"
A Heart in the Noise

In the dusty lanes of Mwananyamala, on the edge of Dar es Salaam, the air was always full — of smoke from chapati stalls, music from boda bodas, and voices rising in greeting or complaint. Life moved quickly. Sometimes too quickly.

Thirteen-year-old Neema stood in front of the community kiosk where her mother sold phone credit and chewing gum. Her shoulders slumped as she stared at her crumpled math test. A red "37%" was circled at the top.
Her teacher's comment — "You must improve your discipline" — rang in her ears.
Around her, the world buzzed with the exuberant energy of boys playing football. It made her sadness feel heavier. Their laughter sounded like a cruel echo.
She stuffed the paper into her backpack.
Unfortunately, she had no words for how embarrassed she felt.
At home, her father, Baba Mussa, was cleaning his tools. He repaired shoes in a small open workshop. He looked up as she walked by.
"How was school, Neema?"
"Fine," she muttered.
He noticed her voice and frowned but said nothing.

The truth was: Neema had stopped studying weeks ago. She didn't know why. Maybe it was the noise at home. Maybe it was the temptation to scroll through her phone instead of revising. Maybe it was the feeling that nothing really mattered.
She had become complacent without even noticing.
That evening, she sat on the roof with her younger brother. They watched the sunset, which painted the buildings with ephemeral gold.
"Do you think I'm smart?" she asked him.
He blinked. "I don't know. But you're kind."
Kind. The word made her eyes sting. It felt like a small remnant of hope.
Later, at dinner, her parents talked about the rising cost of flour. Her mother looked tired. Her father looked older.
Neema stared at her plate. She felt vulnerable, like a balloon drifting away without string.
That night, she dreamed she was in a huge hall where people were shouting, dancing, singing — a crescendo of chaos — and she was the only one trying to read a book.
She woke up feeling melancholy, her heart heavy like soaked cloth.

Over the next week, her emotions fluctuated like the tides. She was happy one morning, then anxious by lunchtime. Sometimes she was elated when a teacher praised her, but the feeling never lasted. She envied friends who seemed stable, focused.
Then came Wednesday.
In English class, the teacher asked students to describe a recent emotional moment using a story. Neema didn't raise her hand.
But later that day, she began to write.
She described her dream. She described the hall and the noise. She added that the book in her hands had no words, only blank pages.
Her teacher, Ms. Salma — who had once worked as a journalist — read the story and clapped.
"This must be published in the school paper," she said. "It captures confusion with such candor."
Neema felt heat rush to her face. But this time, it wasn't shame.
That afternoon, as she walked home, she passed a group of older students shouting jokes. One boy was being obnoxious, calling names. Another girl tried to walk away, but he blocked her.
Neema hesitated.
She stepped forward. "Leave her alone."
The boy laughed. "You? Little Neema?"
But something in her voice had changed. It wasn't loud — but it was certain.
He blinked, shrugged, and left.
The girl thanked her softly.
That evening, Neema told her mother everything — about the test, the sadness, the story.
Her mother listened, then said, "It's easy to be oblivious to what we carry inside. But you're learning."
They sat quietly for a long time.
That weekend, the school hosted a debate event. Neema wasn't planning to go. But her teacher encouraged her.
"You don't have to speak," she said. "You can observe. Maybe write something after."
So Neema went. She sat at the back of the noisy room. Students argued about whether modern society had become more lax or more disciplined.
One boy, dressed in an ostentatious suit, made a bold speech. But his points were shallow. Another girl, though softly spoken, spoke with logic and heart.
Neema wrote down everything — every flaw, every strength. Her notes became a short article that was published in the newsletter the following Monday.
Certainly, that was the first time her writing had reached more than ten people.
One teacher called it "surprisingly sophisticated for someone so young."
Neema smiled for hours after that.
She started spending more time in the library. She read about emotions, dreams, and young leaders around the world. The shelves were dusty, the chairs askew, but it felt like a second home.
In the weeks that followed, she changed — not in a loud, ludicrous way, but slowly, like sunrise.
She still felt nervous at times. Still forgot homework. Still lost her pencil case now and then.
But she also started helping a younger boy in her class who struggled with reading.
"You're patient," he said.
Neema laughed. "Not always. But I'm trying."
Her friends noticed, too.
"You seem different," said Asha. "Like... more sure of yourself."
"I don't know," Neema replied. "Maybe I'm just more... careful."
She now moved through life gingerly, not out of fear but with awareness.

One afternoon, she stayed behind to help clean the class. The teacher had left early, and the chairs were stacked in a haphazard pile. She slowly arranged them, wiped the board, straightened the posters.
It was nothing major. But it felt good.
At the end of term, she wrote a reflection essay. In it, she described the rollercoaster of feelings she had lived through. She included a paragraph comparing her past self with her present.
"I used to think that emotion was weakness," she wrote. "But now I see that feeling deeply can be strength — if I learn from it."
Her essay was selected to be read aloud at the school assembly.
She stood on stage, her paper shaking slightly. But her voice was steady.
She read until the last sentence, looked up, and saw her mother in the crowd, smiling.
The applause that followed wasn't loud. But it was warm, real.
And Neema — who had once felt like a remnant of potential — now felt whole.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the story.
Where does the story take place? Describe the setting in your own words.
What happened to Neema at school that made her feel embarrassed?
Why had Neema stopped studying? Give at least two reasons from the text.
What does the word complacent mean, and how had Neema become complacent?
What did Neema dream about? What do you think the dream symbolised?
How did Neema's emotions fluctuate over the following week? Give examples from the text.
What did Ms. Salma think of Neema's story? What did she say about it?
Describe what happened when Neema stood up to the obnoxious boy. Why was this moment important?
What did Neema's mother mean when she said, "It's easy to be oblivious to what we carry inside"?
How did the debate event change Neema? What did she do afterwards?
How did Neema change by the end of the story? List at least three ways.
What does Neema mean when she writes, "feeling deeply can be strength — if I learn from it"?
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
Neema's brother said she was "kind" instead of "smart." Which quality do you think matters more, and why?
The teacher said Neema's story captured confusion with candor. Why is honesty in writing powerful?
Neema moved through life gingerly — carefully, with awareness. Is this a good way to live? Why or why not?
How does the noise of the city (Dar es Salaam) connect to Neema's inner feelings throughout the story?
If you could give Neema one piece of advice at the beginning of the story, what would it be?
The story is called "A Heart in the Noise." Why do you think the author chose this title?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 4 words and read out the sentence they appear in:
melancholy
exuberant
ludicrous
complacent
ephemeral
obnoxious
discipline
tempt
vulnerable
elated
haphazard
lax
oblivious
ostentatious
sophisticated
askew
fluctuate
gingerly
candor
crescendo
remnant
Grammar in the Story
The story uses several grammar features from this unit. Find examples of:
Prepositions of time: Find at least three sentences that use in, on, or at to show when something happened.
Prepositions of place: Find three sentences that use in, on, or at to show where something happened.
Passive voice: Find two sentences where the passive voice is used (e.g., "was published", "was selected").
Comparative adjectives: Find one sentence where two things are compared.
Sentence adverbs: Find the two sentence adverbs used in the story (unfortunately, certainly).
Now You Try: Writing
Writing Activities
Retell a scene: Choose your favourite part of the story and retell it in your own words. Write at least five sentences. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit.

Write a diary entry: Imagine you are Neema. Write a diary entry for the evening after her story was praised by Ms. Salma. How does she feel? What does she think about? Use at least two sentence adverbs and three vocabulary words.

Character comparison: Compare Neema at the beginning of the story with Neema at the end. Write a paragraph using at least three comparative adjectives. For example: "At the end, Neema is more confident than she was at the beginning."

City and emotion: The story is set in a busy city neighbourhood. Write a short description (one paragraph) of a noisy, busy place you know. Use at least four vocabulary words from this unit and two prepositions of place.
tables:

Theme: Emotional growth and human behaviour.
Setting: A busy neighbourhood in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania.
Moral: Emotions, though difficult to manage, can guide us to growth if we pause, reflect, and empathise.
Read it slowly. When you meet a vocabulary word from your list (shown in bold), say it out loud!

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 4: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 4. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then try the practice activities. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Emotions and Feelings
Practise these words

Write a sentence using exuberant to describe a happy celebration or event.
What is the difference between elated and exuberant? Write one sentence for each.
Group 2: Character and Behaviour
Practise these words

Explain what discipline means. Why is it important for learning?

Group 3: Description and Quality
Practise these words

Write a sentence using haphazard to describe a messy room or market stall.

Group 4: Action and Expression
Practise these words

Use gingerly to describe someone doing something very carefully.

End-of-Unit Vocabulary Challenge
When you have learned all the words, try this challenge:
Choose any five words from your Word Wall. Write one sentence for each that connects to the theme of Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions.
Write a short paragraph (four to six sentences) using at least six vocabulary words from this unit. The paragraph should be about a busy city, a journey, or a moment of strong emotion.
Ask your AI tutor to quiz you on all 30 words. Try to get at least 25 correct!
tables: A feeling of sadness or being unhappy for a long time.
After her friend moved away, she felt a deep sense of melancholy.
Full of energy, excitement, and happiness.

Very happy and excited about something good that has happened.
She was elated when she found out she had won the competition.
Able to be easily hurt or harmed, either physically or emotionally.
The little bird was vulnerable because it had just fallen from its nest.
Careful about possible dangers or problems.
She was wary of walking alone in the dark.
After running for an hour, I felt famished and wanted to eat a big meal.

Feeling satisfied with yourself and not wanting to improve or change.

Very unpleasant or rude; something that annoys people.

The ability to control yourself and make yourself do things you should, even if you do not want to.

To make someone want to do something, especially something that is not good for them.

Not aware of something that is happening around you.

Not having much experience or knowledge about the world, and therefore easily deceived.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 5: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Six grammar lessons

This file contains six grammar lessons that support Unit 5. Work through them one at a time — do not try to learn everything at once. Read each explanation carefully, study the examples, and then complete the practice activities. Check your answers at the bottom of each lesson. Say the example sentences aloud to help them stick.
Lesson 1: -ing Forms (Gerunds and Participles)
What it means and why it matters

The -ing form of a verb is one of the most flexible tools in English. When we add -ing to a verb, the word can work in different ways: as a noun (called a gerund), as part of a continuous tense, or as a describing word (called a present participle). In this lesson, we focus on using -ing forms as nouns (gerunds) — as the subject of a sentence, as the object after certain verbs, and after prepositions.
How to form it
Start with the base verb and add -ing:
Most verbs: add -ing directly — walk → walking, read → reading, play → playing
Verbs ending in silent -e: drop the e, then add -ing — hike → hiking, make → making, explore → exploring
Short verbs (CVC pattern): double the final consonant — run → running, swim → swimming, plan → planning
Verbs ending in -ie: change -ie to -y, then add -ing — die → dying, lie → lying
Three ways to use -ing forms
1. As the subject of a sentence (gerund as subject)
The -ing form acts like a noun and sits at the beginning of the sentence:
Hiking is a great way to explore nature.
Recycling helps reduce waste in our communities.
Protecting endangered animals requires effort from everyone.
Swimming in the river was the best part of the trip.
2. As the object after certain verbs (gerund as object)

Some verbs are always followed by the -ing form, not the infinitive (to + verb). Common verbs include: enjoy, avoid, finish, suggest, recommend, keep, practise, consider, mind, imagine, risk, miss, deny.
I enjoy exploring new places.
We should avoid wasting water.
She finished writing her report on deforestation.
He suggested planting trees in the schoolyard.
They kept walking even when the path got difficult.
Can you imagine living without clean water?
3. After prepositions
When a verb follows a preposition (in, on, at, by, about, without, before, after, for, of, instead of), it must be in the -ing form:
She is interested in learning about wildlife.
They succeeded by working as a team.
We talked about organising a clean-up day.
Before leaving, we checked for litter.
Instead of complaining, he offered a solution.
Thank you for helping us plant the trees.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using the infinitive after a preposition.
Wrong: "She is interested in to learn."  Correct: "She is interested in learning."
Mistake: Using the infinitive after verbs that need -ing.
Wrong: "I enjoy to read."  Correct: "I enjoy reading."
Mistake: Confusing a gerund (noun) with the present continuous (tense).
Gerund (noun): "Swimming is fun." (Swimming answers "What is fun?")
Present continuous (tense): "I am swimming right now." (Describes an action happening now.)
Memory tip
Think of gerunds as "action nouns". If you can replace the -ing word with "it" or "something", it is a gerund. Example: "Camping is fun" → "It is fun" — so "camping" is a gerund.
Practice 1
A. Fill in each gap with the -ing form of the verb in brackets:
______ (plant) trees helps clean the air.
I enjoy ______ (camp) in the mountains.
She is afraid of ______ (climb) steep hills.
______ (protect) the environment is everyone's duty.
They avoided ______ (pollute) the river.
After ______ (walk) for two hours, we rested.
He suggested ______ (use) solar panels at school.
______ (reduce) waste starts with small daily choices.
Answers: 1. Planting  2. camping  3. climbing  4. Protecting  5. polluting  6. walking  7. using  8. Reducing
B. Write three sentences of your own using -ing forms: one as a subject, one as an object after a verb, and one after a preposition.
Lesson 2: Present Passive
What it means and why it matters

The present passive voice is used when we want to focus on the action or the thing that receives the action, rather than on who does it. It is especially useful in scientific and environmental writing, where the process matters more than the person.
How to form it
Subject + is/am/are + past participle (+ by + agent)
"The forest is protected by law."
"Trees are planted every spring."
"Plastic is recycled at this facility."
"The water is tested for pollution regularly."
The agent ("by law", "by volunteers") can be left out when it is unknown or unimportant: "The water is tested regularly."
Active vs. passive comparison
Active: Rangers protect the forest. → Passive: The forest is protected by rangers.
Active: Volunteers plant trees every year. → Passive: Trees are planted every year.
Active: Factories pollute the river. → Passive: The river is polluted by factories.
Common mistakes
Mistake: Wrong form of "to be" — "The trees is planted."
Fix: Match the verb to the subject — "The trees are planted." (Trees = plural = are)
Mistake: Forgetting the past participle — "The car is drive."
Fix: Use the past participle — "The car is driven."
Practice 2
Rewrite each active sentence in the present passive:
Scientists study endangered species.
The school recycles paper and plastic.
People use solar energy to power homes.
Rangers monitor the wildlife in the park.
Farmers grow organic vegetables without chemicals.

Answers: 1. Endangered species are studied by scientists.  2. Paper and plastic are recycled by the school.  3. Solar energy is used to power homes.  4. The wildlife in the park is monitored by rangers.  5. Organic vegetables are grown without chemicals.
Lesson 3: Past Passive
What it means and why it matters

The past passive is used to talk about actions that were done to someone or something in the past. It is very useful for reporting events, describing history, and writing about environmental changes that have already happened.
How to form it
Subject + was/were + past participle (+ by + agent)
"The national park was established in 1985."
"Two hundred trees were planted by volunteers last month."
"The river was polluted by factory waste."
"The damaged trail was repaired before the rainy season."
Use was for singular subjects (he, she, it, the park) and were for plural subjects (they, we, the trees).
Active vs. passive comparison
Active: The storm destroyed the bridge. → Passive: The bridge was destroyed by the storm.
Active: Volunteers cleaned the beach. → Passive: The beach was cleaned by volunteers.
Common mistakes
Mistake: Mixing up was and were — "The trees was planted."
Fix: The trees = plural = were: "The trees were planted."
Practice 3
Rewrite each active sentence in the past passive:
A local farmer discovered the rare bird.
Heavy rains flooded the village last year.
The government built a new wildlife sanctuary.
Illegal loggers destroyed hundreds of trees.
Students organised an environmental awareness day.

Answers: 1. The rare bird was discovered by a local farmer.  2. The village was flooded by heavy rains last year.  3. A new wildlife sanctuary was built by the government.  4. Hundreds of trees were destroyed by illegal loggers.  5. An environmental awareness day was organised by students.
Lesson 4: Modal Verbs + Passive
What it means and why it matters

When we combine modal verbs (must, should, can, may, might, could) with the passive voice, we can talk about rules, obligations, possibilities and recommendations without naming who does the action. This is very common in environmental discussions and formal writing.
How to form it
Subject + modal verb + be + past participle
Obligation: "Endangered species must be protected by law."
Advice: "More trees should be planted in urban areas."
Possibility: "Renewable energy can be used to power entire cities."
Permission: "The park may be visited between 8 a.m. and 6 p.m."
Negative: "Plastic bags must not be thrown into the ocean."
Common mistakes
Mistake: Forgetting "be" — "Pollution must stopped."
Fix: Always include "be" — "Pollution must be stopped."
Mistake: Wrong word order — "The forest is must be protected."
Fix: Remove the extra "is" — "The forest must be protected."
Memory tip
The formula is always: Modal + be + done. Think of it as a three-word chain: must-be-protected, should-be-reduced, can-be-achieved.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence using the modal verb in brackets and the passive voice:
Water pollution ______ (must / reduce) immediately.
More national parks ______ (should / establish) to protect wildlife.
Plastic waste ______ (can / recycle) into useful products.
Forests ______ (must / not / destroy) for short-term profit.
Environmental awareness ______ (should / teach) in every school.
Clean energy solutions ______ (can / develop) with enough research.
Answers: 1. must be reduced  2. should be established  3. can be recycled  4. must not be destroyed  5. should be taught  6. can be developed
Lesson 5: Expressing the Future
What it means and why it matters

English has three main ways to talk about the future. Each one has a slightly different meaning. Choosing the right form helps you communicate clearly whether you are making a prediction, stating a plan, or describing a fixed arrangement.
1. Will — predictions, promises and spontaneous decisions
Structure: Subject + will + base verb
Prediction: "I think it will rain this afternoon."
Promise: "I will help you carry the camping equipment."
Spontaneous decision: "It's getting cold. I will put on my jacket."
Negative: "Don't worry, it won't take long to reach the summit."
2. Going to — plans and evidence-based predictions
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb
Plan: "We are going to visit the Boni Forest next Saturday."
Intention: "I am going to write a report on deforestation."
Evidence: "Look at the sky — it is going to rain soon."
3. Present continuous — fixed future arrangements
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing (with a future time word)
"We are leaving at 6 a.m. tomorrow." (already arranged)
"The conservation team is meeting on Friday morning."
"I am volunteering at the tree-planting event this weekend."
How to choose the right form
Ask yourself:
Is it a prediction or sudden decision? Use will.
Is it a plan or intention you have already decided? Use going to.
Is it a fixed arrangement with a specific time/place? Use present continuous.
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using "will" for a plan that is already decided.
Wrong: "I will visit the park tomorrow." (if already planned)
Correct: "I am going to visit the park tomorrow."
Mistake: Forgetting am/is/are in "going to" sentences.
Wrong: "She going to help."  Correct: "She is going to help."
Practice 5
Choose the best future form for each sentence:
The eco-club ______ (meet) at the school gate at 7 a.m. on Saturday. (fixed arrangement)
I think solar energy ______ (replace) fossil fuels one day. (general prediction)
We have decided — we ______ (organise) a beach clean-up next month. (plan already decided)
That tree looks unstable. It ______ (fall) in the next storm. (evidence-based prediction)
The rain has stopped! I ______ (go) outside for a walk. (spontaneous decision)
They ______ (fly) to Nairobi on Tuesday for the conference. (fixed arrangement)
Answers: 1. is meeting / are meeting  2. will replace  3. are going to organise  4. is going to fall  5. will go  6. are flying
Lesson 6: Comparative and Superlative Forms
What it means and why it matters

Comparatives and superlatives help us compare people, places and things. This is essential when discussing environmental topics — for example, comparing which energy source is cleaner, which forest is the largest, or which approach is the most effective.
How to form comparatives (comparing two things)
One-syllable adjectives: add -er — tall → taller, clean → cleaner
One-syllable ending in -e: add -r — large → larger, safe → safer
One-syllable CVC: double final consonant + -er — hot → hotter, big → bigger
Two syllables ending in -y: change -y to -i + -er — happy → happier, easy → easier
Two or more syllables: use more — beautiful → more beautiful, sustainable → more sustainable
How to form superlatives (comparing three or more things)
One-syllable: add -est — tall → tallest, clean → cleanest
Two syllables ending in -y: change -y to -i + -est — happy → happiest
Two or more syllables: use most — beautiful → most beautiful
Irregular forms
good → better → best  |  bad → worse → worst  |  far → farther/further → farthest/furthest  |  little → less → least  |  much/many → more → most
Example sentences
Solar energy is cleaner than coal. (comparative)
This is the tallest tree in the forest. (superlative)
Hiking is more enjoyable than sitting indoors. (comparative)
That was the most exciting expedition we have ever been on. (superlative)
This solution is better than the one we tried before. (irregular comparative)
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using "more" with a short adjective — "more tall".
Fix: Use the -er form — "taller."
Mistake: Mixing forms — "more easier".
Fix: Use one form only — "easier."
Practice 6
Write the correct comparative or superlative form:
The Amazon is the ______ (large) rainforest in the world.
Wind power is ______ (efficient) than many people think.
This path is ______ (steep) than the other one.
Recycling is one of the ______ (easy) ways to help the environment.
Deforestation is ______ (bad) now than it was ten years ago.
This national park is the ______ (beautiful) place I have ever visited.
Walking is ______ (healthy) than driving.
This is the ______ (good) camping trip we have ever had.
Answers: 1. largest  2. more efficient  3. steeper  4. easiest  5. worse  6. most beautiful  7. healthier  8. best
