paragraphs: Unit 5: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 5! This unit takes you into the great outdoors. You will explore topics connected to outdoor adventures, nature and environmental protection. Whether it is hiking through a forest, camping beside a river, or working together to protect the natural world, this unit gives you the language skills to describe, discuss and write about these experiences with confidence.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use vocabulary related to outdoor equipment, adventure sports, nature features and eco-friendly practices.
Read and understand texts about outdoor activities and environmental challenges.
Use -ing forms (gerunds and present participles) correctly as subjects, objects and after prepositions.
Understand and use the present passive and past passive to describe processes and events.
Form sentences with modal verbs + passive (e.g. "Trees must be protected").
Talk about the future using will, going to and the present continuous for future arrangements.
Compare things using comparative and superlative adjectives.
Write a trip guide or an environmental protection speech.
Speak confidently about outdoor experiences and environmental issues.
Listen to descriptions of adventures and identify key details.
Key Vocabulary Preview
Before you begin reading, look at these important words. You will meet them throughout the unit. Full definitions, examples and practice activities are in your separate Vocabulary file.
Outdoor and Adventure Words

expedition — a long, organised journey for a special purpose  |  terrain — the physical features of an area of land  |  summit — the highest point of a mountain  |  campsite — a place where people set up tents to stay outdoors  |  trail — a path through the countryside or wilderness  |  navigate — to find your way using a map or compass  |  survival — the state of continuing to live, especially in difficult conditions
Environment and Conservation Words

conservation — the protection of natural things such as animals, plants and forests  |  pollution — damage caused to water, air or land by harmful substances  |  ecosystem — all the living things in an area and how they affect each other  |  renewable — a resource that can be replaced naturally and is not used up  |  biodiversity — the variety of plant and animal life in a particular area  |  sustainability — using resources in a way that does not damage the environment for the future  |  deforestation — cutting down or clearing large areas of trees
Unit Vocabulary from Your Word List

You will also practise these important academic words: acknowledge, alleviate, collaborate, demonstrate, establish, evaluate, incorporate, mitigate, resolve, sustain, and many more. Open your Vocabulary file to study the full list.
Reading: "Guardians of the Green"

Read the following passage carefully. It tells the story of a group of students in East Africa who take part in an outdoor expedition that changes the way they think about nature. As you read, underline any words from your Key Vocabulary list.

On a cool Saturday morning in March, a group of Year 7 students from Madarasatul Nuur Academy in Garissa gathered at the school gate. They were about to begin a two-day expedition to the Boni National Reserve, one of Kenya’s least-visited protected forests. Their teacher, Ustadh Mahdi, had been planning the trip for months.
“Today,” he announced, adjusting his backpack, “we are not just visitors. We are guardians. Everything we see, we must protect.”

The journey took three hours by bus. When the students finally stepped onto the forest trail, the air felt different — cooler, damper, and full of unfamiliar sounds. Tall trees stretched upward, their branches forming a thick canopy that blocked most of the sunlight. Birds called from hidden perches, and somewhere in the distance, a stream gurgled over rocks.

Amina, one of the keenest hikers, was the first to notice the damage. Along the edge of the trail, several large trees had been cut down. Their stumps were pale and raw, surrounded by scattered branches. “Deforestation,” she whispered to her friend Yusuf. “Someone has been logging here illegally.”

Ustadh Mahdi gathered the group. “This is exactly what I wanted you to see,” he said seriously. “The Boni Forest is home to rare animals and hundreds of plant species. When trees are removed, the entire ecosystem suffers. Birds lose their nests. The soil washes away in the rain. Rivers become polluted with mud. Biodiversity decreases.”
He asked the students to take notes and photographs to document what they observed. They would use this evidence later to create environmental awareness materials for their school and community.

That afternoon, a local ranger named Hassan joined the group. He explained how conservation efforts were helping the forest recover. “We have established a tree-planting programme,” he said. “Every month, volunteers plant two hundred seedlings. Protecting the forest is not one person’s job — it requires collaboration.”

The students set up their campsite near a clearing. They collected firewood that had already fallen — they did not break any living branches. They filtered water from the stream and cooked a simple meal of rice, beans and chapati. As the sun set, the sky turned from gold to deep violet, and the first stars appeared. Yusuf pointed upward. “SubhanAllah,” he said quietly. “You cannot see stars like this in the city.”

Around the campfire, Ustadh Mahdi asked each student to share one thing they had learned. “I learned that protecting nature is not just about big organisations,” said Amina. “It starts with us — with small choices, like not littering, like planting a tree, like speaking up when something is wrong.”

The next morning, the group joined the ranger’s team to plant thirty saplings along the damaged section of the trail. Each student dug a hole, placed a seedling carefully inside, and covered the roots with soil. It was hard, rewarding work.

On the bus home, the students were tired but changed. They had seen the beauty of the natural world and the threats it faces. They understood that sustainability is not just a word in a textbook — it is a responsibility. And they were ready to become the guardians their teacher had asked them to be.
(Approximately 540 words)
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences:
Where did the students go on their expedition, and how long did the journey take?
What damage did Amina notice along the edge of the trail?
According to Ustadh Mahdi, what happens to an ecosystem when trees are removed? Give at least three effects.
What conservation programme did Ranger Hassan describe?
How did the students make sure they did not harm the environment at their campsite?
What did Amina say she had learned when the group shared around the campfire?
What activity did the students take part in on the second morning?
In your own words, explain what the title "Guardians of the Green" means.
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what it means in your own words:
expedition (paragraph 1)
canopy (paragraph 3)
deforestation (paragraph 4)
ecosystem (paragraph 5)
biodiversity (paragraph 5)
collaboration (paragraph 7)
sustainability (final paragraph)
Think and Discuss
These questions ask you to go beyond the text and share your own ideas:
Ustadh Mahdi says the students are "not just visitors" but "guardians". What is the difference between a visitor and a guardian? How does this change the way you behave in nature?
Amina says protecting nature "starts with us — with small choices". What small choices can you make every day to protect the environment?
Yusuf is amazed by the stars because he cannot see them in the city. What is something beautiful in nature that people often overlook or take for granted?
In Islam, human beings are described as stewards (khalifah) of the Earth. How does this idea connect to the message of the passage?
Grammar Focus A: -ing Forms

The -ing form of a verb is one of the most versatile tools in English. In this section you will learn three important ways to use -ing forms. Detailed lessons with more examples and practice are in your separate Grammar file.
1. -ing Forms as Subjects (Gerunds)
When we use the -ing form as the subject of a sentence, it acts like a noun. This is called a gerund.
Hiking through the forest was an unforgettable experience.
Protecting the environment is everyone’s responsibility.
Camping near a river teaches you survival skills.
Recycling reduces the amount of waste in landfills.
Notice how each -ing word answers the question "What?" and sits at the beginning of the sentence, just like a noun would.
2. -ing Forms as Objects
After certain verbs, we use the -ing form as the object. Common verbs followed by -ing include: enjoy, avoid, finish, suggest, recommend, keep, practise, consider, mind, imagine.
I enjoy exploring new places.
We should avoid polluting rivers and streams.
She finished writing her environmental report.
He suggested planting more trees in the schoolyard.
They kept walking even when the trail got steep.
3. -ing Forms after Prepositions
When a verb comes after a preposition (in, on, at, by, about, without, before, after, for), it must be in the -ing form:
She is interested in learning about wildlife conservation.
They succeeded by working together as a team.
We talked about organising a beach clean-up day.
Before leaving the campsite, we checked for litter.
You can help without spending any money — just volunteer your time.
Spelling Rules for -ing Forms
Most verbs: add -ing directly (walk → walking, read → reading).
Verbs ending in silent -e: drop the e, then add -ing (hike → hiking, explore → exploring).
Short verbs ending in consonant-vowel-consonant: double the final consonant (run → running, swim → swimming, plan → planning).
Quick Practice: -ing Forms
Fill in each gap with the correct -ing form of the verb in brackets:
______ (camp) under the stars is my favourite outdoor activity.
We enjoy ______ (observe) birds in their natural habitat.
She is passionate about ______ (protect) endangered species.
______ (reduce) plastic waste helps keep our oceans clean.
After ______ (hike) for three hours, we reached the summit.
He avoids ______ (waste) water whenever possible.
They are thinking about ______ (start) a recycling programme at school.
______ (plant) trees is one of the best ways to fight climate change.
Answers: 1. Camping  2. observing  3. protecting  4. Reducing  5. hiking  6. wasting  7. starting  8. Planting
Grammar Focus B: Future Forms

In English, we have several ways to talk about the future. Each form has a slightly different meaning. Understanding these differences will help you communicate your plans, predictions and arrangements clearly. More detailed lessons are in your Grammar file.
1. Will — Predictions and Spontaneous Decisions
We use will + base verb for predictions, promises, offers and decisions we make at the moment of speaking.
Prediction: If we do not act, many species will disappear from this forest.
Promise: I will always pick up litter when I see it on the ground.
Offer: I will carry your backpack — it looks heavy.
Spontaneous decision: It is starting to rain. I will put up the tent now.
2. Going To — Plans and Intentions
We use am/is/are + going to + base verb for plans we have already decided on, or for predictions based on evidence we can see right now.
Plan: We are going to visit the national park next weekend.
Intention: I am going to write a letter to the council about the pollution.
Evidence-based prediction: Look at those dark clouds — it is going to rain.
3. Present Continuous — Fixed Arrangements
We use am/is/are + verb-ing for future events that are already arranged, often with a specific time or place.
We are leaving at six o’clock tomorrow morning.
The conservation group is meeting at the community hall on Friday.
I am volunteering at the tree-planting event this Saturday.
Quick Practice: Future Forms
Choose the best future form (will, going to, or present continuous) for each sentence:
Look at the weather forecast — it ______ (snow) tonight. (evidence-based prediction)
We ______ (camp) by the lake this weekend. Everything is booked. (fixed arrangement)
I think renewable energy ______ (become) more common in the future. (general prediction)
I have decided: I ______ (join) the school’s environmental club. (plan already decided)
Wait — I ______ (help) you set up the tent. (spontaneous offer)
Answers: 1. is going to snow  2. are camping  3. will become  4. am going to join  5. will help
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Passive Voice in Nature
Rewrite each sentence in the passive voice. The first one is done for you.
Active: Rangers protect the forest. → Passive: The forest is protected by rangers.
Active: Volunteers planted two hundred trees last month. → Passive: ______
Active: People must not dump waste in rivers. → Passive: ______
Active: The students documented the damage. → Passive: ______
Active: The government should establish more national parks. → Passive: ______
Active: Local communities are organising clean-up events. → Passive: ______

Answers: 2. Two hundred trees were planted by volunteers last month.  3. Waste must not be dumped in rivers.  4. The damage was documented by the students.  5. More national parks should be established by the government.  6. Clean-up events are being organised by local communities.
Exercise 2: Comparatives and Superlatives
Complete each sentence with the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets:
This trail is ______ (steep) than the one we walked yesterday.
The Boni Forest is one of the ______ (rare) forests in East Africa.
Solar energy is ______ (clean) than burning coal.
Planting trees is one of the ______ (effective) ways to fight climate change.
This campsite is ______ (comfortable) than the last one because it has shade.
Protecting biodiversity is ______ (important) than many people realise.
Answers: 1. steeper  2. rarest  3. cleaner  4. most effective  5. more comfortable  6. more important
Exercise 3: Mixed Grammar
Choose the correct option (a or b) for each sentence:
______ is good for both your body and your mind.  a) Hiking  b) To hiking
The river ______ by industrial waste last year.  a) polluted  b) was polluted
We ______ the national park next Saturday. (already arranged)  a) visit  b) are visiting
Endangered animals must ______ by law.  a) be protected  b) protect
She is interested in ______ about renewable energy.  a) learn  b) learning
This forest is the ______ in the whole country.  a) oldest  b) most old
Answers: 1. a  2. b  3. b  4. a  5. b  6. a
Writing Workshop
Choose one of the two writing tasks below. Follow the planning steps, write your first draft, revise it, and then share the final version with your AI tutor or teacher.
Option A: Write a Trip Guide
Imagine you are writing a guide for younger students at your school who are about to go on an outdoor expedition. Your guide should help them prepare, stay safe, and enjoy the experience.
What to include:
A title and a short introduction explaining where the trip is going and why it is exciting.
A section on what to pack (equipment, clothing, food and water).
A section on safety tips (staying on the trail, what to do if you get lost, basic first aid).
A section on respecting the environment (leave no trace, do not disturb wildlife, collect litter).
A conclusion encouraging the readers to enjoy and learn from the trip.
Language to use:
Imperatives for instructions: "Pack a waterproof jacket.", "Stay on the marked trail."
Future forms for what will happen: "You will hike through beautiful scenery.", "We are going to set up camp by the river."
-ing forms: "Exploring nature is the best way to learn about the environment."
Comparatives for advice: "Boots are more comfortable than sandals for hiking."
Planning your guide:
Decide where the trip is going. Choose a real or imagined outdoor location.
Make notes under each section heading before you start writing.
Write at least 250 words.
Check your spelling, grammar and punctuation before submitting.
Option B: Write an Environmental Protection Speech
Imagine you have been asked to give a speech at a school assembly about why students should care about the environment and what actions they can take.
What to include:
An opening that grabs the audience’s attention (a question, a surprising fact, or a short story).
Two or three environmental problems you want to highlight (deforestation, pollution, loss of biodiversity, etc.).
Practical actions students can take (reduce waste, save water, plant trees, use less plastic, walk instead of driving).
A strong closing that inspires the audience to act.
Language to use:
Passive voice for describing problems: "Forests are being destroyed at an alarming rate."
Modal verbs for obligation and possibility: "We must act now.", "Change can be achieved."
-ing forms: "Reducing our waste is something every student can do."
Future forms for vision: "If we work together, our school will become the greenest school in the city."
Rhetorical questions to engage the audience: "Do we really want to live in a world without forests?"
Planning your speech:
Choose two or three environmental issues that matter to you.
Write an attention-grabbing opening sentence or question.
Plan your main points and the actions you will recommend.
Write at least 300 words.
Practise reading your speech aloud before your final draft.
Listening and Speaking
Listening Activity 1: An Outdoor Adventure Report
Ask your AI tutor to read the following report aloud while you listen. Then answer the questions below.

"Last weekend, our school’s eco-club went on a nature walk along the Jubba River. We left at seven in the morning and walked for about four kilometres. Along the way, we identified fifteen different species of birds and collected three bags of litter from the riverbank. Our guide explained that the river’s water level has been dropping because trees along the bank have been cut down. Without tree roots, the soil erodes and the river becomes shallower. We are going to return next month to plant fifty mangrove seedlings along the riverbank."
Where did the eco-club go?
How far did they walk?
How many species of birds did they identify?
Why has the river’s water level been dropping?
What are they going to do next month?
Which future form is used in the last sentence, and why?
Listening Activity 2: Comparing Two Campsites
Ask your AI tutor to describe two different campsites. Listen carefully, then say which campsite you would prefer and explain why, using comparative adjectives.

"Campsite A is next to a quiet lake. It has shade from tall acacia trees and a clean water supply. However, it is far from the main road, so it takes longer to reach. Campsite B is near the road and easy to access. It has modern toilet facilities but very little shade, and the area can be noisy because of passing vehicles."
Now compare the two campsites using sentences like:
"Campsite A is quieter than Campsite B."
"Campsite B is easier to reach than Campsite A."
"I would prefer Campsite ______ because ______"
Speaking Activity 1: Describe Your Ideal Outdoor Adventure
Prepare a short talk (1–2 minutes) describing your ideal outdoor adventure. Include:
Where you would go and who you would go with.
What activities you would do (use -ing forms: "I would enjoy hiking...").
What equipment you would need.
How you would protect the environment during your trip.
Practise your talk with your AI tutor. Ask for feedback on your pronunciation, vocabulary and grammar.
Speaking Activity 2: Environmental Debate
Choose one of these debate topics and prepare arguments for both sides. Then discuss with your AI tutor, taking turns arguing for and against:
"Adventure tourism helps the environment more than it harms it."
"Schools should make environmental studies a compulsory subject."
"Individuals can make a bigger difference than governments when it comes to protecting the environment."
Use phrases like:
"I believe that..." / "In my opinion..."
"On the other hand..." / "However..."
"The evidence suggests that..."
"To conclude, I think..."
Connecting to the Story

Your companion story for this unit is called "The Weight of a Choice". It is set in a secondary school in Mbale, Uganda, and follows a student named Tendo who is elected Head Prefect. The story explores themes of responsibility, integrity and leadership — values that are just as important in protecting the environment as they are in school life.
As you read the story, think about these connections:
Tendo says, "Integrity is action." How does this idea relate to environmental protection?
The students who took the projector had good intentions but went about it the wrong way. Can good intentions sometimes cause harm to the environment too? Give an example.

Tendo chooses to understand rather than simply punish. How might this approach work when dealing with people who damage the environment — for example, people who cut down trees because they need firewood?
Tendo eventually steps down from leadership to let others grow. How is this connected to the idea of sustainability?
Faith and Values Connection

Islam teaches us that human beings are stewards (khulafaa) of the Earth. This means we have been entrusted with caring for the natural world — the animals, the plants, the water and the land. The Quran reminds us that everything in creation has been placed in balance, and it is our responsibility not to upset that balance through greed, waste or carelessness.

The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said: "If the Final Hour comes while you have a seedling in your hand, and you are able to plant it before the Hour arrives, then plant it." This hadith shows us that caring for the Earth is an act of faith, no matter how small the action may seem.
Think about how these values connect to what you have learned in this unit:
Stewardship (khilafah): We are caretakers of the Earth, not its owners. How does this change the way you think about nature?
Avoiding waste (israf): Islam teaches us not to waste resources, even water during wudu. How can you avoid waste in your daily life?
Balance (mizan): Every part of an ecosystem has a role. What happens when the balance is broken — for example, when too many trees are cut down?
Gratitude (shukr): Being thankful for the blessings of nature means protecting those blessings for future generations. What part of nature are you most grateful for?
Extra Practice: Environmental Problem-Solving
Read the following scenario and answer the questions below using the grammar and vocabulary you have learned in this unit.

Scenario: Your town has a river that runs through the centre. Over the past few years, the river has become polluted. Rubbish is floating in the water. Fish are dying. The trees along the riverbank have been cut down, causing soil erosion. Local businesses are dumping waste into the river at night. A group of students wants to do something about it.
Describe the problem using passive voice: write three sentences about what has happened to the river.
Suggest solutions using modal + passive: write three sentences starting with "The river must...", "Waste should...", and "Trees can..."
Write two sentences about the students' plans using going to and present continuous for future arrangements.
Write a sentence using an -ing form as the subject about why protecting the river matters.
Compare the river before and after the pollution using comparative adjectives (e.g. cleaner, dirtier, more polluted).
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is available to help you at any time. Use it well:
Ask for explanations: "I don’t understand how to use -ing forms after prepositions. Can you explain with examples?"
Practise speaking: "Can you have a conversation with me about outdoor adventures?"
Get writing feedback: "Can you read my trip guide and tell me how to improve it?"
Request extra practice: "Can you give me more exercises on passive voice?"
Prepare for your teacher session: "What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?"
A Suggested Weekly Plan
Check Your Learning
Tick "Yes" or "Not yet" for each statement. For every "Not yet", go back to that section and practise again. Ask your AI tutor for extra help.
I can use vocabulary about outdoor adventures and the environment.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can read a text about nature and answer comprehension questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use -ing forms as subjects, objects and after prepositions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can change sentences from active to passive voice.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use modal verbs with the passive (must be protected, should be established).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about the future using will, going to and the present continuous.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare things using comparative and superlative adjectives.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a trip guide or an environmental speech.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can speak about outdoor experiences and environmental issues with confidence.   Yes □   Not yet □
I have read and understood the story "The Weight of a Choice".   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 6
Make sure you know all the Unit 5 vocabulary words. Test yourself using your Vocabulary file.
Keep using your new grammar skills in everyday English — practise -ing forms and future forms whenever you can.
In Unit 6, Sports, Olympics, and Achievement, you will learn about sports roles, equipment, skills, and the history of the Olympic Games. Start thinking about your favourite sports and athletes.
Tell your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 6? Can you help me get ready?"
Well done — you have explored the great outdoors and learned how to protect our natural world. Keep exploring, keep learning, and keep making a difference.

Aim for 45–60 minutes of study each day. Spread the lesson across the week rather than trying to do everything at once.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 5: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "The Weight of a Choice"
Before You Read
Think about these questions before you begin the story:
What makes a good leader? What qualities do you think a school leader needs?
Have you ever had to make a difficult decision where there was no perfect answer?
What does the word "integrity" mean to you?
As you read, look out for these vocabulary words from your Unit 5 word list. They appear naturally in the story:

abdicate, acknowledge, admonish, alleviate, collaborate, significant, demonstrate, establish, evaluate, embellish, forsake, imitate, incorporate, infringe, plagiarize, inform, relinquish, resolve, sustain, tarnish
The Weight of a Choice

The air at Kasisira Secondary School was dry and warm on the morning the student prefect elections were announced. Posters flapped on walls, dust blew over the netball court, and excited whispers filled the corridor. Among the crowd was sixteen-year-old Tendo, tall and calm, with a sharp mind and quiet confidence.
Tendo had never planned to lead. But when his classmates began asking him to run for Head Prefect, he could not help but consider it.
"People respect you," said Halima, his friend. "You listen. You don't show off. You're fair."
At first, Tendo hesitated. Leadership meant responsibility. And mistakes.

That night, he lay awake evaluating the consequences. Becoming Head Prefect would mean sacrificing some free time, facing criticism, and being held to a higher standard. But it would also be a chance to help fix real problems — like the broken water tank or the crumbling library roof.
The next morning, he signed the nomination form. His campaign slogan was simple: Integrity is action.

His main opponent was Bako, a clever boy who was known for charm and style. Bako's speeches were full of energy. But some students whispered that Bako had once been caught trying to plagiarize an essay from the internet.
"I hope he's changed," Tendo said.
The week before voting, candidates had to give a speech. Tendo stood on the assembly platform and spoke clearly.

"We have problems," he said. "Water is scarce. Classrooms are crowded. Our voices must be heard. I will not promise perfection, but I will promise effort. I will listen, act, and always acknowledge when I am wrong."
The students clapped — not loudly, but sincerely.
Two days later, the results were announced: Tendo had won. The head teacher handed him a badge, and the whole school cheered.
His leadership began well. He organised a clean-up day. He met with teachers about better exam preparation. He even worked to establish a suggestion box system.
But a month later, trouble came.
During break, Halima ran to him, breathless. "Tendo — someone has stolen the lab projector."
The projector, donated by a local NGO, was one of the school's few digital tools. Without it, the science department would suffer.
The news spread fast. The head teacher was furious. "This is a significant loss," she said. "The police have been informed."
Later that day, Tendo sat in the staffroom, thinking. How could something so formidable be stolen without notice?
One teacher suggested, "Security needs to be stricter. Clearly, rules are being infringed."
Investigating the theft became Tendo's top priority. He interviewed students, examined the storage room, and reviewed the gate logs. The cameras did not work — that system had never been repaired.
By Friday, suspicion turned toward a group of Form 4 boys who were often late and once found smoking behind the kitchen. Among them was a boy named Job, a known trickster.
Job was called in. He denied everything.
But that night, Tendo received an anonymous note under his dorm pillow:
"The projector was hidden. Behind the old library."
He raced there with two friends. Behind the crumbling shelves, wrapped in a sack, was the missing device. Still working. Not stolen — just moved.
But why?
A week later, a Form 3 boy came forward and confessed: "We borrowed it. To practise PowerPoint. For a regional ICT competition."
Tendo was shocked. "Why not ask?"
The boy shrugged. "No one listens. We thought we'd be admonished. So we hid it."
Tendo knew the school would want to punish them. But he also knew their intention was not to harm.

He wrote a report to the head teacher. In it, he did not try to embellish the facts. He explained everything honestly and suggested a solution: "The boys should apologise publicly, and they must fix what they've broken — but their motive must be considered."
The head teacher nodded slowly. "This is a reasonable proposal. Well evaluated."
The boys were not expelled. Instead, they helped repair the lab room and wrote a guide for using the projector.
Later, Tendo told Halima, "Sometimes, to lead is to alleviate conflict, not increase it."
But the story did not end there.

Two weeks before the school's Independence Day event, Tendo faced another challenge. The student drama group wanted to perform a play that had been written by a famous Ugandan playwright. But one teacher raised a concern: "We must request permission. This play is copyrighted. Using it without permission is plagiarizing."
Some students were angry. "Who will know?" one asked. "Let's just imitate it."
Tendo shook his head. "We must not pretend. Copying must be avoided. Integrity is not just for show."
Instead, he suggested the students write their own play — about school life. He offered to help them collaborate.
Writing it was not easy. Ideas clashed. Tempers rose. Some students nearly quit. But eventually, a script was developed: funny, honest, and original.

During the performance, laughter echoed in the hall. Teachers smiled. Parents clapped. And when the principal stood to speak, she said, "This is one of the most significant student efforts I have seen."
A local education officer, who had attended, requested a copy to share with other schools.
After the event, Tendo reflected. Leading a school was like trying to sustain a flame in the wind. Not flashy, but steady.
Still, challenges continued. During exam week, two students were caught trying to sneak answer sheets.
"They must be suspended," said one teacher. "Cheating must be punished."
But Tendo asked, "What led to this?"
After speaking with the students, he learned both were under pressure. One cared for three siblings. The other worked nights to help at his uncle's shop.
"It doesn't excuse them," Tendo said, "but understanding the cause can help us respond better."
He recommended that, alongside punishment, the boys be offered support. A reading club was started. Evening prep hours were extended. Discipline was balanced with help.
Certainly, not everyone agreed. Some called Tendo naive.
But others began to follow his example.
One teacher said, "Tendo reminds us that solving a problem is harder than punishing it — but far more valuable."
Another challenge came when the school's debate club invited students to argue whether leaders should be allowed to relinquish power before their term ends.
Tendo, listening from the back, wondered: Should he step down now, in his final term, to let others grow?
He wrote in his diary: "Is knowing when to lead as important as knowing when to leave?"
The next week, he gathered the prefects.
"I believe it's time," he said. "I will abdicate my role early so that a new leader can take over — with fresh ideas."
Halima gasped. "Why?"
Tendo smiled. "Because true leadership is about growth — not power."
His decision was not forced. It was respected.
His replacement was a younger student, attentive and eager.
At the final assembly, the principal spoke.
"Tendo has demonstrated the kind of leadership that teaches others to lead. His actions will not be forgotten. He leaves behind not just policies — but a culture."
The hall stood to applaud.
As Tendo walked off stage, badge in hand, he felt no regret. Only peace.
His time had ended, but the values he planted would remain — like seeds, waiting for the next rain.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer each question in full sentences:
Why did Tendo's classmates want him to run for Head Prefect? What qualities did they see in him?
What was Tendo's campaign slogan, and what did it mean?
What was the "significant loss" that the school experienced? How did Tendo investigate it?
Why had the Form 3 boys taken the projector? What was their intention?
How did Tendo's report to the head teacher show balanced judgement?
Why did the drama group end up writing their own play instead of performing the famous playwright's work?
What did Tendo discover about the two students caught cheating during exam week?
Why did Tendo decide to abdicate his position as Head Prefect before his term ended?
What did the principal say about Tendo's leadership at the final assembly?
Explain the meaning of the last sentence: "His time had ended, but the values he planted would remain — like seeds, waiting for the next rain."
Vocabulary Spotlight
Find each word in the story. Write the sentence it appears in, then write your own sentence using the same word:
evaluate — Tendo lay awake _evaluating_ the consequences.
establish — He worked to _establish_ a suggestion box system.
significant — "This is a _significant_ loss."
infringe — "Clearly, rules are being _infringed_."
admonish — "We thought we'd be _admonished_."
embellish — He did not try to _embellish_ the facts.
alleviate — "To lead is to _alleviate_ conflict."
plagiarize — "Using it without permission is _plagiarizing_."
collaborate — He offered to help them _collaborate_.
sustain — Leading a school was like trying to _sustain_ a flame.
relinquish — Whether leaders should be allowed to _relinquish_ power.
abdicate — "I will _abdicate_ my role early."
demonstrate — "Tendo has _demonstrated_ the kind of leadership..."
Think Deeper
These questions ask you to go beyond the text and share your own thinking:
Tendo says, "Sometimes, to lead is to alleviate conflict, not increase it." Do you agree? Can you think of a real-life example where reducing a conflict was better than punishing people?
The Form 3 boys hid the projector because they felt no one would listen to them. What could the school have done differently so that students felt comfortable asking for help?
One teacher says, "Solving a problem is harder than punishing it — but far more valuable." What does this mean in your own words? Do you agree?
Tendo steps down from his role to let a younger student lead. Is this a sign of strength or weakness? Explain your reasoning.

The story's moral is: "True leadership means facing uncomfortable truths and making decisions that help others, not just yourself." Can you think of a leader (from history, your community, or your family) who fits this description?
In Islam, the concept of amanah (trust and responsibility) teaches that leadership is a trust from Allah, not a privilege. How does Tendo's behaviour reflect this value?
Language Focus: Grammar in the Story
Find examples of each grammar point in the story:
-ing forms:
Find two sentences where an -ing form is used as a subject (gerund).
Find two sentences where an -ing form comes after a preposition.
Find one sentence where an -ing form is used after a verb.
Passive voice:
Find two sentences in the present passive (is/are + past participle).
Find two sentences in the past passive (was/were + past participle).
Find one sentence with a modal verb + passive (must/should + be + past participle).
Future forms:
Find one sentence using "will" for a promise or prediction.
Find one sentence describing a future plan or intention.
Your Turn: Creative Response
Choose one of these activities:
Option A: Write a Diary Entry

Imagine you are Tendo. Write a diary entry for the night after you decided to step down as Head Prefect. Include your feelings, your reasons, and your hopes for the future. Write at least 150 words. Use -ing forms and future forms where you can.
Option B: Write a Letter

Write a letter from Tendo to the new Head Prefect, giving advice on how to be a good leader. Include at least five pieces of advice. Use vocabulary words from the unit (e.g. acknowledge, collaborate, evaluate, resolve, sustain).
Option C: Discussion

With your AI tutor or a classmate, discuss this question: "Is it ever right to break a rule if your intention is good?" Think about the Form 3 boys who took the projector. Prepare arguments for both sides and come to your own conclusion.
tables:

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 5: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 5. They are organised into groups to help you learn them step by step. For each word, read the definition, study the example sentence, and then do the practice activities. Your AI tutor can help you say the words correctly, check your sentences, and quiz you on the meanings.
Group 1: Action and Effort
Practise Group 1
Write a sentence using acknowledge about something you have learned this week.
Describe a time when you had to collaborate with someone on a project.
What is something your teacher could demonstrate in class?
Complete: "Setting up the nature reserve was a great ______."
What rule or system would you like to establish at your school?
Group 2: Judgement and Evaluation
Practise Group 2
How would you evaluate whether a campsite is a good place to stay? List three things you would check.
Write a sentence about a significant environmental problem in your area.
What is one problem in your community that you would like to help resolve?
Finish this sentence: "After the expedition, the students ______ that nature needs our protection."
How can a poster convey an important environmental message?
Group 3: Negative Actions and Consequences
Practise Group 3
What kind of natural disaster could devastate a coastal area?
Give an example of how someone might infringe environmental rules.
Write a sentence using obliterate about damage to a natural habitat.
How might pollution tarnish the reputation of a tourist destination?
What is one animal population that is dwindling in your region? Why?
Use oppress in a sentence about unfair treatment of a community.
Group 4: Improvement and Support
Practise Group 4
What is one action that could alleviate air pollution in a city?
How could a school augment its efforts to be more eco-friendly?
Write a sentence using mitigate about reducing the harm of climate change.
Why is it important to sustain conservation programmes over many years?
How could you incorporate recycling into your daily routine?
Group 5: Honesty and Integrity
Practise Group 5
When might a teacher admonish students during an outdoor trip?
Why is it wrong to embellish a scientific report about the environment?
Why should you never plagiarize someone else's research?
What useful skills might a young explorer imitate from an experienced guide?
Who should you inform if you discover pollution in a local river?
Group 6: Power and Responsibility
Practise Group 6
Write a sentence using abdicate about a leader stepping down.
What is something we should never forsake when it comes to the environment?
Use relinquish in a sentence about giving up control of a project.
What is the difference between abdicate, forsake and relinquish? (Hint: Think about what is being given up.)
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, spell it, explain its meaning, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check your progress.

Review Activities
Activity 1: Match the Word to Its Meaning
Draw a line from each word to its correct definition:
alleviate       a) to completely destroy something
obliterate      b) to work together with others
collaborate     c) to make something less serious
sustain         d) to break a rule or law
infringe        e) to keep something going over time
Answers: 1-c,  2-a,  3-b,  4-e,  5-d
Activity 2: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word from the box to complete each sentence:
[ evaluate   demonstrate   significant   mitigate   embellish   establish   resolve   dwindle ]
The ranger will ______ how to use a compass on the trail.
We need to ______ the problem of litter in our neighbourhood.
The number of elephants in the region continues to ______ each year.
Scientists ______ water samples to check for pollution.
Planting mangrove trees can ______ the damage caused by coastal erosion.
A new wildlife sanctuary was ______ to protect endangered species.
Losing the forest would have a ______ impact on local communities.
Do not ______ your report — write only the facts.
Answers: 1. demonstrate  2. resolve  3. dwindle  4. evaluate  5. mitigate  6. established  7. significant  8. embellish
Activity 3: Use It in Writing
Write a short paragraph (6 to 8 sentences) about an environmental issue you care about. Use at least six words from your Unit 5 vocabulary list. Underline the vocabulary words in your paragraph.

💡  How to learn vocabulary well
Learn 3 to 5 words per day, not all at once.
Say each word aloud at least three times.
Write your own sentence for every word.
Use new words in conversation with your AI tutor.
Review words from earlier days before learning new ones.
To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something.
I want to acknowledge that you did a great job on your project.
To work together with others to achieve a common goal.
The students will collaborate to create posters about protecting the environment.
To show how something works or to prove something clearly.
The ranger will demonstrate how to set up a tent safely.
A serious effort or attempt to do something.
Learning to navigate with a compass was a worthwhile endeavour.
To create or start something that is meant to last.
The school wants to establish an environmental club for students.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me pronounce these words correctly?
Can you give me more example sentences for these words?
What is the difference between acknowledge and demonstrate?
To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is.
Before choosing a campsite, we need to evaluate which location is safest.
Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect.
Losing so many trees was a significant blow to the local ecosystem.
To find a solution to a problem, or to make a firm decision.
We need to resolve the issue of plastic waste in our rivers.
To finish something, or to reach a decision after thinking carefully.
After examining the evidence, the team concluded that the river was polluted.
To communicate or express a message, feeling or idea.
The documentary conveyed the importance of protecting rainforests.
To cause a lot of damage or destruction.
The wildfire devastated thousands of hectares of forest.
To break a rule or law, or to limit someone's rights.
Dumping waste in the river infringes environmental protection laws.
To completely destroy or remove something so it no longer exists.
The flood obliterated the village's crops and farmland.
To treat someone or something in a cruel or unfair way, making them powerless.
Communities that depend on the forest feel oppressed when logging companies take their land.
To damage or spoil something, especially a reputation or quality.
Illegal dumping can tarnish the beauty of a national park.
To become smaller or less in amount, size or strength.
As deforestation continues, the number of wildlife species will dwindle.
To make something less serious or easier to deal with.
Planting trees can help alleviate the effects of flooding.
To make something bigger, better or stronger by adding to it.
We can augment our recycling programme by adding more collection points.
To make something less severe, harmful or serious.
Building a dam will mitigate the damage caused by future floods.
To keep something going or to support it over a long period of time.
We need to sustain our efforts to protect the forest, not just act once.
To include something as part of a larger whole.
The school decided to incorporate environmental education into every subject.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
What is the difference between alleviate and mitigate?
Can you quiz me on the Group 3 and Group 4 words?
Can you help me write sentences using these words about the environment?
To tell someone firmly that they have done something wrong, often in a caring way.
The teacher had to admonish the students for leaving litter in the park.
To make something more interesting by adding details, which may not be true.
She likes to embellish her adventure stories with exciting but exaggerated details.
To copy someone else's work or ideas and pretend they are your own.
If you plagiarize your essay, you could face serious consequences.
To copy someone or something in order to be like them.
Young hikers often imitate their guides when learning survival skills.
To tell someone something important or give them information.
Please inform the ranger if you see any signs of illegal logging.
To give up a position of power or responsibility.
The leader decided to abdicate his role to give younger members a chance to lead.
To give up or leave something or someone behind.
We must not forsake our duty to protect the natural world.
To give up or let go of something, especially power or a right.
She decided to relinquish her position as club president at the end of the year.
