paragraphs: Grammar — Unit 6: Sports, Olympics, and Achievement
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Five grammar mini-lessons

This file has five grammar lessons for Unit 6. Do not try to learn them all in one day! Work through one lesson at a time, complete the practice, and check your answers at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it.
Lesson 1: Compound Nouns
What is a compound noun?
A compound noun is made by combining two or more words to create a new noun with a specific meaning. Understanding compound nouns helps you express yourself precisely and makes your writing richer.
How to form compound nouns
Step 1: Identify two words that can work together to create a new idea.
Step 2: Combine them in one of three ways:
Closed form — no space: football, goalkeeper, notebook, teamwork
Hyphenated form — joined with a hyphen: runner-up, team-mate, half-time, mother-in-law
Open form — two separate words: swimming pool, world record, ice cream, starting line
Step 3: Make sure you understand the new meaning. In football, the word foot describes the kind of ball sport. In world record, the word world describes the scale of the record.
Step 4: Use it in a sentence. Example: "I created a summary table for my project."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Spacing errors: Writing "goal keeper" instead of goalkeeper, or "tooth brush" instead of toothbrush. Always check whether the compound is closed, hyphenated, or open.
Missing hyphens: Writing "runner up" instead of runner-up or "mother in law" instead of mother-in-law.

Meaning confusion: Some compound nouns have meanings that are not obvious from the individual words. For example, football means different sports in different countries. Always check the meaning in context.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence with the correct compound noun formed from the words in brackets.
I need to buy a new __________ (tooth + brush) because mine is old.
Can you check the __________ (score + board) to see who is winning?
The __________ (goal + keeper) made an incredible save in the last minute.
The __________ (swimming + pool) at our school is twenty-five metres long.
The __________ (half + time) break gave the team a chance to rest.
Lesson 2: Abstract Nouns
What is an abstract noun?

Abstract nouns are words that name things you cannot see, touch, or measure with your hands. They represent ideas, feelings, qualities, or states. Understanding abstract nouns helps you express thoughts and emotions clearly and makes your writing more powerful.
How to form abstract nouns
Most abstract nouns come from adjectives or verbs. Here are the most common patterns:
Adjective + -ness: happy → happiness, kind → kindness, conscious → consciousness
Adjective + -ity: diverse → diversity, inevitable → inevitability
Adjective + -ry / -ery: brave → bravery
Verb + -tion / -sion: organise → organisation, resolve → resolution, introduce → introduction
Verb + -ment: achieve → achievement, argue → argument
Noun + -ship: friend → friendship, sportsman → sportsmanship
Noun + -hood: child → childhood, neighbour → neighbourhood
Real sentence examples
The evidence in the case was very convincing.
The theme of the story is about friendship.
We learned about the resolution of the conflict in the play.
Good organisation is key to writing a great essay.
Her bravery during the competition inspired the whole team.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using the adjective instead of the noun: Saying "the brave of the athlete" instead of "the bravery of the athlete." Always check that the form matches the role.
Forgetting to change form: Saying "the organise was good" instead of "the organisation was good."
Confusing abstract and concrete: Remember, abstract nouns are things you cannot physically touch. You can hold a medal (concrete), but you cannot hold courage (abstract).
Practice 2
Complete each sentence with the correct abstract noun form of the word in brackets.
The teacher praised the student’s __________ (organise).
We discussed the __________ (happy) of the main character.
His __________ (brave) during the rescue was admirable.
__________ (diverse) is what makes the Olympic Games so special.
The team’s __________ (determine) carried them to victory.
Lesson 3: Pronouns — Everyone, Someone, Anyone
What are indefinite pronouns?

Pronouns like everyone, someone, and anyone are called indefinite pronouns. They refer to people in a general way, without naming anyone specifically. Using them well makes your English sound natural and fluent.
How to use them
Use everyone when you mean all people: "Everyone is excited about the match."
Use someone when you mean one person but you do not know who: "Someone left their water bottle on the pitch."
Use anyone when you mean any person at all: "Anyone can join the training session."
Important rule: These pronouns are always singular, even though they refer to many people. Use singular verbs with them:
Correct: "Everyone is happy."   Wrong: "Everyone are happy."
Correct: "Someone has a question."   Wrong: "Someone have a question."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using a plural verb: "Everyone are ready" should be "Everyone is ready."
Pronoun agreement: In informal English, we often say "Everyone brought their kit." In formal writing, some guides prefer "his or her", but "their" is now widely accepted.
Practice 3
Choose the correct pronoun (everyone, someone, anyone) and verb form to complete each sentence.
__________ in the stadium __________ (was/were) cheering for the home team.
__________ __________ (has/have) forgotten their sports kit in the changing room.
__________ who wants to play in the tournament __________ (need/needs) to sign up today.
__________ on the team __________ (deserve/deserves) a medal for their effort.
Lesson 4: Comparative Adverbs
What are comparative adverbs?

Comparative adverbs help us compare how actions are done. If you want to say that one person runs faster than another, or that a team played more skilfully in the second half, you use a comparative adverb. They make your descriptions of sport vivid and precise.
How to form comparative adverbs
Step 1: Identify the adverb. Adverbs often end in "-ly" but not always. Examples: quickly, clearly, fast, hard, well.
Step 2: Decide how to form the comparative:
Short adverbs (usually one syllable): add -er. fast → faster, hard → harder, early → earlier.
Longer adverbs (two or more syllables, often ending in -ly): use more or less. quickly → more quickly, carefully → less carefully, clearly → more clearly.
Irregular adverbs: well → better, badly → worse, far → further (or farther).
Step 3: Always use than when comparing two actions: "She runs faster than her opponent."
Real sentence examples
"My sister studies more diligently than I do."
"He speaks less clearly when he is nervous."
"This book explains the topic more thoroughly than the last one."
"She plays football better than anyone else on the team."
"The second runner finished more quickly than expected."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using more with short adverbs: "She runs more fast" should be "She runs faster."
Forgetting than: "She runs more quickly" is incomplete. Add: "She runs more quickly than her friend."
Double comparatives: "She runs more faster" is wrong. Use either "more quickly" or "faster", never both together.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence using the correct comparative form of the adverb in brackets.
My brother plays video games __________ (skilfully) than I do.
She explained the tactics __________ (thoroughly) than the assistant coach.
He runs __________ (fast) than anyone else in the squad.
The team defended __________ (well) in the second half than in the first.
Lesson 5: Expressing the Future
What does it mean?

When we talk about the future in English, we express things that will happen later. This is essential for discussing plans, making predictions, and talking about hopes and goals — all important in the world of sport.
Three ways to express the future
1. Will + base verb — for predictions, promises, and decisions made at the moment of speaking:
"I will train harder this season."
"The match will start at three o’clock."
2. Be going to + base verb — for plans already made and predictions based on evidence:
"She is going to compete in the national championships."
"Look at those clouds — it is going to rain during the match."
3. Present continuous for future arrangements — for fixed plans in the near future:
"We are playing against Hillside School tomorrow."
"The coach is arriving at noon."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using will for definite plans: "I will go to practice on Friday" should be "I am going to go to practice on Friday" if you have already decided.
Confusing present continuous with simple present: "I see my coach tomorrow" should be "I am seeing my coach tomorrow."
Practice 5
Complete each sentence using will, am/is/are going to, or the present continuous form of the verb in brackets.
I __________ (write) my match report this weekend.
She __________ (present) her research on the Olympics next Monday.
We __________ (discuss) the results in our next lesson.
They __________ (finish) their training before the tournament begins.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Compound Nouns)
1. toothbrush   2. scoreboard   3. goalkeeper   4. swimming pool   5. half-time
Practice 2 (Abstract Nouns)
1. organisation   2. happiness   3. bravery   4. Diversity   5. determination
Practice 3 (Pronouns)
1. Everyone … was   2. Someone … has   3. Anyone … needs   4. Everyone … deserves
Practice 4 (Comparative Adverbs)
1. more skilfully   2. more thoroughly   3. faster   4. better
Practice 5 (Expressing the Future)
1. am going to write   2. is presenting (or is going to present)   3. will discuss (or are going to discuss)   4. will finish (or are going to finish)
tables:
💡  Memory Tip
Think of compound nouns as team players. Just like a team works together to win a game, two words team up to create a new word with a special meaning!

💡  Memory Tip
A-B-S-T-R-A-C-T = A Big Space To Remember Abstract Concepts Truly! If you can feel it or think it but cannot hold it in your hand, it is probably an abstract noun.

💡  Memory Tip
Think of "everyone" as a single unit, like a team. Even though it refers to many people, you treat it as one. So remember: "Everyone is a team, so use 'is'!"

💡  Memory Tip
"More or less, it is a test!" Use more when something happens to a greater degree and less when it happens to a lesser degree. Short adverbs just add -er.

💡  Memory Tip
Think of "will" as a promise for the future and "going to" as a plan. Present continuous is for fixed diary entries — it is already in your calendar!

paragraphs: Unit 6: Sports, Olympics, and Achievement
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 6! This unit is all about sports, the Olympics, and what it means to achieve something great. You will explore how athletes train, how the Olympic Games began in ancient Greece, and how the Paralympics have changed our understanding of what the human body can do. Along the way you will build powerful vocabulary, strengthen your grammar, and learn to write about sports with confidence and skill.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand non-fiction texts about sports and the Olympic Games.
Use important vocabulary such as analogy, bias, evidence, viewpoint, and more.
Recognise and form compound nouns (e.g. football, swimming pool, team-mate).
Identify abstract nouns that name ideas and feelings (e.g. courage, achievement, diversity).
Compare actions using comparative adverbs (e.g. more quickly, less carefully, better).
Write a sports profile and a match report using clear structure and lively detail.
Speak confidently about sports topics, present research, and discuss ethical questions.
Think critically about inclusion, fairness, and achievement in sport.
Key Vocabulary

Before you start reading, look through these important words. You will meet them again and again throughout the unit. A full vocabulary guide with definitions, examples, and practice activities is in your separate Vocabulary file.
Words you will need
analogy – explaining something by comparing it to something else
bias – an unfair preference that affects how you think
evidence – facts that help prove something is true
viewpoint – a way of thinking about something
diversity – having many different types of people or things
objective – a goal you want to achieve
resolution – a decision or promise to do something
structure – how something is built or organised
estimate – a careful guess about an amount
inevitable – certain to happen and impossible to avoid
Reading: The Spirit of the Olympics
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the words in bold — they are vocabulary words from this unit.

Every four years, athletes from around the world gather for the Olympic Games. The event is a celebration of sport, hard work, and human achievement. But the story of the Olympics stretches back thousands of years, to a time when ancient Greece hosted the very first competitions in a place called Olympia.

The ancient Games began around 776 BCE. At first, there was only one race — a short sprint called the stadion. Over time, the Greeks added wrestling, boxing, chariot racing, and the pentathlon. Athletes trained for months. Winning brought enormous honour, not just to the individual but to the athlete’s entire city-state. There was no silver or bronze in those days; only the winner received the olive wreath. The contrast between ancient and modern ceremonies is striking, yet the spirit of fair competition has remained the same.

The modern Olympics were revived in 1896 by Pierre de Coubertin, a French educator who believed sport could bring nations together. The first modern Games, held in Athens, featured just 241 athletes from 14 countries. Today, the Games welcome over 10,000 competitors from more than 200 nations — a powerful symbol of global diversity.

One of the most important developments in recent decades has been the growth of the Paralympic Games. The Paralympics celebrate athletes with physical disabilities, proving that courage and determination matter far more than physical limitations. Wheelchair basketball, sitting volleyball, and blade running have become some of the most exciting events to watch. An analogy that many coaches use is this: “Ability is like an iceberg — what you see on the surface is only a small part of the whole.”

Yet the world of sport also faces serious challenges. Bias can influence decisions made by judges and officials. Doping — the use of banned substances to improve performance — threatens fair play. Some critics argue that hosting the Games costs too much money and that ordinary citizens rarely benefit. These are not easy problems, and people hold different viewpoints about how to solve them.

Despite these challenges, the Olympic movement continues to inspire. When a young athlete from a small village stands on the podium, the message is clear: with discipline, training, and a strong objective, extraordinary things are possible. The evidence is in every record broken, every personal best achieved, and every handshake between competitors who moments earlier were rivals.

Sport teaches us that success is not inevitable — it must be earned through effort and perseverance. Whether you play football in a dusty field or swim laps in an Olympic pool, the lessons are the same: set a goal, work hard, respect your opponents, and never stop improving. That is the true spirit of the Olympics, and it belongs to everyone.
Reading 2: A Day in the Life of a Young Athlete
Read this short text about a young runner and think about the discipline needed to succeed in sport.

Halima wakes at five o'clock every morning, even on weekends. Before the sun rises over Hargeisa, she is already stretching on the dusty road outside her home. Running is more than a hobby for Halima — it is her objective, her daily commitment, and the one thing that makes her feel truly alive.

Her training routine is strict. She runs eight kilometres before breakfast, rests during the hottest part of the day, and then does strength exercises in the afternoon. Her coach, a former national athlete, watches her progress carefully and keeps a training log so they can estimate how fast she is improving each month. "Numbers are evidence of hard work," he tells her. "They don't lie."

Halima's dream is to compete at the East African Schools Championships. She knows the competition will be fierce — runners from Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, and Ethiopia train just as hard as she does. But she is not discouraged. "My mother always says that nothing worth having comes easily," Halima explains. "When I feel tired, I remember that every step forward is a step closer to my goal."

Not everything is easy. Halima sometimes struggles to balance training with her schoolwork. She misses social events because she has to sleep early. Some classmates do not understand why she works so hard. But her resolution is firm: she will represent her country one day. For Halima, achievement is not about medals alone. It is about proving to herself that discipline and determination can overcome any obstacle.
After reading, answer:
What is Halima's daily training routine?
Why does the coach keep a training log? What word does he use to describe the numbers?
What challenges does Halima face outside of training?
How does Halima define achievement? Do you agree with her definition?
Compare Halima's story with the Olympic athletes described in Reading 1. What do they have in common?
Understanding the Passage
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
When and where did the ancient Olympic Games begin?
What was the only event at the very first ancient Games?
How many athletes competed in the first modern Olympics in 1896, and how does that compare to today?
What analogy do coaches use to describe ability? Explain what it means in your own words.
Name two challenges that the world of sport faces today, according to the passage.
The passage says success is not inevitable. What does it say success requires instead?
What is the main message of the final paragraph?
Think Deeper
These questions ask you to go beyond the text. There is no single correct answer — the important thing is to explain your thinking clearly.
The passage mentions that only the winner received an olive wreath in ancient Greece. Do you think giving medals to second and third place in modern Games is a good change? Why or why not?
Why do you think the Paralympic Games are important for changing people's viewpoints about disability?
The passage says some people believe hosting the Olympics costs too much. Do you agree or disagree? Give at least one reason.
Think about a sport you enjoy or admire. What qualities does a good athlete in that sport need? Use at least two vocabulary words from this unit in your answer.
Grammar Focus

In this unit you will study three important grammar topics. Your separate Grammar file has full lessons on all five grammar areas for Unit 6. Here we focus on the three most important ones and give you guided practice.
Grammar Topic 1: Compound Nouns
A compound noun is made by combining two or more words to create a new noun with its own specific meaning. Compound nouns are everywhere in the language of sport.
There are three types:
Closed form — two words joined with no space: football, goalkeeper, teamwork
Hyphenated form — two words joined with a hyphen: team-mate, runner-up, half-time
Open form — two separate words that work as one noun: swimming pool, world record, starting line

The key rule is that the first word usually describes or limits the second word. In football, the word foot tells us what kind of ball it is. In world record, the word world tells us the scale of the record.

A common mistake is putting a space in closed compound nouns (writing "goal keeper" instead of goalkeeper) or forgetting the hyphen in hyphenated ones (writing "runner up" instead of runner-up). When in doubt, check a dictionary.
Practice: Compound Nouns
Exercise 1: Identify the compound noun in each sentence and say whether it is closed, hyphenated, or open form.
The goalkeeper saved three shots during the match.
She broke the world record in the 100-metre sprint.
Our team-mate scored the winning goal.
The swimming pool was closed for repairs.
Good teamwork helped them win the tournament.
Exercise 2: Combine these word pairs to make compound nouns. Write each one and use it in a sentence.
score + board = ?
basket + ball = ?
half + time = ?
starting + line = ?
sports + man + ship = ?
Grammar Topic 2: Abstract Nouns

An abstract noun names something you cannot see, touch, or hold. It names an idea, a feeling, a quality, or a state. Abstract nouns are essential when we talk about achievement, character, and the values of sport.
Many abstract nouns are formed from adjectives or verbs:
brave → bravery
achieve → achievement
diverse → diversity
organise → organisation
resolve → resolution
conscious → consciousness
Common suffixes that signal an abstract noun: -ment, -ness, -ity, -tion, -ence, -ance, -dom, -ship.

A common mistake is confusing the adjective with the abstract noun. For instance, saying "The brave of the athlete was amazing" instead of "The bravery of the athlete was amazing." Always check that the form matches the role the word plays in the sentence.
Practice: Abstract Nouns
Exercise 3: Change the word in brackets into an abstract noun and complete the sentence.
The athlete’s __________ (determine) helped her finish the race.
Good __________ (organise) is the key to running a successful tournament.
The team showed great __________ (brave) in the final minutes.
__________ (diverse) makes the Olympic Games special.
His __________ (achieve) inspired young athletes everywhere.
Exercise 4: Sort these words into two columns — concrete nouns (things you can touch) and abstract nouns (ideas you cannot touch).
medal — courage — trophy — fairness — stadium — ambition — whistle — perseverance — track — friendship
Grammar Topic 3: Comparative Adverbs

Comparative adverbs let us compare how actions are done. They are essential for describing sport because we constantly compare performances: who ran faster, who played more skillfully, who trained harder.
How to form them:
Short adverbs (one syllable) usually add -er: fast → faster, hard → harder.
Longer adverbs (two or more syllables, often ending in -ly) use more or less: quickly → more quickly, carefully → less carefully.
Irregular forms must be memorised: well → better, badly → worse, far → further.
Always include than when making a comparison: "She runs faster than her opponent." Forgetting "than" is one of the most common mistakes.
Practice: Comparative Adverbs
Exercise 5: Complete each sentence with the correct comparative adverb form of the word in brackets.
Amina sprints __________ (fast) than anyone else on the team.
The goalkeeper reacted __________ (quickly) in the second half than in the first.
Our school team played __________ (well) this season than last season.
He throws the javelin __________ (far) than all the other competitors.
The referee explained the rules __________ (clearly) the second time.
Exercise 6: Rewrite each sentence using a comparative adverb.
Yusuf trains with a lot of effort. Khalid trains with less effort. → Yusuf trains ...
The swimmer completed the first lap at high speed. The second lap was at higher speed. → She swam ...
Fatima spoke about her experience in a confident way. Amina spoke about it in a less confident way. → Fatima spoke ...
Practice and Review
Mixed Grammar Exercise
This exercise tests all three grammar topics together.
Identify the compound noun: "The scoreboard showed the final result."
Which word is an abstract noun: "The crowd admired her courage"?
Complete with a comparative adverb: "The second-place runner finished __________ (close) to the winner than expected."
Form a compound noun from "basket" and "ball". Is it closed, hyphenated, or open?
Change "strong" into an abstract noun and use it in a sentence about an athlete.
Write a sentence comparing two athletes using the comparative form of "gracefully".
Vocabulary in Context
Choose the correct Unit 6 vocabulary word to complete each sentence.
Word bank: analogy, bias, evidence, viewpoint, objective, resolution, estimate, inevitable, diversity, structure
The coach used an __________ to explain teamwork: "A team is like a machine — every part matters."
Her main __________ this season is to qualify for the national championships.
The journalist showed __________ by only interviewing players from one team.
Scientists found __________ that regular exercise improves concentration.
After the disagreement, the two players reached a peaceful __________.
The __________ of the Olympic village reflects the many cultures of the world.
Experts __________ that over three billion people watched the last Olympic opening ceremony.
From the coach’s __________, the team’s defence needed the most improvement.
Losing a match is not __________ if you prepare well and stay focused.
The __________ of the article was clear: introduction, evidence, conclusion.
Writing Workshop
Writing Task 1: Sports Profile

A sports profile is a short piece of writing that introduces a sportsperson to the reader. It gives key facts, describes achievements, and includes a personal touch that makes the person come alive on the page.
Choose a sportsperson you admire — this can be a famous international athlete, a local player, or even someone from your school or community. Write a sports profile of 200 to 250 words.
Your profile should include:
Introduction: Full name, sport, and country or city of origin.
Background: How they started, what inspired them, any challenges they faced.
Achievements: Key results, records, awards. Use at least one compound noun (e.g. world record, team-mate, championship title).
Qualities: What makes them special? Use at least one abstract noun (e.g. determination, courage, resilience).
Your opinion: Why do you admire this person? End with a strong closing sentence.
Model opening (for guidance):

Mo Farah is a long-distance runner from Somalia who represented Great Britain at the Olympic Games. Born in Mogadishu, he moved to London as a young boy and discovered his love for running at school. His determination and hard work earned him four Olympic gold medals, making him one of the greatest distance runners of all time.
Writing Task 2: Match Report
A match report describes what happened during a sporting event. It is a type of non-fiction writing that uses clear, factual language with some descriptive detail to bring the event to life.
Think of a match or sporting event you have watched or played in. Write a match report of 200 to 250 words.
Your report should include:
Headline: A short, attention-grabbing title (e.g. "Eagles Soar to Victory in Thrilling Final").
Opening paragraph: Who played? When and where? What was the final result?
Key moments: Describe two or three important moments from the match. Use at least one comparative adverb (e.g. "The striker moved more quickly than the defenders").
Outcome and reaction: How did the match end? How did the players or crowd react?
Closing sentence: A comment on what the result means for the team or the season.
Model opening (for guidance):

On Saturday afternoon, Al-Baraka FC defeated Hilaal United 3-1 in a thrilling cup final at the National Stadium. The home side started more aggressively than their opponents, and midfielder Yusuf Ali opened the scoring in the fifteenth minute with a powerful long-range shot.
Writing Task 3: Comparison Paragraph

Write a paragraph of 100 to 150 words comparing the ancient Olympic Games with the modern Olympic Games. Use the information from the reading passage and your own research to make at least three clear points of comparison.
Your paragraph should:
Begin with a clear topic sentence that introduces the comparison.
Use at least two comparative adverbs (e.g. more widely, less frequently, better).
Include at least one compound noun (e.g. city-state, opening ceremony, world record).
End with a sentence explaining which version of the Games you find more interesting and why.
Useful sentence starters for comparisons:
"The ancient Games were organised more simply than the modern Games because..."
"Today, athletes compete more frequently than in ancient times, with events held..."
"While the ancient Olympics featured fewer events, the modern Games include..."
"The opening ceremony is planned more elaborately today than it was in ancient Greece."
Peer Review Checklist
After you finish each piece of writing, use this checklist to review your work. You can also ask a classmate or your AI tutor to check it for you.
Does the writing have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Are there at least two compound nouns used correctly?
Is there at least one abstract noun (e.g. courage, achievement, determination)?
Is there at least one comparative adverb used with "than"?
Are vocabulary words from Unit 6 used accurately?
Is every sentence complete, with correct punctuation?
Does the writing stay on topic throughout?
Listening and Speaking
Speaking Task 1: Sports Debate
A good debate helps you practise giving opinions with evidence. Work with your AI tutor or a partner. Choose one of these debate topics:
"The Olympic Games should be held in the same city every time."
"Paralympic athletes deserve the same prize money as Olympic athletes."
"Professional athletes are paid too much money."
"Traditional games are more valuable than modern sports."
For your chosen topic:
Decide whether you agree or disagree.
Prepare three reasons to support your position. Use words like evidence, viewpoint, and alternative.
Present your argument for one minute, speaking clearly and confidently.
Listen to the opposing view and respond respectfully.
Speaking Task 2: Olympic Research Presentation
Choose one of the following topics and prepare a short presentation of about two minutes:
The history of the Olympic Games: from ancient Greece to today.
A sport that is popular in your community: its rules, equipment, and why people enjoy it.
A Paralympic sport: what it is, who competes, and why it matters.
A famous Muslim athlete: their background, achievements, and what you admire about them.

Your presentation should have a clear structure: an introduction, a middle section with facts and examples, and a conclusion. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit. Practise with your AI tutor before presenting to your teacher.
Speaking Task 3: Sports Interview Role-Play
Practise an interview with your AI tutor or a partner. One person plays a famous athlete, and the other plays a sports journalist.
The journalist should ask at least five questions. Here are some ideas:
"How did you first become interested in your sport?"
"What was the most difficult moment in your career, and how did you overcome it?"
"What advice would you give to young athletes who want to follow in your footsteps?"
"How do you stay motivated when training becomes hard?"
"What is your objective for the next competition?"
The athlete should answer in full sentences, using vocabulary from this unit where possible. After the interview, swap roles so both people get to practise.
Listening Practice: Sports Commentary
Ask your AI tutor to read you a short sports commentary. Listen carefully, then answer these questions without looking at the text:
Which sport is being described?
What key event happened during the match?
What compound nouns did you hear in the commentary?
How did the commentary describe the players’ actions? (Look for comparative adverbs.)
After answering, ask your AI tutor to show you the text so you can check your answers. Highlight any vocabulary words you recognise.
Critical Thinking Corner
Disability and Inclusion in Sport
The Paralympic Games have done a great deal to change how society views people with disabilities. But there is still much work to be done.
Why do you think the Paralympics receive less media attention than the Olympics? Is this fair?
What can schools do to make sports more inclusive for students of all abilities?
The passage used an analogy: "Ability is like an iceberg." Create your own analogy for the idea that people have hidden strengths.
Ethics in Sport
Sports sometimes raise difficult ethical questions. Think about these situations and write your opinion in two or three sentences for each:
An athlete uses a banned substance to win a competition. Later, they say they did not know the substance was banned. Should they be punished? Why or why not?
A country spends billions of dollars to host the Olympic Games while many of its citizens live in poverty. Is this the right decision? What is the alternative?
A referee makes a mistake that costs a team the match. Should technology always be used to check decisions? What are the advantages and disadvantages?
Ancient vs. Modern Olympics

Create a comparison table with two columns: Ancient Olympics and Modern Olympics. Include at least five differences. Think about: events, participants, prizes, countries involved, and the role of women. After completing your table, write a short paragraph explaining which version of the Olympics you find more interesting and why.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is available to help you at any time. Here are some powerful ways to use it:
Vocabulary boost: "Can you give me five more sentences using the word 'inevitable'?"
Grammar check: "Can you check if I used comparative adverbs correctly in my writing?"
Writing feedback: "Can you read my match report and suggest improvements?"
Speaking practice: "Can you ask me questions about the Olympics so I can practise answering?"
Exam preparation: "Can you give me a short quiz on compound nouns and abstract nouns?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Use this checklist to track your progress. Tick "Yes" or "Not yet" for each skill. For every "Not yet", ask your AI tutor for extra practice.
I can read and understand texts about sports and the Olympic Games.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use Unit 6 vocabulary accurately in my own sentences.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can identify and form compound nouns (closed, hyphenated, and open).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can recognise abstract nouns and form them from adjectives and verbs.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare actions using comparative adverbs correctly.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a sports profile with clear structure and strong vocabulary.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a match report using factual and descriptive language.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can speak about sports topics with confidence and evidence.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can think critically about inclusion, fairness, and ethics in sport.   Yes □   Not yet □
Answer Key
Exercise 1: Compound Nouns
1. goalkeeper (closed)   2. world record (open)   3. team-mate (hyphenated)   4. swimming pool (open)   5. teamwork (closed)
Exercise 2: Compound Nouns — example answers

1. scoreboard (closed) — "The scoreboard lit up when the team scored."   2. basketball (closed) — "We played basketball after school."   3. half-time (hyphenated) — "The coach spoke to the team at half-time."   4. starting line (open) — "All the runners stood behind the starting line."   5. sportsmanship (closed) — "Good sportsmanship means respecting your opponents."
Exercise 3: Abstract Nouns
1. determination   2. organisation   3. bravery   4. Diversity   5. achievement
Exercise 4: Sorting Nouns
Concrete: medal, trophy, stadium, whistle, track   Abstract: courage, fairness, ambition, perseverance, friendship
Exercise 5: Comparative Adverbs
1. faster   2. more quickly   3. better   4. further (or farther)   5. more clearly
Exercise 6: Comparative Adverbs — example answers
1. Yusuf trains harder than Khalid.   2. She swam the second lap more quickly (or faster) than the first.   3. Fatima spoke more confidently than Amina.
Mixed Grammar Exercise

1. scoreboard (closed compound noun)   2. courage (abstract noun)   3. more closely   4. basketball — closed form   5. strength — e.g. "The athlete’s strength helped him lift the weight."   6. e.g. "She performed more gracefully than any other gymnast in the competition."
Vocabulary in Context
1. analogy   2. objective   3. bias   4. evidence   5. resolution   6. diversity   7. estimate   8. viewpoint   9. inevitable   10. structure
Getting Ready for Unit 7
In Unit 7 you will explore Success, Careers, and Personal Growth.
Make sure you know all the Unit 6 vocabulary words on your word list.
Think about what "success" means to you — is it about winning, or something more?
Ask your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 7? Can you help me get ready?"
Well done on completing Unit 6. Every word you learn and every sentence you write brings you closer to your goals. Keep going — you are building something extraordinary.

paragraphs: Story — Unit 6: Sports, Olympics, and Achievement
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Truth in the Margins”
The Truth in the Margins
“Everyone is talking about it,” said Jonah, sliding his tray onto the lunch table. “Did you see the new story in the school magazine?”
Asha looked up. “No. What’s it about?”
Jonah lowered his voice. “It’s a piece about exam cheating — but it blames the Form 3 students. People are angry. They say it’s full of bias.”
Asha blinked. She was the editor of The Crescent, the school’s monthly publication. As far as she knew, no article like that had been approved. She stood up and walked straight to the notice board.
There it was: a stapled page titled “The Downfall of Discipline.”

She read quickly. The article claimed that cheating had become inevitable, and it made sharp comparisons between current students and “the golden classes of the past.” There was a dramatic climax accusing one entire stream of “undermining the school’s values.”
“No name. No byline,” she murmured. “But it’s written like an opinion piece. And it’s not neutral.”

The problem wasn’t just the anger. It was the structure. The article mixed opinion and fact with no clear introduction or logical organization. Worse, it didn’t use proper evidence. It quoted unnamed sources and offered no balance in viewpoint.
By afternoon, students from Form 3 were yelling in the corridor. One group accused the editorial team of targeting them. Another group defended the piece, saying it was an “honest reflection.”
That evening, Asha called a meeting in the journalism room. Everyone on the team came — except Faith, the assistant editor.
“I didn’t publish that,” Asha said. “It wasn’t part of the approved structure.”
“Then how did it get printed?” Jonah asked.
Asha paused. “We only printed five copies. Someone added a sixth.”
Jonah frowned. “So someone illustrated their opinion with false facts.”
“It’s more serious than that,” Asha said. “It could damage our school’s unity. The implication is that a whole group is dishonest.”
The next morning, the head teacher summoned her. “This has become a judicial matter within the school. The article sparked complaints. You need to respond.”
Asha nodded. “We’re preparing a public resolution. And I want to publish a follow-up article that explains the context.”
The head teacher tapped his pen. “Just be careful. More damage could happen if the issue isn’t handled more objectively.”
Back at the newsroom, the team argued over what to do next. One writer said, “Let’s write an angry reply. Expose whoever did it.”
Jonah shook his head. “That’s just argumentative for no reason. We need to think.”
Asha took a deep breath. “We should focus on explaining the process. Use facts. Include quotations. And explore multiple viewpoints.”

“We’ll write a new piece,” she said, “called The Truth in the Margins. It’ll have a clear introduction, supported by real evidence, and offer alternative voices. It’ll ask questions instead of blaming.”

They began the next day. Asha gathered real accounts from students, teachers, and prefects. Jonah wrote an excerpt about the history of cheating incidents. They included a summary of school rules and policies and made sure each paragraph had proper organization.
“I’ll also add a section about censorship,” Jonah said. “So readers understand why unofficial publications matter.”
Faith arrived at the newsroom midweek, red-faced and silent. After a long pause, she whispered, “I added the article. I thought it would create discussion.”
Asha stared at her. “But without approval?”
“I’m sorry,” Faith said. “I just wanted to raise the issue. I didn’t expect it to spread.”
Asha could have been angry. But she saw something in Faith’s face — fear and regret.
“We can’t undo it,” she said. “But we can illustrate the bigger issue. Why did you feel unheard?”
Faith’s eyes welled. “I felt like no one was acknowledging the real pressure we’re under. We act like everything’s fine, but it’s not.”
The team sat quietly. Then Jonah said, “Maybe that’s the real story.”

They added a new section: Behind the Panic: Student Voices. It showed anonymous student reflections on stress, comparing the pressure now to earlier years. They used an analogy: “Stress is like a rope. The more you pull, the more likely it snaps.”
They also estimated that over 80% of senior students worried about exams daily.

The piece included proper structure, used neutral language, and had diverse perspectives. One line read: “Some believe discipline has dwindled; others say expectations have changed. The truth may lie in the contrast.”
On Friday, they published the corrected edition.
The school read it during homeroom. It did not cause a riot. It caused reflection.
One teacher said, “It’s far more concisely written than the first.”
Another said, “It’s balanced. I see no bias.”
Faith was not punished. But she did write a short note in the next issue, acknowledging the importance of proper publishing process.
Over the next weeks, students submitted more writing — essays about resilience, opinion pieces about uniform rules, even poems about exam fear.
Jonah grinned. “Our next edition is going to be big.”
Asha smiled. “And far less chaotic.”
At the end of the term, The Crescent was nominated for a regional schools writing award.
As they gathered for the celebration, Asha reflected: organization, truth, and diversity don’t just make good stories — they build strong communities.
In a world where censorship, bias, and disconnection can spread quickly, storytelling had become their tool for clarity.
And in the margin of that moment, truth had finally found its voice.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer in full sentences. Use evidence from the story to support your answers.
What was Asha’s role at the school, and what was the name of the school magazine?
What did the unauthorised article claim, and why did it anger the Form 3 students?
How did Asha discover that the article had not been officially published?
What problems did Asha identify with the structure of the original article?
Why did the head teacher call it a “judicial matter”?
What steps did Asha and the team take to create the corrected edition?
Who was responsible for adding the article, and what was her reason?
What analogy did the team use to describe student stress?
How did the school react to the corrected edition of The Crescent?
What happened at the end of the term that showed the team’s work had been successful?
Think Deeper
Asha chose not to punish Faith directly. Do you think this was the right decision? Explain your viewpoint.
The story suggests that the original article lacked balance and evidence. Why are these two things so important in any piece of writing?
Jonah said, “Maybe that’s the real story.” What did he mean? What was the “real story” behind the controversy?
The title is “The Truth in the Margins.” What do you think the word “margins” represents in this story? Think beyond the literal meaning.
How does this story connect to the theme of “Sports, Olympics, and Achievement”? Think about qualities like teamwork, fairness, and working towards a goal.
Find the Vocabulary
Go back through the story and find each of these Unit 6 vocabulary words in bold. Write the sentence each word appears in, then explain the word in your own language:
analogy, argumentative, bias, climax, context, contrast
diversity, estimate, evidence, excerpt, alternative, illustrate
implication, inevitable, introduction, judicial, quotation, objective
organization, resolution, structure, summary, viewpoint, censorship
Language Study
Find examples of these grammar features in the story:
Find two compound nouns (e.g. notice board, newsroom, homeroom).
Find two abstract nouns that name feelings or ideas (e.g. unity, reflection, resilience).
Find one example of a comparative adverb (e.g. "more concisely", "more objectively").
Find one sentence that expresses the future (e.g. "Our next edition is going to be big.").
Now You Try: Writing
Write a letter to Asha. In 100 to 150 words, give her your advice about how to handle the situation. Do you agree with how she resolved it? What would you have done differently?

Write a short editorial. Imagine you are a student journalist at your own school. Write a 150-word editorial about an issue that matters to your school community. Use clear structure, evidence, and at least five vocabulary words from this unit.
Create a comparison. Write a short paragraph comparing the original article with the corrected edition. Use at least two comparative adverbs (e.g. more objectively, more clearly, less aggressively).

Setting: A mixed-day school in Kisumu, Kenya.

Read it slowly. When you meet a word in bold, it is a Unit 6 vocabulary word. Say it out loud!
