💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you check my grammar answers and explain
any mistakes? I want to make sure I understand each rule properly.'

paragraphs: Unit 8: Media, Technology, and News
Year 7 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 8. This unit is called "Media, Technology, and News" because you will explore how information travels in the modern world and why it matters to think critically about everything you read, watch, and share. Every day, millions of messages flow through newspapers, television channels, websites, and social media platforms. Some of these messages are carefully researched and truthful. Others are misleading, exaggerated, or completely false. As a young person growing up in the digital age, one of the most important skills you can develop is the ability to tell the difference between reliable information and misinformation.

Throughout this unit you will read a new passage about a school journalism club that investigates a viral rumour, study the grammar of reported speech, multi-word verbs, and conditionals, and complete a writing workshop where you create a school magazine article and analyse fake news. You will also take part in speaking and listening activities that bring media literacy to life, including role-play interviews, news broadcasts, and structured debates. By the end of this unit you will understand how news is created, how to evaluate sources, and how to communicate information responsibly and accurately. These are skills that will serve you well in every subject, in your community, and in your future career.

In Islam, truthfulness is one of the highest virtues. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said, "Truthfulness leads to righteousness, and righteousness leads to Paradise." The Quran also warns us in Surah Al-Hujurat (49:6) to verify information before acting upon it: "O you who believe, if a sinful person brings you news, verify it, lest you harm people unknowingly and then regret what you have done." This unit will help you put that principle into practice every time you encounter a piece of news or a claim on social media.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what media literacy means and why it is important for young people today.
Identify the key features of a reliable news source and an unreliable one.
Read a text about media and answer comprehension and analysis questions in full sentences.
Use reported speech correctly to report what someone said or wrote.
Recognise and use common multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) related to media and communication.
Form and use first and second conditional sentences to discuss possibilities and consequences.
Write a school magazine article that follows journalistic conventions such as headlines, leads, and quotes.
Analyse a piece of fake news and explain why it is unreliable, using specific evidence.
Take part in a mock news broadcast, a debate about digital media ethics, and a role-play interview.
Reflect on your own learning using self-assessment statements and set goals for improvement.
Key Vocabulary

The words below appear throughout this unit. Read each definition carefully and study the example sentence. Try to use at least four of these words in your own writing this week. Whenever you meet an unfamiliar word in the reading passage or activities, come back to this table. Your full vocabulary companion file has all thirty unit words with extended practice activities.
Reading: Breaking News at Ilays Academy

Read the following passage carefully. It tells the story of a school journalism club that investigates a viral rumour. As you read, pay attention to the steps the students take to verify the information and how they communicate their findings. Think about what you would do if you encountered a similar situation at your own school.
Breaking News at Ilays Academy

It was a Tuesday morning when Farah first saw the message on his phone. Someone had posted a video on social media claiming that Ilays Academy would be shut down at the end of the month. The video showed a blurry photograph of a letter supposedly from the Ministry of Education, and a voice said, "This school has failed its inspection. All students must find new schools immediately."

Within minutes, the message had been shared hundreds of times. Parents began calling the school in a panic. Some students arrived at the gates looking worried, asking their friends if the news was true. Farah's younger sister Sumaya ran to him during the morning break, her eyes wide with fear.
"Is it true?" she asked. "Are they closing our school?"

Farah looked at the video again carefully. Something about it did not feel right. The letter in the photograph was blurry, and he could not see an official stamp or signature. The voice in the video belonged to someone he did not recognise, and there was no name or source given. He remembered what his English teacher, Ustadh Khalid, had taught them about evaluating sources: always check who is making the claim, look for evidence, and verify information before sharing it.
"I do not think this is real," Farah told Sumaya. "But let us find out for certain."

During the lunch break, Farah gathered a small group of students from the school's journalism club. They decided to investigate the rumour systematically. First, they visited the school office and asked the administrator directly. The administrator confirmed that no such letter existed and that the school had passed its most recent inspection with excellent marks. She even showed them the real inspection report.

Next, Yusra, the club's best researcher, searched the Ministry of Education's official website. There was no announcement about closing any school in their district. She also checked the accounts that had shared the original video and noticed they were all very new accounts with no other posts, which is a common sign of a deliberate misinformation campaign.

The journalism club wrote a short, clear article for the school noticeboard and the school's messaging group. They included three pieces of evidence: a quote from the administrator, a link to the official inspection results, and an explanation of why the video was not trustworthy. They also added a helpful list of tips for spotting fake news, such as checking the source, looking for official evidence, and waiting before sharing unverified information.

By the end of the day, the panic had calmed. Parents who had been worried sent messages of thanks. The headteacher praised the journalism club in the next assembly and said, "These students have shown us that the best response to false information is not fear or anger. It is careful investigation, clear communication, and the truth."

Farah felt proud. He had learned something important that day: in the age of social media, everyone has a responsibility to check information before they believe it or share it. The truth does not spread by itself. It needs people who care enough to find it and share it responsibly.
Understanding the Text

Now that you have read the passage about Ilays Academy, answer the following questions in full sentences. Write your answers in your exercise book or type them on your device. Think carefully about the details in the text and use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What was the viral message about, and how did it first reach Farah?
List three signs that made Farah suspicious about the video. Why should these things make someone doubt a piece of news?
What did Ustadh Khalid's lesson teach the students about evaluating sources? Summarise the advice in your own words.
Describe the three steps the journalism club took to investigate the rumour. Why was each step important?
What evidence did the journalism club include in their article for the school noticeboard? Why did they choose these specific pieces of evidence?
How did the accounts that shared the original video suggest it was part of a deliberate misinformation campaign?
The headteacher said, "The best response to false information is not fear or anger. It is careful investigation, clear communication, and the truth." Explain what this means in your own words.
Why does Farah say that "the truth does not spread by itself"? Do you agree with him? Explain your answer.
Imagine someone at your school shared a similar fake message on social media. Write down three things you would do to respond responsibly.
How does this passage connect to the Islamic principle of verifying information before acting on it (Surah Al-Hujurat 49:6)?
Grammar Focus: Reported Speech

When journalists write news stories, they often need to report what someone said or wrote. Instead of using the person's exact words (direct speech), they change the words into reported speech (also called indirect speech). This is one of the most important grammar skills for writing about media and news, because reporters rarely copy someone's words exactly. They summarise, paraphrase, and attribute statements to their sources. Learning reported speech will also help you in everyday English when you tell a friend what someone else told you.
Direct Speech vs. Reported Speech
Direct speech uses the speaker's exact words inside quotation marks. Reported speech removes the quotation marks and changes the tense, pronouns, and time words to fit the reporter's perspective.
Examples:
Direct: Farah said, "I do not think this is real."
Reported: Farah said that he did not think it was real.
Direct: The administrator said, "The school has passed its inspection."
Reported: The administrator said that the school had passed its inspection.
Direct: Yusra told the group, "I will check the official website."
Reported: Yusra told the group that she would check the official website.
How to Change Direct Speech to Reported Speech
Follow these steps:
Remove the quotation marks and add the word that after the reporting verb (said, told, explained, etc.). The word "that" is optional but helpful for clarity.
Change the tense back by one step: present simple becomes past simple, present perfect becomes past perfect, "will" becomes "would", "can" becomes "could".
Change the pronouns to match the person being reported: "I" often becomes "he" or "she"; "we" becomes "they"; "my" becomes "his" or "her".
Change time and place words if needed: "today" becomes "that day"; "tomorrow" becomes "the next day"; "here" becomes "there"; "now" becomes "then".
Common Reporting Verbs
Journalists use different reporting verbs depending on how the person spoke. Here are some useful ones:
said — the most common and neutral: "She said that the report was inaccurate."
told — always followed by a person: "He told the students that the rumour was false."
explained — when giving reasons or details: "The teacher explained that checking sources is important."
claimed — when the truth is uncertain: "The video claimed that the school would close."
admitted — when accepting something reluctantly: "He admitted that he had shared the message without checking it."
denied — when saying something is not true: "The ministry denied that any school was being closed."
warned — when alerting about danger: "She warned us that fake news can cause real harm."
A Common Mistake
Mistake: "Farah said that he does not think it is real." (tense not changed)
Fix: "Farah said that he did not think it was real." (tense shifted back one step)
Grammar Focus: Multi-Word Verbs (Phrasal Verbs)

Multi-word verbs (also called phrasal verbs) are verbs made up of a main verb plus one or two small words called particles (such as up, down, out, off, on, into). The meaning of a multi-word verb is often different from the meaning of the main verb on its own. For example, "look" means to use your eyes, but "look into" means to investigate. Multi-word verbs are extremely common in English, especially in everyday speech and in media writing. Learning them will make your English sound more natural and fluent.
Multi-Word Verbs Related to Media and Communication
find out — to discover information: "The journalists found out that the story was false."
look into — to investigate: "The police are looking into the source of the fake video."
turn out — to be discovered to be: "The rumour turned out to be completely untrue."
come across — to find by chance: "I came across an interesting article about media literacy."
point out — to draw attention to something: "The editor pointed out several errors in the report."
make up — to invent (often a lie): "Someone made up the story to cause panic."
pass on — to share information with others: "Please do not pass on unverified news."
bring up — to raise a topic in conversation: "Aminah brought up the issue at the meeting."
set up — to create or establish: "The students set up a school newspaper."
carry out — to complete or perform a task: "The club carried out a thorough investigation."
A Common Mistake
Mistake: "The students looked the rumour into." (particle in wrong position)
Fix: "The students looked into the rumour." (particle stays with the verb when the object is a noun phrase)

Note: Some multi-word verbs are separable (you can put the object between the verb and the particle) and some are inseparable (the particle must stay next to the verb). "Look into" is inseparable. "Find out" is separable: "She found the truth out" or "She found out the truth" are both correct.
Grammar Focus: Conditionals (First and Second)

Conditional sentences use the word if to talk about possibilities and their results. They are very useful in media discussions because they help us talk about what might happen, what could have been avoided, and what we should do in certain situations. There are several types of conditionals, but in this unit we will focus on the first conditional (real and likely possibilities) and the second conditional (imaginary or unlikely situations).
First Conditional (Real Possibilities)
We use the first conditional to talk about things that are likely to happen in the future. The structure is:
If + present simple, will + base verb
Examples:
If you check the source, you will know whether the news is real.
If students share fake news, it will cause unnecessary panic.
If the school sets up a newspaper, more students will learn about journalism.
The club will publish the article if they find enough evidence.
Notice that the "if" clause can come first or second in the sentence. When it comes first, we use a comma to separate the two parts. When it comes second, no comma is needed.
Second Conditional (Imaginary Situations)
We use the second conditional to talk about things that are imaginary, unlikely, or not true now. The structure is:
If + past simple, would + base verb
Examples:
If everyone verified information before sharing it, there would be much less misinformation.
If I were a journalist, I would always check my facts carefully.
If social media did not exist, news would travel much more slowly.
People would trust the media more if reporters were always honest.

Note: In the second conditional, we often use "were" instead of "was" for all subjects (including I, he, she, it). This is especially common in formal writing. "If I were a journalist" is preferred over "If I was a journalist," although both are acceptable in everyday speech.
A Common Mistake
Mistake: "If I will check the source, I will know the truth." ("will" used in both clauses)
Fix: "If I check the source, I will know the truth." (present simple in the if-clause, "will" only in the result clause)
Grammar Practice

Complete the following exercises in your exercise book or on your device. Check your answers using the answer keys provided. If you get an answer wrong, read the explanation and try again. Your AI tutor can also help you understand any mistakes.
Exercise 1 — Change to Reported Speech
Rewrite each sentence in reported speech. Remember to change the tense, pronouns, and time words as needed.
Farah said, "I do not believe this video is real."
The administrator told them, "The school has passed its inspection."
Yusra said, "I will search the official website tomorrow."
The headteacher announced, "These students have shown great responsibility."
Sumaya asked, "Are they closing our school?"

Answer Key: 1. Farah said that he did not believe the video was real. 2. The administrator told them that the school had passed its inspection. 3. Yusra said that she would search the official website the next day. 4. The headteacher announced that those students had shown great responsibility. 5. Sumaya asked whether / if they were closing their school.
Exercise 2 — Complete with Multi-Word Verbs
Fill in each gap with the correct multi-word verb from the list: find out, look into, turn out, point out, make up, pass on, set up, carry out.
The journalism club decided to __________ the rumour before writing their article.
Someone had completely __________ the story about the school closing.
The students __________ a noticeboard article to share the truth.
The teacher __________ that the video had no official stamp or signature.
The fake story __________ to be a deliberate attempt to cause panic.
Please do not __________ unverified information to your friends and family.
The reporter __________ a thorough investigation before publishing the story.
We need to __________ the facts before we can write an accurate report.
Answer Key: 1. look into  2. made up  3. set up  4. pointed out  5. turned out  6. pass on  7. carried out  8. find out
Exercise 3 — First or Second Conditional?
Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets. Decide whether each sentence needs the first conditional (real possibility) or the second conditional (imaginary situation).
If you __________ (check) the source, you __________ (know) whether the news is real. (first conditional)
If social media __________ (not exist), fake news __________ (not spread) so quickly. (second conditional)
If the journalist __________ (verify) the story, she __________ (publish) it tomorrow. (first conditional)
If I __________ (be) the editor, I __________ (never publish) an article without checking the facts. (second conditional)
If people __________ (share) the correction, the truth __________ (reach) everyone by tonight. (first conditional)
Answer Key: 1. check; will know  2. did not exist; would not spread  3. verifies; will publish  4. were; would never publish  5. share; will reach
Exercise 4 — Write Your Own Sentences
Write one original sentence for each of the following. Use the grammar you have learned in this unit:
A sentence using reported speech to report something your teacher said recently.
A sentence using the multi-word verb find out about a piece of news.
A first conditional sentence about what will happen if students learn media literacy.
A second conditional sentence imagining a world without social media.
Writing Workshop

This unit includes two writing projects. The first asks you to write a school magazine article using journalistic conventions. The second asks you to analyse a piece of fake news and explain why it is unreliable. Both projects will help you develop critical thinking, clear communication, and media literacy skills that are valuable far beyond the English classroom.
Project A — Write a School Magazine Article

Imagine your school has just launched its first student magazine. You have been chosen as one of the reporters. Your task is to write an article about a recent event at your school or in your community. The article should be between 200 and 300 words and must follow the conventions of good journalism.
Journalistic conventions to include:
A headline that is short, attention-grabbing, and accurate.
A lead paragraph (the first paragraph) that answers the five Ws: Who? What? When? Where? Why?
At least one direct quote from a person involved in the event (you may make this up if you are writing about an imaginary event).
At least one sentence using reported speech to paraphrase what someone said.
Factual, balanced language — avoid personal opinions unless you are writing an opinion column.
A concluding sentence that summarises the main point or looks ahead to what might happen next.
Suggested topics (choose one or invent your own):
A new club, team, or project that has started at your school.
A community event such as a charity collection, sports day, or cultural celebration.
A visit by a guest speaker or an interview with a member of staff.
A debate or discussion about a school rule that students want to change.
A feature article about how students at your school use technology for learning.
Before you submit, check your article against this checklist:
My headline is short, clear, and makes the reader want to read more.
My lead paragraph answers at least four of the five Ws.
I have included at least one direct quote with correct punctuation.
I have included at least one sentence in reported speech.
My language is factual, clear, and balanced.
I have checked my spelling, punctuation, and grammar.
My article is between 200 and 300 words.
Project B — Fake News Analysis

In this project, you will look at a piece of fake news and analyse why it is unreliable. Your teacher or AI tutor can give you a sample fake news article, or you can use the example below. Your analysis should be written in clear paragraphs and should include specific evidence for each point you make.
Sample fake news claim:

"BREAKING: Government announces that all schools will switch to online-only learning permanently starting next month. Students will no longer need to attend school in person. A source close to the Ministry confirmed the plan."
Analyse the claim by answering these questions:
Who is the source? Is the source named, or is it anonymous? Why does this matter?
What evidence is provided to support the claim? Is there any official document, statement, or data?
Does the claim use emotional or sensational language (such as "BREAKING" or extreme claims) to grab attention? Why might this be a warning sign?
Can you find the same information on any official or trusted news source? If not, what does that suggest?
What would be the consequences of sharing this fake claim before checking it? Who could be harmed?
Write a short correction (3–4 sentences) that you could share to help people understand why this claim is false.
Listening and Speaking Activities

The activities below will help you practise thinking about media, news, and communication through speaking and listening. You can do these activities with a partner, in a small group, with a family member at home, or with your AI tutor. Each activity builds a different skill, so try to complete all three over the course of this unit.
Activity 1 — Mock News Broadcast

In this activity, you will prepare and deliver a short news broadcast about an event at your school or in your community. This is an excellent way to practise clear, formal spoken English and to experience what it feels like to be a journalist presenting information to an audience.
How to prepare:
Choose a recent event to report on. It can be real or imaginary.
Write a short script (about 1 minute of speaking time, roughly 120–150 words). Include a greeting, the main news, at least one quote from a person involved, and a closing sentence.
Practise reading your script aloud at least twice. Focus on speaking clearly, at a steady pace, and with confident eye contact.
Deliver your broadcast to your family, classmates, or AI tutor. Record it if possible so you can listen back and improve.
After the broadcast, ask your audience: Was the information clear? Did the broadcaster sound confident and professional?
Activity 2 — Debate: Digital Media Ethics
A debate is a structured discussion where two sides argue for and against a statement. Choose one of the topics below and organise a debate with a partner, a group, or your AI tutor.
Debate topics (choose one):
"Social media does more harm than good for young people."
"Schools should teach media literacy as a compulsory subject."
"People who deliberately spread fake news should face legal consequences."
"Everyone has the right to post whatever they want online, even if it is not true."
Debate rules:
Divide into two teams: For (agrees with the statement) and Against (disagrees).
Each speaker has one to two minutes to present their argument clearly.
Every speaker must use at least one opinion phrase (I think, I believe, In my opinion).
When disagreeing with the other team, begin with a polite acknowledging phrase such as "I see your point, but I think that..."
At the end, the audience votes on which team made the most convincing arguments. Discuss whether anyone changed their mind and why.
Activity 3 — Role-Play Interview

In this activity, one person plays a journalist and the other plays a person who has been involved in a news event. The journalist must ask thoughtful questions, and the interviewee must answer in character. This activity practises both questioning skills and the ability to speak fluently and expressively.
Suggested characters to interview:
Farah from the reading passage, after the journalism club published their article.
The headteacher of Ilyas Academy, explaining what the school has learned from the incident.
A parent who was worried by the fake video and now feels relieved.
Aminah from the story "The Duty of Truth", explaining how she stood up for fairness.
A social media expert explaining how to spot fake news.

The journalist should prepare at least five questions before the interview. After the interview, the journalist should write a short summary (3–4 sentences) using reported speech to describe what the interviewee said. For example: "Farah explained that the journalism club had decided to investigate the rumour because they wanted to find the truth. He added that checking sources was the most important step."
Check Your Learning

You have now worked through the whole of Unit 8. Well done! Before you move on, take a few minutes to read each statement below and think honestly about whether you can do it confidently. Place a tick next to each statement you feel sure about, and a question mark next to any that you would like to practise more. There are no wrong answers here. This is a tool to help you understand your own progress and set goals for improvement.
I can...
Explain what media literacy means and why it is important in my own words.
Identify at least three features of a reliable news source and three features of an unreliable one.
Read a passage about media and news and answer comprehension and analysis questions using evidence from the text.
Change direct speech to reported speech correctly, shifting tense, pronouns, and time words.
Use at least five multi-word verbs related to media and communication in my writing and speech.
Form first conditional sentences to talk about real, likely possibilities.
Form second conditional sentences to talk about imaginary or unlikely situations.
Write a school magazine article with a headline, a lead paragraph answering the five Ws, and at least one quote.
Analyse a piece of fake news and explain specifically why it is unreliable, using evidence.
Prepare and deliver a mock news broadcast clearly and confidently.
Take part in a debate about digital media ethics using opinion phrases and polite disagreement language.
Conduct a role-play interview as either the journalist or the interviewee, using appropriate questions and answers.
Use reported speech to write a summary of what someone said during an interview or conversation.
Reflect on my own learning, identify areas of strength, and set goals for areas where I need more practice.

If you ticked most of these statements, you have a strong understanding of media literacy, reported speech, and journalistic writing. Excellent work! Challenge yourself by starting a real school newspaper or blog, or by analysing a piece of real-world news to see whether it meets the standards of good journalism. If you placed a question mark next to several statements, do not worry. Go back and review the relevant section of this lesson, try the exercises again, or ask your AI tutor for extra practice activities. Remember that learning is a journey, and every step you take brings you closer to becoming a thoughtful, critical, and responsible consumer and creator of information.

🧭  How to Use This Unit
This is your main lesson file. Work through it section by section at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar, and the Story "The Duty of Truth".
Start with the Vocabulary file, then come back here for the Reading, Grammar, Writing, and Speaking sections.
Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck. Bring your finished work to your teacher sessions.
to say something is true without proving it yet
The report alleged that the company had broken safety rules.
to state something is true; a statement said to be true
She claimed that the article contained several errors.
the power to change or affect someone or something
Social media has a huge influence on how young people get their news.
to show or tell what you think or feel
The journalist expressed concern about the spread of misinformation.
to communicate by writing; to match or be similar to
The facts in the article did not correspond with what actually happened.
a person who helps different groups communicate
Aminah was the liaison between the student council and the school board.
a person chosen to represent others; to give a task to someone
Each class sent a delegate to the student press conference.
a change or addition to a rule, law, or document
The editor made an amendment to the headline before publishing.
Checking your sources before publishing is mandatory in good journalism.
There was strong opposition to the proposed social media ban at school.
failing to take proper care, causing harm or problems
The reporter was negligent in not verifying the story before broadcasting it.
to show, point out, or suggest
The evidence indicates that the viral message was deliberately false.

📖  Did You Know?
The word 'journalism' comes from the French word 'journal', meaning
'daily'. Early newspapers were literally daily journals of events.
Today, news travels much faster than once a day. A single social media
post can reach millions of people within minutes, which makes it even
more important to verify information before sharing it.

Explain (say why your evidence supports your point). This structure
helps you write clear, well-supported answers in any subject.

paragraphs: Story — Unit 8: Media, Technology, and News
Year 7 English · Ehel Academy · “The Duty of Truth”
The Duty of Truth

The first day back after the mid-term break started with a murmur in the halls of Bahari Secondary School. Everyone was talking about the new rule that had been posted abruptly on the school notice board: "All Form 3 students must now report for Saturday cleaning duty. Attendance is mandatory."
Aminah stood reading the notice, arms folded. She was the liaison between the student council and the school board — a role that required calm thinking and strong communication.
"They've added a new duty without asking anyone," she muttered. "No explanation. No vote."
"I heard it was the deputy head teacher's idea," said Peter, her classmate. "Apparently he wants to punish us for being 'lazy' during the last clean-up day."
"But that's not true," Aminah said. "Our class actually contributed the most."
During lunch, the student council held an emergency meeting. As they gathered under the neem tree, Aminah pulled out her notebook.
"We need to respond," she said. "Either we accept this quietly, or we challenge it the right way."
Their secretary, Ruth, raised her hand. "Let's write a formal letter requesting an amendment to the rule."
"But who will sign it?" asked Kevin.
"Everyone," said Aminah. "If we want to be taken seriously."
They agreed to organize a petition. Meanwhile, Aminah offered to consult the deputy head teacher for more explanation.
She visited his office that afternoon.
"We've just received complaints from parents," he said. "So we added the duty to teach accountability."
"Sir, the students weren't even informed. The rule appeared overnight."
"The board agreed. It is a necessary policy."
"But it feels more like punishment than learning."
He leaned back. "It's not up for debate."
Back outside, Aminah sighed. She was beginning to realize that being a delegate wasn't just about passing messages — it was about standing firm.
That evening, she and Ruth drafted a letter:

To the School Board: We request an amendment to the newly posted Saturday rule. We feel the decision was made without student input. We suggest an alternate plan where duty is shared across all forms, or rotated biweekly.
They included a list of students who had contributed to the last clean-up. It was clear: Form 3 had not been negligent.
By Friday, over 200 students had signed the petition. It was submitted to the principal's office.
That weekend, instead of cleaning, the students held a quiet assembly in the school field, holding signs that read "Shared Duty is Fair Duty."
The following Monday, a notice appeared: The Saturday duty rule has been paused. A review is underway.
Aminah smiled. "That's progress."
But the story didn't end there.
The school board called for a student representative to attend their monthly meeting. Aminah was chosen.
She entered the conference room where teachers, community leaders, and government officials sat. The atmosphere was formal. She was nervous, but ready.
A senior board member asked, "Miss Aminah, why are students opposed to the cleaning rule?"
She stood and replied, "We are not opposed to cleaning. We are opposed to unfair allocation of tasks. Either all students share the duty, or it becomes a punishment. That is unjust."
Another member raised a paper. "But an anonymous teacher alleges that students in Form 3 have skipped duty before."
"That's a claim," Aminah said. "But we provided logs showing our participation."
The principal cleared his throat. "Actually, the student records indicate that Form 3 cleaned more consecutively than other forms."
A silence followed.
"Well," one board member said, "this is a good example of democratic dialogue."
A proposal was made to delegate cleaning duties in a rotating system. Aminah helped draft the new schedule.
Later that day, she met with her classmates.
"We did it. The rule has been changed."
"You were amazing," said Ruth. "Your voice really influenced them."
Aminah shrugged. "I think the facts did. I just helped present them."
The news spread fast. Students felt more eligible to speak up now. Others who had felt ignored started joining meetings. The school felt more united.
The English teacher, Mr. Gathu, was especially proud.
He said, "Aminah has shown us the power of communication. This is not just about a rule — it's about how rules are made."
As part of an assignment, Aminah wrote a report titled Civic Responsibility in School Spaces. In it, she explained how one small act can lead to big development.
She concluded: "It is important to question rules that are imposed without consulting those they affect. Students are not just followers. They are also citizens."
Meanwhile, another issue came up. A student had been accused of vandalizing a locker. But no one saw it happen.
"He's liable," said one prefect. "He was nearby."
"But that's not proof," Aminah said. "You can't punish someone on what's alleged."
They held a hearing. After testimonies were reviewed, the boy was exonerated. It turned out a loose screw had caused the damage.
The experience left a mark.
"From now on," said Aminah, "let's rely on facts, not fear."

By the end of term, the student council had created a newsletter. They corresponded regularly with the board. More students became industrious, organizing events, tutoring, and contributing to school improvement.
The school even tried a hybrid student-teacher planning group to create future policies.
And as Aminah looked over the minutes of their last meeting, she smiled.
Progress isn't just about big speeches. It's about organizing voices, balancing rights and rules, and believing that structure can serve justice.
Even when it starts with a mop and a notice board.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
What new rule was posted on the school notice board, and why were students upset about it?
What was Aminah's role in the school, and why was it important?
What did the deputy head teacher say was the reason for the new rule? Did Aminah agree with his explanation?
Describe the steps the student council took to challenge the rule. List at least three actions they took.
What evidence did the students provide to show that Form 3 had not been negligent?
How did Aminah respond when a board member said that an anonymous teacher alleged Form 3 students had skipped duty?
What was the final outcome of the board meeting? How were cleaning duties changed?
What happened when a student was accused of vandalizing a locker? How was the situation resolved fairly?
What did Aminah mean when she said, "Let's rely on facts, not fear"?
How did the school change by the end of term? Give at least two examples from the story.
Think and Discuss
Aminah says that students are "not just followers" but "also citizens." What does she mean by this? Do you agree?
The story shows that one person's courage can lead to change. Can you think of an example from your own life or community where someone stood up for what was right?
How does this story relate to the Islamic principle of standing for justice? Think about the verse: "O you who believe, stand firmly for justice" (Surah An-Nisa 4:135).
If you were in Aminah's position, what would you have done differently, and why?
Find the Words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 8 vocabulary words and write the sentence they appear in. Then try using each word in a new sentence of your own:
liaison
mandatory
amendment
consult
contribute
negligent
delegate
allege
indicate
consecutive
eligible
exonerated
industrious
hybrid
correspond
influence
development
allocate
abruptly
opposition
Writing Extension

Imagine you are a journalist reporting on the events at Bahari Secondary School. Write a short news article (150–200 words) about the student petition and its outcome. Include a headline, a lead paragraph, and at least one quote in reported speech.
Write a diary entry from Aminah's point of view on the evening after the board meeting. How does she feel? What did she learn? Aim for 100–150 words.
The story says, "Progress is not just about big speeches. It is about organizing voices." Write a short paragraph explaining what this means to you and how it connects to your own life.

tables: To show or tell what you think or feel.
I want to express my ideas in the school magazine.
To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours.
She will claim her prize after winning the contest.
To say that something is true, but not prove it yet.
The teacher alleges that the student cheated on the test.
To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something.
The colours of the flags correspond with the team colours.
A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand.
The teacher gave a clear explanation of the problem.
The power to change or affect someone or something.
Her kindness had a positive influence on her friends.
A person who helps different groups communicate and work together.
The teacher acted as a liaison between the students and the principal.
To persuade someone to believe or do something.
She convinced her classmates to join the journalism club.

A change or addition to a law or document.

Something that is required or must be done.

Likely to do something or to be affected by something.

Having the right to do or receive something.

To be declared not guilty of a crime or blame.

To give something, like help or ideas, to a common purpose.

To give something to someone for a specific purpose.

To arrange things in a neat and orderly way.

Not taking care of something you should, causing problems.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar mini-lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! All examples use the digital technology and innovation theme of Unit 9.
Lesson 1:  Present Perfect + Just
What it means and why it matters

The present perfect tense is used to talk about actions that have happened at an unspecified time before now. When we add just, it means the action happened very recently. This is important in the digital world because technology changes fast — we often need to share updates and news that feel immediate and fresh. For example, if you say, “She has just uploaded the code,” it tells us the upload happened only a moment ago.
How to form it
Follow these steps:
Identify the subject: the person or thing doing the action (e.g., She, They, I).
Use has or have: has for singular (he, she, it) and have for plural (they, we) and with I and you.
Add the past participle: the third form of the verb (e.g., uploaded, finished, built).
Place just before the past participle: to show the action happened recently.
Structure:
Singular: Subject + has + just + past participle — “She has just uploaded the code.”
Plural: Subject + have + just + past participle — “They have just finished the app.”
Variations and exceptions
Just always goes before the past participle, not after it.
We do not use just with specific time references like yesterday or last week. Say “She has just finished” (not “She has just finished yesterday”).
In informal speech, some people use the past simple instead: “She just finished.” In academic and formal writing, the present perfect form is preferred.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “She has uploaded just the app.” ✗  (just in the wrong place)
Fix: “She has just uploaded the app.” ✓
Mistake: “She just recognised the error.” ✗  (missing has/have)
Fix: “She has just recognised the error.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of just as a tiny time marker that means “moments ago.” Remember: “Just happened, just in time!” The event is fresh like a notification that has just appeared on your phone screen.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence using the present perfect + just:
They ______ (just / finish) building the prototype.
I ______ (just / download) the new coding software.
He ______ (just / arrive) at the robotics workshop.
We ______ (just / receive) the sensor data from the farm.
She ______ (just / complete) her digital detox diary.
Lesson 2:  Connectives
What it means and why it matters

Connectives are words that link ideas together. They help us explain reasons, show results, make contrasts, and express purposes. When we discuss technology, connectives help us build clear, logical arguments — for example, explaining why an app works or what happens when we use too much screen time.
Key connectives for this unit:
because — to give a reason.
so that — to show purpose.
although — to show contrast.
therefore — to show a result.
How to use them
because: “The farmers use the app because it sends weather alerts.”
so that: “She coded the programme so that it would run on low-cost phones.”
although: “Although the internet was slow, the students managed to submit their project.”
therefore: “The sensor detected dry soil; therefore, the app sent a watering alert.”
Variations
Connectives can start a sentence: “Although it was a rudimentary prototype, it worked well.”
So that can be used with future tense: “I will practise coding every day so that I improve.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “The server crashed the students lost their data.” ✗  (missing connective)
Fix: “The server crashed; therefore, the students lost their data.” ✓
Do not swap because and so that. Use because for a reason, so that for a purpose.
Memory tip
Think: “Connectives Connect!” They are the glue that holds your digital-world arguments together.
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with because, so that, although, or therefore:
I backed up my files ______ I would not lose them.
______ the battery was low, the drone still completed its scan.
She shared her code online ______ other students could learn from it.
The app crashed ______ there was a bug in the latest update.
The internet was slow; ______, the download took much longer than expected.
Lesson 3:  Either / Neither / Both
What it means and why it matters

Understanding how to use either, neither, and both helps you express choices, agreements, and disagreements clearly. In the tech world, you often need to compare two options — two apps, two devices, or two solutions — and these words help you do that precisely.
How to use them
Either — one of two options: “You can use either the laptop or the tablet.”
Neither — not one and not the other: “Neither app is working properly.”
Both — the two together: “Both apps have been updated.”
Important grammar rules
Either takes a singular noun and verb: “Either device is suitable.”
Neither also takes a singular verb: “Neither option is available.”
Both takes a plural noun and verb: “Both systems are connected.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “Neither options are good.” ✗  (plural verb with neither)
Fix: “Neither option is good.” ✓
Mistake: “Both app are updated.” ✗  (singular noun with both)
Fix: “Both apps are updated.” ✓
Memory tip
Either = Election (choose one). Neither = Not one (none). Both = Both together (two).
Practice 3
Fill in with either, neither, or both:
You can connect to ______ the Wi-Fi or the mobile data. (one of two)
______ of the chargers is working. We need a new one. (not one, not the other)
______ students passed the coding test. Well done! (the two together)
I have tried ______ browsers, but ______ loads the page correctly. (two / not one)
Lesson 4:  Past Continuous
What it means and why it matters

The past continuous describes actions that were happening at a specific moment in the past. It helps us set the scene in stories and describe background actions. For example: “While the students were coding, the teacher was checking the server.” It shows two actions happening at the same time.
How to form it
Subject + was/were + verb(-ing)
I / he / she / it → was: “She was testing the app.”
You / we / they → were: “They were building the drone.”
Negative: add not → “They were not (weren’t) paying attention to the screen.”
Question: swap subject and was/were → “Were they coding all afternoon?”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “They was building the drone.” ✗  (wrong form of to be)
Fix: “They were building the drone.” ✓
Mistake: “She was test the app.” ✗  (missing -ing)
Fix: “She was testing the app.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the past continuous as a “video playing in the background.” The action was ongoing, like a live stream you can picture in your mind.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence using the past continuous:
Yesterday at 4 PM, I ______ (research) future farming techniques.
While she ______ (present) her project, the Wi-Fi disconnected.
They ______ (not listen) when the teacher explained the coding task.
______ he ______ (use) the drone when it ran out of battery?
The students ______ (converge) in the lab when the alarm sounded.
Lesson 5:  Complex Noun Phrases
What it means and why it matters

A complex noun phrase is a group of words that work together to describe a person, place, thing, or idea in more detail. In the digital world, we use complex noun phrases to describe inventions, apps, and devices precisely. Instead of saying “a system,” we can say “a solar-powered, automated irrigation system,” which gives a much clearer picture.
How to build one
Start with the noun: the main word you want to describe (e.g., system, device, app).
Add adjectives before it: opinion, size, age, colour, material (e.g., “a small, lightweight sensor”).
Use compound modifiers (hyphenated): “a solar-powered charger”, “a user-friendly platform”.
Add phrases after the noun: prepositional phrases or relative clauses (e.g., “the app that monitors soil moisture”).
Examples from this unit:
“an obscure but intriguing wireless experiment” — two adjectives + noun.
“a rudimentary, student-built weather-monitoring application” — adjective + compound modifier + compound noun.
“the high-resolution agricultural drone that photographed the fields” — adjective + adjective + noun + relative clause.
Common mistakes
Mistake: forgetting hyphens in compound modifiers: “a solar powered charger.” ✗
Fix: “a solar-powered charger.” ✓
Mistake: putting too many adjectives without commas: “a small lightweight waterproof sensor.” ✗
Fix: “a small, lightweight, waterproof sensor.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of complex noun phrases as “super nouns.” The noun is the core, and modifiers are the upgrades — like adding features to an app. The more detail you add, the clearer your description becomes!
Practice 5
Build a complex noun phrase from the parts given, then use it in a sentence:
Noun: drone. Modifiers: small, solar-powered, lightweight.
Noun: platform. Modifiers: free, open-source, multilingual, coding.
Noun: sensor. Modifiers: the, waterproof, that measures soil moisture.
Lesson 6:  Adjective + Infinitive
What it means and why it matters

The adjective + infinitive structure uses an adjective followed by to + a base verb. We use it to express feelings, opinions, and states of being. In the digital world, this structure helps us describe reactions to technology: “It is difficult to determine the truth online” or “She was eager to acquire new coding skills.”
How to form it
Choose an adjective: easy, difficult, happy, eager, important, safe, dangerous, exciting.
Add to: after the adjective.
Use the infinitive (base verb): the plain form of the verb.
Examples:
“It is difficult to omit important data from a report.”
“She was eager to acquire the latest coding skills.”
“It is important to recognise misinformation online.”
“The app is easy to use even for beginners.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “It is difficult omitting a key detail.” ✗  (gerund instead of infinitive)
Fix: “It is difficult to omit a key detail.” ✓
Mistake: “She was eager acquire new skills.” ✗  (missing to)
Fix: “She was eager to acquire new skills.” ✓
Memory tip
Think: “Adjectives love to describe!” The adjective tells us how we feel, and the infinitive tells us about what.
Practice 6
Complete each sentence with the correct adjective + infinitive:
It is ______ (important) ______ (check) your sources before sharing news online.
The students were ______ (excited) ______ (present) their app at the science fair.
It is ______ (dangerous) ______ (share) personal information on social media.
He was ______ (happy) ______ (reveal) the results of his experiment.
Lesson 7:  Verb + -ing Form
What it means and why it matters

The -ing form of a verb (the gerund) is created by adding -ing to the base verb. We use it after certain verbs (enjoy, avoid, consider, keep, suggest, practise) and after prepositions. In the digital world, we use the -ing form often: “I enjoy coding,” “She avoided revealing the password,” “He kept jostling for a seat at the computer.”
How to form it
Take the base verb: e.g., code, reveal, jostle.
Add -ing: coding, revealing, jostling.
Special rules: drop a final -e (make → making), double the last consonant after a short vowel (sit → sitting).
Use the -ing form:
After certain verbs: “They avoided revealing too much information.”
“She kept jostling for access to the computer.”
As the subject of a sentence: “Coding is a useful skill for the future.”
After prepositions: “He is good at building circuits.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “I enjoy to code.” ✗  (infinitive after enjoy)
Fix: “I enjoy coding.” ✓
Mistake: “She avoided to reveal the answer.” ✗
Fix: “She avoided revealing the answer.” ✓
Memory tip
After enjoy, avoid, consider, keep, suggest, practise, always use the -ing form. Think: “I enjoy doing!” not “I enjoy to do!”
Practice 7
Complete each sentence with the correct -ing form of the verb in brackets:
They enjoy ______ (build) robots in their after-school club.
She is good at ______ (solve) coding problems quickly.
We talked about ______ (create) a school tech blog.
He avoided ______ (share) unverified information online.
______ (Learn) to code takes patience and practice.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Perfect + Just)
1. have just finished   2. have just downloaded   3. has just arrived   4. have just received   5. has just completed
Practice 2 (Connectives)
1. so that   2. Although   3. so that   4. because   5. therefore
Practice 3 (Either / Neither / Both)
1. either   2. Neither   3. Both   4. both … neither
Practice 4 (Past Continuous)
1. was researching   2. was presenting   3. were not listening   4. Was … using   5. were converging
Practice 5 (Complex Noun Phrases)
1. a small, lightweight, solar-powered drone — “The team tested a small, lightweight, solar-powered drone over the field.”
2. a free, open-source, multilingual coding platform — “Students used a free, open-source, multilingual coding platform to build their app.”
3. the waterproof sensor that measures soil moisture — “The waterproof sensor that measures soil moisture saved the crops.”
Practice 6 (Adjective + Infinitive)
1. important to check   2. excited to present   3. dangerous to share   4. happy to reveal
Practice 7 (Verb + -ing)
1. building   2. solving   3. creating   4. sharing   5. Learning
