💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers?
Can you explain why we say has just and not have just with she?
Can you give me five more sentences to practise any of these grammar rules?

paragraphs: Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation — the final unit of Year 7! In this unit you will explore how digital technology, robotics, and innovative farming are reshaping daily life across East Africa and the wider world. You will read about young people who use smartphones, coding skills, and creative thinking to solve real problems in their communities. Through reading, writing, speaking, and grammar activities, you will sharpen your English skills while thinking critically about the role of technology in our lives.

This unit brings together everything you have learned this year. You will use reported speech to relay what others have said about technology, conditionals (if/unless) to discuss possible outcomes, and complex noun phrases to describe inventions precisely. You will also practise debating, diary writing, and report writing — all essential skills for Cambridge Lower Secondary English.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a passage about digital innovation and discuss its ideas confidently in English.
Use reported speech to relay what someone has said: “She said that the app had just been updated.”
Form conditional sentences with if and unless: “If we use technology wisely, it will help us.”
Build complex noun phrases to describe tech inventions precisely: “a solar-powered automated irrigation system.”
Write a structured debate argument, a digital detox diary, or a future farming report.
Present your ideas about technology clearly in a spoken discussion or debate.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently in speech and writing.
Think critically about technology addiction, misinformation, and sustainability.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the seven lessons in the Grammar file (Present Perfect + Just, Connectives, Either/Neither/Both, Past Continuous, Complex Noun Phrases, Adjective + Infinitive, Verb + -ing).
Read the Story file, “Shadows in Mogadishu”, and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary

Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
Digital Technology and Innovation
conceive — to think of or create an idea or plan in your mind.
acquire — to get or gain something, usually by effort or learning.
introduce — to make something known for the first time; to bring something new.
reveal — to show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
determine — to find out or decide something after thinking about it.
versatile — able to do many different things or adapt to many situations.
rudimentary — basic or simple; not fully developed.
Behaviour and Society
agitate — to make someone feel worried or upset.
ostracise — to exclude someone from a group or society.
bamboozle — to trick or confuse someone.
demeanor — the way a person behaves or shows their feelings.
eccentric — strange or unusual in a way that is interesting or different.
blunt — speaking in a direct, honest way without trying to be polite.
fugitive — a person who is running away or trying to escape.
Description and Analysis
compare — to look at two or more things to see how they are similar or different.
converge — to come together from different directions to meet at one point.
obscure — not well known or hard to understand.
omit — to leave out or not include something.
adequate — good enough for what is needed; sufficient.
lapse — a small mistake or failure that happens because you forget something.
intrigue — a secret plan or a mysterious event that makes people curious.
Reading: Smart Farms and Digital Dreams
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions and discuss the ideas.

In recent years, digital technology has begun to reveal new possibilities for communities across East Africa. From Hargeisa to Nairobi, young innovators are finding versatile ways to use smartphones, sensors, and coding to solve problems that once seemed impossible. One of the most exciting areas of change is future farming — using technology to grow more food with fewer resources.

In a small town outside Mogadishu, a group of secondary-school students conceived a plan to help local farmers. They had noticed that many families were struggling because rainfall had become unpredictable. Crops failed, and food prices grew worse every season. The students decided to acquire basic coding skills and build a simple weather-monitoring app. Their teacher, Mr. Guled, introduced them to rudimentary electronics — small sensors that could measure temperature, humidity, and soil moisture.

At first, some adults were sceptical. A local shopkeeper said that students were too young to build anything useful. He told them that technology would not replace hard work. But the students were not agitated by the criticism. They determined that if they worked together, they could prove the doubters wrong. Unless they tried, they would never know what was possible.

Within three months, the team had built a working prototype. The app collected data from small sensors placed in the soil and sent alerts to farmers’ phones. If the soil was too dry, the app told the farmer to water the crops. Unless the temperature dropped below a safe level, the system stayed quiet. One farmer, Halimo, reported that she had just saved an entire field of maize thanks to an early warning from the app.

Word about the project spread quickly. People from neighbouring villages converged on the school to see demonstrations. A journalist from a Mogadishu radio station interviewed the students. She asked them how they had learned to code. Amina, the team leader, replied that they had taught themselves using free online tutorials. She said that anyone with a phone and internet access could learn the same skills.

The project also introduced a drone that could photograph fields from above. By comparing images taken at different times, the students could spot which parts of a field were healthy and which were struggling. This kind of analysis, once obscure and expensive, was now adequate for a school team with basic equipment.

Mr. Guled told a visiting teacher that the students had just completed their most ambitious test. He reported that if the government supported similar programmes, thousands of farms could benefit. He said that unless young people were given opportunities to innovate, the country would fall behind.

Today, the students’ app is used by over two hundred farmers in three districts. The team has not stopped innovating. They are now working on a solar-powered irrigation system — a complex, automated, solar-powered water-delivery device — that could change farming in the region for ever. As Amina says, “Technology is not magic. It is just people solving problems, step by step.”
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What problem did the students notice that local farmers were facing?
How did Mr. Guled help the students get started with their project?
What did the sceptical shopkeeper say? Write his words using reported speech.
How does the weather-monitoring app work? Explain the process in three steps.
What conditional sentence (if/unless) can you find in the passage? Write it out and explain what it means.
How did the drone help the students analyse the farmland?
What did Halimo report about the app? Why was this important?
Find the complex noun phrase used to describe the irrigation system. What does each word in the phrase tell us?
The passage says, “Technology is not magic. It is just people solving problems, step by step.” What does Amina mean by this?
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
The shopkeeper said students were too young to build anything useful. Do you agree or disagree? Explain your reasons using evidence from the passage.
The passage describes technology helping farmers. Can you think of another area of daily life where technology could help your community?
Mr. Guled said that unless young people were given opportunities to innovate, the country would fall behind. What does this mean for education in your country?
Do you think it is a good thing that the app sends alerts to farmers’ phones? Could there be any disadvantages?
Compare the students in this passage to Sahra in “Shadows in Mogadishu.” How are they similar?
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
conceived
versatile
rudimentary
converged
obscure
adequate
Grammar Focus: Reported Speech
This section introduces a key grammar topic for Unit 9. You will practise it further in the Grammar Practice section, but here is a summary to get you started.
What is reported speech?
Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is when we tell someone what another person said, without using their exact words. We change the sentence to fit into our own report of the conversation.
Look at the difference:
Direct speech: Amina said, “I have built an app.”
Reported speech: Amina said that she had built an app.
How to form reported speech
When we change direct speech to reported speech, we usually make these changes:
Remove the quotation marks and add that (optional but helpful).
Change the pronoun: I → she/he, we → they, my → her/his.
Move the tense back one step: present simple → past simple, present perfect → past perfect, will → would, can → could.
Examples from the passage:
Direct: The shopkeeper said, “Students are too young.” → Reported: The shopkeeper said that students were too young.
Direct: Amina said, “We have taught ourselves.” → Reported: Amina said that they had taught themselves.
Direct: Mr. Guled said, “The students will succeed.” → Reported: Mr. Guled said that the students would succeed.
Reported questions
When we report a question, we change the word order so it is no longer a question:
Direct: She asked, “How did you learn to code?” → Reported: She asked how they had learned to code.
Direct: He asked, “Do you use sensors?” → Reported: He asked whether/if they used sensors.
Notice: in reported questions, we do not use a question mark at the end.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “She said that she has built an app.” ✗  (tense not shifted)
Fix: “She said that she had built an app.” ✓
Mistake: “He asked did they use sensors.” ✗  (question word order kept)
Fix: “He asked whether they used sensors.” ✓
Quick Practice
Change these direct speech sentences to reported speech:
Halimo said, “The app has just saved my crops.”
The journalist asked, “How did you build the prototype?”
Mr. Guled said, “We will test the drone next week.”
A farmer asked, “Can the app work without internet?”
Amina said, “I am learning to code every day.”

Answers: 1. Halimo said that the app had just saved her crops.  2. The journalist asked how they had built the prototype.  3. Mr. Guled said that they would test the drone the following week.  4. A farmer asked whether/if the app could work without internet.  5. Amina said that she was learning to code every day.
Grammar Focus: Conditionals (if / unless)
What are conditional sentences?

A conditional sentence talks about a possible situation and its result. We use if to say what might happen, and unless to say what will happen if something does not occur. Conditionals are very useful when discussing technology and its effects.
First conditional (likely future)
Use the first conditional to talk about things that are likely to happen in the future:
If + present simple, will + base verb.
“If we use technology wisely, it will help our community.”
“If the soil is too dry, the app will send an alert.”
“Farming will improve if the government supports innovation.”
Using unless
Unless means “if not”. It introduces the negative condition:
“Unless the temperature drops, the crops will survive.” (= If the temperature does not drop, the crops will survive.)
“You will not learn to code unless you practise every day.”
“Unless we reduce screen time, our eyes will get worse.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “If we will use technology wisely, it will help us.” ✗  (will in the if-clause)
Fix: “If we use technology wisely, it will help us.” ✓  (present simple after if)
Mistake: “Unless we don’t reduce screen time...” ✗  (double negative)
Fix: “Unless we reduce screen time...” ✓  (unless already means if not)
Quick Practice
Complete each sentence with the correct verb form:
If farmers ______ (use) the app, they will save water.
Unless the students ______ (work) together, the project will fail.
The drone will take off if the battery ______ (be) fully charged.
Unless you ______ (check) your sources, you might share misinformation.
If she ______ (learn) to code, she will build her own app.
Answers: 1. use   2. work   3. is   4. check   5. learns
Grammar Focus: Complex Noun Phrases
What is a complex noun phrase?

A complex noun phrase is a group of words built around a noun that gives extra detail. In the reading passage, you saw the phrase: “a complex, automated, solar-powered water-delivery device.” This single phrase tells us the size, type, power source, and purpose of the invention — all before the main noun.
How to build a complex noun phrase
Start with the main noun, then add modifiers before and after it:
Adjectives before the noun: “a fast, reliable connection”
Compound modifiers (hyphenated): “a solar-powered irrigation system”
Prepositional phrases after the noun: “the sensor in the field”
Relative clauses after the noun: “the app that monitors soil moisture”
Technology descriptions often use several modifiers together:
“a small, lightweight, waterproof sensor” — three adjectives before the noun.
“a community-funded, open-source farming application” — two compound modifiers.
“the rudimentary weather-monitoring device that the students built” — adjective + compound modifier + relative clause.
Common mistakes
Mistake: piling up too many adjectives without commas or hyphens: “a solar powered automated water delivery device.” ✗
Fix: use commas between coordinate adjectives and hyphens in compound modifiers: “a solar-powered, automated water-delivery device.” ✓
Quick Practice
Expand each simple noun into a complex noun phrase by adding at least two modifiers:
a phone → ______
a drone → ______
a system → ______
an app → ______

Example answers: 1. a lightweight, high-speed smartphone with a powerful camera  2. a small, solar-powered agricultural drone  3. an automated, sensor-based irrigation system  4. a free, user-friendly weather-monitoring app that farmers can download
Grammar Practice
These exercises bring together reported speech, conditionals, and complex noun phrases. Complete them in your notebook, then check your answers at the bottom or ask your AI tutor.
Exercise A: Reported Speech in a Digital World
Change each sentence from direct speech to reported speech:
The teacher said, “The students have just finished the coding project.”
Amina told the journalist, “I will present our app at the science fair.”
A parent asked, “Is the app safe for children to use?”
The engineer said, “We can build a better drone next year.”
Yusuf asked his classmate, “Where did you learn to programme?”
Halimo said, “I am using the app every day on my farm.”

Answers: 1. The teacher said that the students had just finished the coding project.  2. Amina told the journalist that she would present their app at the science fair.  3. A parent asked whether/if the app was safe for children to use.  4. The engineer said that they could build a better drone the following year.  5. Yusuf asked his classmate where he/she had learned to programme.  6. Halimo said that she was using the app every day on her farm.
Exercise B: Conditionals about Technology
Complete each conditional sentence with the correct form of the verb:
If we ______ (not limit) our screen time, our health will suffer.
Unless the internet connection ______ (improve), online learning will be difficult.
Farmers will lose their crops if the warning system ______ (fail).
Unless schools ______ (teach) coding, students will miss important skills.
If artificial intelligence ______ (continue) to develop, many jobs will change.
You will not spot misinformation unless you ______ (check) multiple sources.
Answers: 1. do not limit   2. improves   3. fails   4. teach   5. continues   6. check
Exercise C: Build Complex Noun Phrases
Combine the words in brackets into one complex noun phrase, then use it in a sentence:
(small / portable / solar-powered) + charger
(innovative / student-designed / weather-monitoring) + application
(high-resolution / lightweight / agricultural) + drone
(free / open-source / multilingual) + coding platform

Example answers: 1. a small, portable, solar-powered charger — “Every student should carry a small, portable, solar-powered charger in their bag.”  2. an innovative, student-designed, weather-monitoring application  3. a high-resolution, lightweight agricultural drone  4. a free, open-source, multilingual coding platform
Exercise D: Mixed Grammar — Write Your Own
Write six sentences about technology in your community or school. Each sentence must include:
Two sentences using reported speech (e.g., “My teacher said that we would learn to code this year.”).
Two sentences using a conditional with if or unless (e.g., “If we have internet access, we will be able to research online.”).
Two sentences containing a complex noun phrase (e.g., “Our school has a new, high-speed wireless internet connection.”).
Writing Workshop

In this section, you will choose one of three writing projects. Each project uses the vocabulary, grammar, and ideas from this unit. The goal is to practise extended writing while thinking critically about the digital world.
Option A: Debate — Is Technology Helping or Harming Young People?
Write a structured debate argument (about 250 words) arguing for or against this statement: “Technology is doing more harm than good to young people.”
Your argument should include:
An introduction stating your position clearly.
Three main points with evidence or examples. Use conditionals: “If young people spend too much time on screens, they will...”
A counter-argument acknowledging the other side: “Some people say that... However,...” Use reported speech here.
A conclusion summarising your view.

Example opening: “I believe that technology is helping young people more than it is harming them. If students have access to online learning platforms, they will be able to study subjects that their schools do not offer.”
Option B: Digital Detox Diary

Imagine you spend one full week without any digital devices — no phone, no tablet, no computer, no television. Write a diary covering three days of that week (about 250 words total). For each day, describe:
What you did instead of using technology.
How you felt (use adjectives and complex noun phrases to describe your emotions).
What someone said to you about your experiment (use reported speech): “My mother told me that she was proud of me.”
What you learned about your relationship with technology.
Option C: Future Farming Report
Write a short report (about 250 words) describing how technology could improve farming in your region over the next ten years. Your report should include:
An introduction explaining the current challenges farmers face.
Two or three technological solutions (e.g., drones, smart sensors, solar-powered irrigation). Describe each using a complex noun phrase.
Conditional predictions: “If farmers adopt these technologies, yields will...” / “Unless investment increases, farmers will...”
A quote from an expert (you may invent one) reported in indirect speech: “Dr. Abdi said that smart farming would transform East Africa.”
A conclusion with your personal opinion on the future of farming.
Editing Checklist
Before you submit your writing, check these points:
Have I used at least two examples of reported speech?   ☐
Have I included at least one conditional sentence with if or unless?   ☐
Have I used at least one complex noun phrase to describe a technology?   ☐
Have I used at least five vocabulary words from this unit?   ☐
Does every sentence start with a capital letter and end with punctuation?   ☐
Have I read my work out loud to check that it sounds natural?   ☐
Listening & Speaking
Listening: A Radio Interview about Technology in Schools
Ask your AI tutor to read this interview aloud while you listen. Then read it yourself.
Interviewer: Tell us about the coding club at your school.
Farhan: We started the club last term with just eight students. Now everyone wants to join. We have just learned how to build simple apps using free software.
Interviewer: What have you built so far?

Farhan: Our first project was a rudimentary quiz app for younger students. It asks questions about maths and science, and it gives hints if you get stuck. We also built a small weather station using a versatile sensor kit our teacher ordered online.
Interviewer: Has anyone been sceptical about the club?

Farhan: At first, some parents said that coding was a waste of time. They told us that we should focus on traditional subjects. But when they saw what we had conceived and built, they changed their minds. My father said that he was proud of what we had acquired.
Interviewer: What advice would you give to students who want to start a similar club?
Farhan: Start with what you have. Unless you try, you will never know what you can build. If you have a phone and internet access, you already have adequate resources to begin.
After listening, answer these questions:
How many students were in the coding club when it started?
What was the club’s first project? Describe it in one sentence.
What did some parents say about the coding club at first? Write their words in reported speech.
Find two conditional sentences in the interview and write them out.
What vocabulary words from this unit does Farhan use? List at least three.
Speaking 1: Present Your Ideas about Technology
Prepare a short spoken presentation (about two minutes) on this topic: “How one piece of technology has changed my daily life.” Follow this structure:
Introduction: Name the technology and say when you first used it.
Description: Use a complex noun phrase to describe it: “It is a lightweight, portable, internet-connected device.”
Impact: Explain how it changed your routine. Use conditionals: “If I did not have it, I would...”
Other views: Report what someone else thinks: “My grandmother said that life was simpler before phones.”
Conclusion: Give your opinion on whether the change has been positive or negative.
Record yourself and listen back. Check that you spoke clearly and used grammar from this unit.
Speaking 2: Debate — Should Schools Ban Mobile Phones?
In pairs or with your AI tutor, debate this question: Should mobile phones be banned in schools?
One person argues for the ban and the other argues against. Use evidence, conditionals, and reported speech to support your arguments:
For the ban: “If phones are allowed in class, students will be distracted.” / “Teachers have said that phones disrupt learning.”
Against the ban: “Unless students learn to use phones responsibly, they will not be prepared for the digital world.” / “Researchers have reported that phones can support learning.”
After the debate, write one paragraph explaining which side you agree with and why.
Speaking Game: Report What They Said
Play this game with your AI tutor or a classmate:
One person says a sentence in direct speech about technology (e.g., “I love using my tablet for drawing.”).
The other person must report it correctly: “She said that she loved using her tablet for drawing.”
Take turns. Score one point for each correct reported speech sentence. First to ten points wins!
Check Your Learning

You have now completed Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation. This is the final unit of Year 7 — well done for making it this far! Use the checklist below to see what you have learned and what you might want to review.
Self-Assessment
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can read and understand a passage about digital innovation and future farming.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use reported speech to relay what someone has said.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can form conditional sentences with if and unless.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can build complex noun phrases to describe technology precisely.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a debate argument, a digital detox diary, or a farming report.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can discuss technology topics clearly in spoken English.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I know at least fifteen vocabulary words from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can think critically about technology addiction and misinformation.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Reflection
In your notebook, write a short paragraph (three to five sentences) answering this question:
What is the most important thing you learned in Unit 9, and how will you use it outside of school?
End-of-Year Celebration

Congratulations! You have completed all nine units of Year 7 English. Look back at how far you have come since Unit 1. You have learned to read complex texts, write structured arguments, debate ideas, and express yourself confidently in English.
Here are some things to be proud of:
You can discuss complex topics like identity, media, careers, sports, and now the digital world.
You know hundreds of English vocabulary words and can use them accurately in context.
You can use grammar confidently: reported speech, conditionals, passive voice, complex noun phrases, and more.
You have read and understood nine stories set in East Africa and beyond.
You have become a more confident reader, writer, speaker, and critical thinker in English.
Keep reading, keep writing, keep speaking. English is a skill that grows stronger with daily practice. Ask your AI tutor: “What should I do over the holidays to keep my English strong?”
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don’t understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to my presentation and give me feedback?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more questions about reported speech?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Well done — you have completed the final unit of Year 7! Be proud of every word you have learned, every sentence you have written, and every idea you have shared. The digital world is yours to shape!

You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (Present Perfect + Just, Connectives, and more), and the Story “Shadows in Mogadishu”.
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions.

✍️  Writing rules to remember

Can you play the Report What They Said game with me?

🕑  About 30–45 minutes a day
Morning: review your vocabulary words and say one sentence using reported speech.
Afternoon: do one grammar exercise OR one writing activity from this file.
Evening: read a section of the story and practise speaking with your AI tutor.
Twice a week: bring your recordings and writing to your teacher session.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation
Year 7 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "Shadows in Mogadishu"
Shadows in Mogadishu

The compound walls were cream-coloured, edged with modern vines. From the rooftop of Sahra's apartment building, the skyline of Mogadishu looked sharp and full of life — construction cranes beside old domes, taxis jostling through roundabouts, teenagers laughing as they left tutoring centres.
Sahra leaned astride the bench's armrest, sketchpad on her lap. She had just finished a rough drawing of the sunset when her younger brother barged in.
"Did you hear about the boy from Hamar Weyne?" Yasin asked.
"No," Sahra replied, barely looking up.
"He was ostracised from school. They say he was caught hacking the computer lab!"
Sahra blinked. "Hacking? Like, code or stealing?"
"Both, maybe. No one knows. It's all over X (the app formerly known as Twitter)."
She sighed. Mogadishu buzzed with digital gossip. But Sahra, age fifteen, preferred fact over fiction.
At Ocean Breeze International School, news spread faster than light. The next morning, Sahra overheard classmates comparing the boy's story.
"I heard he's a fugitive, hiding somewhere near KM4."
"No, no," said another, "he's in custody. His uncle revealed it on Facebook Live."
"People just want to bamboozle each other with drama," Sahra muttered.
During lunch, she sat with her best friend, Amal, who was versatile in everything from coding to poetry.
"We don't even know the guy," Amal said. "Why is everyone so quick to judge?"
"I want to know what really happened," Sahra said. "Not what TikTok says."
Later, she spoke with her tech club mentor, Mr. Hassan, a patient, blunt man who ran a small robotics lab on the school's second floor.
"You want the real story?" he said. "Then do what smart people do — acquire real information."
"How?"
"Start asking. But gently. With care."
Sahra began by reaching out to Ladan, a quiet girl who had been close to the accused student.
"His name's Bilal," Ladan said. "He's... different. Eccentric, maybe. Always coding, building circuits from old radios. But not dangerous."
"Then why was he blamed?"
"There was a lapse in the exam server last week. People panicked. Teachers needed someone to blame."
Sahra listened, her mind forming connections.
"What was his demeanour like that day?" she asked.
"Calm. Maybe too calm."
Bilal's silence had become a blank canvas for gossip. Some said he had confessed. Others claimed he had vanished.
That evening, Sahra couldn't stop thinking. Her mind was conceiving a plan: an article for the school blog.
She spent the night comparing rumours, tracing message threads, and speaking with classmates who had known Bilal.

She even visited the dusty IT server room the next morning, asking for archived network logs. The technician, surprised but curious, let her observe. There, among the logs, was a timestamp proving Bilal had emailed a teacher minutes before the system crash. The content described a wireless experiment — not a hack.
By Friday, she had just completed a rough draft: "Misjudged in the Digital Age."
In it, she presented evidence Bilal had been working on a separate wireless project — one that accidentally overloaded the lab's router. He wasn't a hacker. Just a curious kid caught in a tech lapse.
She explained how bias grows in silence. She used phrases like "a lull in truth" and "an obscure, misunderstood project." Her language was careful — not to excuse, but to question.
The blog post was uploaded Saturday morning. It caused instant discussion.
In the school library, students were converging at the computers.
"Have you read it?"
"Yeah. I didn't know Bilal had actually shared his plans with the computer teacher before!"
"Neither did I. The post says the logs prove it."
Bilal hadn't been a hacker. He had been misunderstood. Even the head of IT commented, saying the article helped ease tension.
By Monday, Bilal's name was cleared. He was exonerated by school officials and invited back.
Still, the damage had changed him. Sahra saw him during robotics club — quieter, more cautious.
"You're back," she said.
He nodded. "It's weird. Like walking through fog."
"I'm glad you're here. I almost didn't write that article," Sahra confessed.
"It helped," Bilal replied. "Not just me. People are listening more carefully now."
Later, Mr. Hassan congratulated Sahra. "It's not easy to speak against the crowd. It takes guts."
"It was difficult to determine what to omit," Sahra said. "Too much, and I'd agitate people. Too little, and I'd hide the point."
He nodded. "But you chose well. That's journalism."
Weeks later, Sahra was selected to present at the Somali Young Writers' Forum. She prepared a multimedia talk titled "Perception vs. Reality: A Student's Dilemma."

During her presentation, she used a rudimentary animation to show how rumours spread. She included screenshots, student quotes, and even a short comparison between how Bilal had been treated versus another student who had admitted to plagiarism months earlier.
After her talk, a boy from another school approached her.
"I've just started a youth podcast. Would you consider guesting?"
Sahra smiled. "Let me check with my parents."
"Of course."
In the following weeks, her episode aired. She spoke about the need to recognise misinformation and to give people space to explain.
One listener messaged: "Your story helped me reconnect with a cousin I'd ostracised. I realised I never asked him for his side."
And another wrote, "My sister was wrongly blamed at school last year. After hearing you, I realised how deeply that affected her."
For Sahra, it wasn't fame she wanted. It was clarity.
And in the city she called home, where internet cables crossed old alleyways and stories raced through smart devices, clarity was sometimes the rarest thing of all.

A month later, Sahra found herself sitting on that same rooftop bench, sketchpad in hand. The wind carried sounds of evening adhaan, and her brother Yasin was back from school, this time holding a drone kit their uncle had mailed from Garowe.
"Can you help me build this?" he asked.
She looked at the box, then at her sketchbook, and smiled.
"Let's build it — and write about it afterward. But first, we do it right. Step by step. No shortcuts."
In that moment, Sahra understood something simple but powerful: clarity doesn't always come in a rush. Sometimes it comes slowly, piece by piece — just like a story, or a life.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the story to support your answers.
Where is the story set? Describe the neighbourhood in two or three sentences.
What was Bilal accused of doing, and how did the rumours spread?
Why did Sahra decide to investigate the story herself instead of believing the rumours?
What evidence did Sahra find in the IT server room? Why was this evidence important?
How did Bilal's demeanour contribute to people's suspicion of him?
What was the title of Sahra's blog post? Explain what the title means.
How did the school community react after reading Sahra's article?
What happened to Sahra after she presented at the Somali Young Writers' Forum?
Find three examples of the present perfect with just in the story. Write out each sentence.
The story says, "Clarity doesn't always come in a rush." What does this mean?
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
Bilal was ostracised because of a misunderstanding. Have you ever seen someone judged unfairly because of incomplete information? What happened?
Sahra chose to investigate rather than share rumours. Why is this important in the digital age, when news spreads so quickly?
Mr. Hassan said, "It's not easy to speak against the crowd." Why is speaking up sometimes difficult? What gives people the courage to do it?
The story describes how Mogadishu is a city where "internet cables crossed old alleyways." What does this image tell us about the city?
At the end, Sahra tells Yasin, "We do it right. Step by step. No shortcuts." How does this connect to the lesson she learned about journalism and truth?
Vocabulary in the Story
Find these Unit 9 words in the story. Write the sentence each word appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
jostle
astride
ostracise
fugitive
bamboozle
versatile

omit

Grammar in the Story
The story uses several grammar structures from this unit. Find one example of each:
Present perfect + just: Find a sentence where a character has just done something.
Reported speech: Find a place where someone's words are reported indirectly.
Complex noun phrase: Find a phrase with more than one modifier before or after a noun.
Connective: Find a sentence that uses because, so that, although, or therefore.
Now You Try:  Writing
Write a short newspaper article (five to eight sentences) about Bilal's case, using reported speech to quote at least two characters from the story.
Imagine you are Bilal. Write a diary entry (six to eight sentences) about the day you returned to school. Use past continuous and present perfect + just to describe your feelings.
Write a paragraph (five sentences) giving advice to students about checking information online before sharing it. Use conditional sentences with if and unless.
Write a book review of "Shadows in Mogadishu" (four to six sentences). Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit and one complex noun phrase to describe the story.
tables:
📖  About this story
Genre: Realistic fiction / Urban mystery.
Theme: Conflict, perception, misinformation, and the power of investigative thinking.
Setting: Modern-day Mogadishu, Somalia -- a middle-class neighbourhood with tech-savvy students and stable schools.
Moral: Perception is not always truth -- understanding comes from looking closer.
The Unit 9 vocabulary words are in bold the first time they appear. When you meet one, say it out loud!

End-of-Unit Vocabulary Challenge
When you have learned all the words, try this challenge:
Choose any five words from your Word Wall. Write one sentence for each that connects to the theme of The Digital World and Future Innovation.

tables: To make someone feel worried or upset.
The loud notification sound began to agitate the students during the coding test.
To think of or create an idea or plan in your mind.
The team conceived a clever plan to build a weather-monitoring app.
To come together from different directions to meet at one point.
Students from three schools converged at the robotics competition.
To make something known for the first time; to bring something new.
The teacher introduced us to a new coding language this term.
To show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
The data analysis revealed that the sensor was sending incorrect readings.
To see or hear something and know what it is; to identify.
I can recognise fake news because the sources are never named.
To get or gain something, usually by effort or learning.
She worked hard to acquire the coding skills she needed for the project.
To find out or decide something after thinking about it.
We need to determine which app is the most reliable before recommending it.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the action and change words?
Can you help me use acquire in a sentence about learning a new skill?
What is the difference between agitate and determine?
Speaking in a direct, honest way without trying to be polite; also: not sharp.
Mr. Hassan gave blunt advice: 'Stop guessing and start researching.'
Strange or unusual in a way that is interesting or different.
The eccentric inventor built a robot from old radio parts and broken keyboards.
Able to do many different things or adapt to many situations.
A smartphone is a versatile device because it can be used for calls, photos, and coding.
The way a person behaves or shows their feelings.
Her calm demeanour during the presentation impressed the judges.
A person who is running away from the police or trying to escape.
The rumour said the boy was a fugitive, but it turned out to be completely false.
The way two or more people or things are connected or interact with each other.
My relationship with technology changed after a week of digital detox.
Fake websites are designed to bamboozle people into sharing personal information.
To exclude someone from a group or society.
When students ostracise a classmate online, it can cause serious harm.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the people and behaviour words?
Can you explain the difference between versatile and eccentric?
Can you help me use demeanour in a sentence about a job interview?
To look at two or more things to see how they are similar or different.
In our project, we compared two different weather apps to see which was more accurate.
Verb: to show or display something to an audience. Noun: a gift.
She will present her research on future farming at the school science fair.
Not well known or hard to understand.
The error message was obscure, so the students asked their teacher to explain it.
Good enough for what is needed; sufficient.
The school's internet speed was barely adequate for video calls.
Basic or simple; not fully developed.
Their first prototype was rudimentary, but it proved the concept worked.
More bad than something else; of lower quality.
The old phone's camera is worse than the new one, so the photos are blurry.
Noun: comfort or freedom from worry. Verb: to make something less difficult.
The new software helped ease the workload for the teachers.
Noun: a mysterious event that makes people curious. Verb: to arouse curiosity.
The mystery of who hacked the server was full of intrigue.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the description and analysis words?
What is the difference between adequate and rudimentary?
Can you help me use intrigue in a sentence about a mystery?
To push or bump against someone or something while trying to move.
In the crowded tech fair, visitors jostled each other to see the new robots.
Noun: a small mistake or failure, often from forgetting. Verb: to gradually stop.
A lapse in concentration caused the programmer to introduce a bug in the code.
Noun: a temporary calm or pause. Verb: to make someone feel calm or sleepy.
There was a lull in the conversation before the speaker announced the winner.
To struggle or have difficulty in doing something.
Without clear instructions, the students began to flounder with the new software.
To leave out or not include something.
Do not omit any data from your report, even if the results are unexpected.
With one leg on each side of something.
She sat astride the bench, balancing her laptop on her knees.
